title: Abandoned Property
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A person has no reasonable expectation of privacy in property that has been abandoned.

title: Abandonment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: At common law, abandonment is not a defense once attempt is complete, though some modern statutes recognize voluntary and complete renunciation.

title: Abatement
subject: Trusts
ruleText: Abatement determines the order in which gifts are reduced to satisfy debts.

title: Abatement
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Abatement is the reduction of gifts when estate assets are insufficient to satisfy debts, expenses, and devises. Absent contrary intent, property generally abates in this order: intestate property, residuary devises, general devises, and specific devises last.

title: Abnormally Dangerous Activities
subject: Torts
ruleText: A defendant is strictly liable for harm resulting from an abnormally dangerous activity that creates a foreseeable and highly significant risk of serious harm even when reasonable care is used and is not a matter of common usage.

title: Abnormally Dangerous Activities
subject: Torts
ruleText: Strict liability applies to abnormally dangerous activities that create a foreseeable and highly significant risk of harm even when reasonable care is exercised.

title: Absence of Business or Public Record
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence that a matter is not included in business or public records may be admitted to prove the matter did not occur.

title: Absence of Business Record
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence that a matter is not included in a record described by the business-records exception is admissible to prove the matter did not occur or exist if the matter was of a kind regularly recorded and the opponent does not show untrustworthiness.

title: Absence of Public Record
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Testimony or certification that a diligent search failed to disclose a public record or statement is admissible to prove that the record or statement does not exist or that a matter did not occur if a public office regularly kept such records.

title: Abuse of Discretion Review
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Many case management, evidentiary, discovery, and similar discretionary rulings are reviewed for abuse of discretion.

title: Acceleration Clauses
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An acceleration clause permits the lender, upon default, to declare the entire balance immediately due.

title: Acceptance
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Acceptance is an objective manifestation of assent to the terms of the offer in a manner invited or required by the offer.

title: Acceptance — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Acceptance is a manifestation of assent to the terms of the offer in the manner invited or required by the offer.

title: Acceptance by Silence
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Silence constitutes acceptance only when the offeree takes the benefit of offered services with reasonable opportunity to reject and reason to know compensation is expected, or when prior dealings make silence reasonable.

title: Acceptance of Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Acceptance of a deed by the grantee is required and is presumed if the transfer benefits the grantee.

title: Acceptance of Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A deed must be accepted by the grantee, though acceptance is generally presumed if the conveyance is beneficial.

title: Acceptance of Option Contract
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Acceptance of an option contract is effective only when received by the offeror.

title: Acceptance Under UCC
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An offer to buy goods may be accepted by a prompt promise to ship or by prompt shipment of conforming or nonconforming goods, unless the seller seasonably notifies that nonconforming goods are only an accommodation.

title: Accessions
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: An accession is goods physically united with other goods in such a manner that the identity of the original goods is not lost, and a security interest may continue in collateral that becomes an accession.

title: Accessory After the Fact
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An accessory after the fact knowingly assists a felon after completion of the felony in order to hinder apprehension, prosecution, or punishment.

title: Accomplice Liability
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Accomplice liability requires assisting, encouraging, or facilitating the crime with the intent to aid and the intent that the principal commit the crime.

title: Accomplice Liability — Act Requirement
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Accomplice liability requires some act of aiding, abetting, counseling, or encouraging the principal.

title: Accomplice Liability — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An accomplice is one who intentionally aids, abets, or encourages the commission of a crime.

title: Accomplice Liability — Effect of Principal Acquittal
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An accomplice may be convicted even if the principal is not convicted or is acquitted.

title: Accomplice Liability — Mens Rea
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Accomplice liability requires the intent to assist the principal in committing the crime.

title: Accomplice Liability — Natural and Probable Consequences
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An accomplice is liable for additional crimes that are reasonably foreseeable results of the target crime.

title: Accomplice Liability — Scope of Liability
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An accomplice is liable for the crimes the accomplice intended to facilitate and any other crimes that are the natural and probable consequences of the criminal conduct.

title: Accomplice Liability — Withdrawal
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Withdrawal from accomplice liability requires timely notice to the principal and efforts to neutralize the assistance provided.

title: Accord and Satisfaction
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An accord and satisfaction discharges a contract when parties agree to substitute a new performance and that performance is completed.

title: Accord and Satisfaction
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An accord is an agreement to accept substituted performance in discharge of an existing duty, and satisfaction is the performance of that accord.

title: Accounts
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: An account is a right to payment for property sold, leased, licensed, assigned, or otherwise disposed of, or for services rendered, not evidenced by chattel paper or an instrument, and includes certain rights to payment arising from insurance, energy, credit cards, and other listed sources.

title: Acknowledgment of Parentage
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Parentage may often be established by a valid voluntary acknowledgment or affidavit of parentage executed in compliance with statute, giving it substantial legal effect unless timely rescinded or challenged.

title: Acquittal — Finality
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An acquittal bars retrial even if the acquittal was based on an erroneous legal ruling.

title: Acts of Independent Significance
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A will may dispose of property by reference to acts or events that have significance apart from affecting the testamentary disposition, such as a gift to 'the employees at my death' or to 'the car I own at death.'

title: Acts Outside the Ordinary Course
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An act of a partner that is not apparently for carrying on the partnership business in the ordinary course binds the partnership only if the act was authorized by the other partners.

title: Actual Cause: But For
subject: Torts
ruleText: Actual cause exists when the plaintiff's injury would not have occurred but for the defendant's breach.

title: Actual Eviction
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Actual eviction occurs when the tenant is physically excluded from the premises.

title: Actual Harm
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence requires actual legally cognizable harm or damage.

title: Actual Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Actual possession means the possessor physically occupies the land in a manner appropriate to the land's nature and location.

title: Actual Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Actual possession requires physical occupation of the land in a manner appropriate to its nature.

title: Actus Reus — Omission
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An omission may satisfy actus reus only when the defendant had a legal duty to act and failed to do so.

title: Actus Reus — Voluntary Act
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Actus reus requires a voluntary physical act by the defendant.

title: Additional Child Expenses
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Courts may allocate uninsured medical costs, childcare expenses, and certain educational expenses between parents in addition to base guideline support, depending on statute and the child's needs.

title: Additional Crimes of Principal
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An accomplice is liable for the intended offense and for other crimes that are the natural and probable consequences of the criminal venture.

title: Ademption by Extinction
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If specifically devised property is not in the estate at the testator's death, the specific gift generally fails by ademption, subject to statutory exceptions and replacement-property rules.

title: Ademption by Extinction
subject: Trusts
ruleText: Ademption occurs when specifically devised property is not in the estate at death.

title: Ademption by Satisfaction
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A lifetime gift to a devisee is treated as satisfaction of a testamentary gift only if the will provides for deduction, the testator declares in a contemporaneous writing that it is in satisfaction, or the devisee acknowledges this in writing.

title: Ademption Exceptions
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Many statutes provide that a specific devisee may receive replacement property, insurance proceeds, condemnation awards, or sale proceeds unpaid at death, and if a conservator sold specifically devised property while the testator lacked capacity, the devisee may be entitled to traceable sale proceeds.

title: Adequacy vs Sufficiency of Consideration
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Courts do not inquire into the adequacy of consideration as long as the consideration is legally sufficient.

title: Adequate and Independent State Ground
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Supreme Court may not review a state court judgment resting on an adequate and independent state law ground sufficient to support the result.

title: Adequate Assurances (UCC)
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, a party may demand adequate assurances of performance when there are reasonable grounds for insecurity.

title: Adequate Assurances Under UCC
subject: Contracts
ruleText: When reasonable grounds for insecurity arise, a party may demand adequate assurances of due performance and may suspend performance if commercially reasonable until assurances are received.

title: Adequate Provocation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Adequate provocation is provocation that would arouse sudden and intense passion in an ordinary person, causing loss of self-control.

title: Adequate Provocation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Adequate provocation exists when a reasonable person would lose self-control under the circumstances.

title: Adopted Children
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: An adopted child is generally treated as the child of the adoptive parent and not of the biological parents for intestate succession, except where statutes preserve limited inheritance through or from a biological family in certain situations such as stepparent adoption.

title: Adoption
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Adoption requires consent of biological parents unless parental rights have been terminated.

title: Adoption
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A valid adoption creates a legal parent-child relationship between the adoptive parent and the child and generally terminates the legal rights and duties of the biological parents, except in cases such as stepparent adoption where one parent's rights may remain.

title: Adoptive Admission
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement is admissible as an adoptive admission if the party manifested an adoption or belief in its truth, including in some circumstances by silence when a reasonable person would deny the statement.

title: Advancements
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A lifetime gift to an heir is treated as an advancement against the heir's intestate share only if declared in a contemporaneous writing by the decedent or acknowledged in writing by the heir.

title: Adverse and Hostile
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Adverse possession requires possession without permission of the true owner.

title: Adverse Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Adverse possession requires possession that is actual, open and notorious, exclusive, hostile, and continuous for the statutory period.

title: Adverse Possession — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Adverse possession allows a possessor to acquire title by open, notorious, actual, exclusive, adverse, and continuous possession for the statutory period.

title: Advertisements
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Advertisements are generally not offers unless they are clear, definite, and explicit, leaving nothing open for negotiation.

title: Advertisements
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Advertisements are usually invitations to deal rather than offers unless they are specific and leave nothing open for negotiation.

title: Affirmative Action — Education
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Diversity in higher education may be a compelling interest if race is used in a narrowly tailored manner.

title: Affirmative Action — Race
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Government affirmative action programs using race are subject to strict scrutiny.

title: Affirmative Defenses
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Affirmative defenses must be stated in the answer or they are waived.

title: After-Acquired Property Clause
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security agreement may provide for a security interest in after-acquired collateral, except that an after-acquired property clause generally does not attach to consumer goods acquired more than 10 days after the secured party gives value or to commercial tort claims.

title: Agency — Actual and Apparent Authority
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Actual authority arises from the principal’s manifestations to the agent, while apparent authority arises from the principal’s manifestations to third parties causing reasonable reliance.

title: Agency — Agent’s Liability
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An agent is personally liable when acting without authority, when the principal is undisclosed or partially disclosed, or when the agent expressly assumes liability.

title: Agency — Formation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An agency relationship is created when a principal manifests assent that an agent act on the principal’s behalf and subject to the principal’s control, and the agent consents.

title: Agency — Ratification
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A principal may ratify an unauthorized act by knowingly accepting its benefits or affirming the act with full knowledge of material facts.

title: Agency by Estoppel
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
ruleText: A person may be estopped to deny agency when the person intentionally or carelessly causes a third party to believe another has authority to act for the person, and the third party justifiably and detrimentally relies on that belief.

title: Agent Liability for Undisclosed or Partially Disclosed Principal
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
ruleText: An agent who contracts on behalf of an undisclosed or partially disclosed principal is generally liable on the contract unless the parties agree otherwise.

title: Agent Liability on Contract for Disclosed Principal
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
ruleText: An agent who enters a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal is generally not liable on the contract if the agent had authority and the contract indicates representative capacity.

title: Agent's Duty of Care
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An agent owes the principal a duty to act with the care, competence, and diligence normally exercised by agents in similar circumstances.

title: Agent's Duty of Loyalty
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An agent owes the principal a duty of loyalty and must act solely for the principal's benefit in matters connected with the agency, avoiding self-dealing, usurpation of business opportunities, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and secret profits.

title: Agent's Duty of Obedience
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An agent must act within the scope of actual authority and obey the principal's lawful instructions concerning the agency relationship.

title: Aggravated Battery
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Aggravated battery occurs when a battery results in serious bodily injury or is committed using a deadly weapon.

title: Aggregation by Multiple Plaintiffs
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Multiple plaintiffs generally may not aggregate separate and distinct claims to satisfy the amount in controversy requirement, but they may aggregate if they share a common undivided interest.

title: Aggregation by One Plaintiff
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A single plaintiff may aggregate all claims against a single defendant to satisfy the amount in controversy requirement, whether or not the claims are related.

title: Aggregation of Claims
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A single plaintiff may aggregate all claims against a single defendant to satisfy the amount in controversy.

title: Ambiguity
subject: Contracts
ruleText: If contract language is ambiguous, courts may consider admissible extrinsic evidence to determine the parties' intent.

title: Ambiguity
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract term is ambiguous if it is reasonably susceptible to more than one interpretation.

title: Ameliorative Waste
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Ameliorative waste is a substantial change to property that increases its value but alters its character, and modern courts may allow it in changed-neighborhood circumstances.

title: Amending Articles
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An amendment to the articles of incorporation generally requires board adoption and shareholder approval, plus filing of articles of amendment with the state, unless a statutory exception permits board-only action.

title: Amendment as a Matter of Course
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A party may amend a pleading once as a matter of course within the time allowed by the federal rules before needing leave of court or consent of the opposing party.

title: Amendment or Revocation of Revocable Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A settlor may amend or revoke a revocable trust by the method provided in the trust instrument, or, absent an exclusive method, by any method showing clear and convincing evidence of the settlor's intent.

title: Amendments — As of Right
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A party may amend a pleading once as a matter of course within 21 days of service or within 21 days after a responsive pleading or motion.

title: Amendments — By Leave of Court
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: After the time for amendment as of right, pleadings may be amended only with the opposing party’s consent or the court’s leave, which should be freely given.

title: Amount in Controversy
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: The amount in controversy requirement is satisfied if the plaintiff’s good-faith claim exceeds $75,000.

title: Amount in Controversy
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: The amount in controversy must exceed $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs.

title: Ancient Documents Exception
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement in a document prepared before January 1, 1998, whose authenticity is established, is admissible under the ancient-documents hearsay exception.

title: Animals — Domestic Animals
subject: Torts
ruleText: An owner of a domestic animal is strictly liable if the owner knew or should have known of the animal’s dangerous propensities.

title: Animals — Wild Animals
subject: Torts
ruleText: An owner of a wild animal is strictly liable for harm caused by the animal’s dangerous propensities.

title: Annulment
subject: Family Law
ruleText: An annulment is a judicial declaration that a marriage was invalid because of a defect existing at the time of the marriage, such as incapacity, fraud, duress, or bigamy.

title: Annulment
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Annulment declares a marriage invalid from the beginning based on a defect existing at the time of formation.

title: Anonymous Speech
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Anonymous speech is generally protected by the First Amendment, subject to ordinary valid content-neutral regulations and other applicable constitutional limits.

title: Answer — Admissions and Denials
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An answer must admit or deny each allegation, and failure to deny constitutes an admission.

title: Answer — Timing
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A defendant must serve an answer within 21 days after service of the complaint, unless a motion is filed.

title: Anti-Assignment Clauses
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: In many commercial contexts, Article 9 limits the effectiveness of contractual terms that prohibit or restrict assignment of accounts, chattel paper, payment intangibles, or promissory notes, though these clauses may still have some contractual effect between the original parties.

title: Anti-Commandeering Doctrine
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may not compel states to enact or administer federal regulatory programs.

title: Anti-Commandeering Principle
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may not compel state legislatures or state executive officials to enact, administer, or enforce a federal regulatory program.

title: Anti-Deficiency Statutes
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Anti-deficiency statutes may bar lenders from obtaining deficiency judgments.

title: Anti-Lapse Statute
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: An anti-lapse statute generally saves a devise when the predeceasing beneficiary was within a protected degree of relationship to the testator and left surviving descendants, who then take the gift by substitution unless the will provides otherwise.

title: Anti-Lapse Statute
subject: Trusts
ruleText: An anti-lapse statute saves a gift when the beneficiary predeceases the testator and is a close relative.

title: Anticipatory Repudiation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Anticipatory repudiation occurs when a party unequivocally indicates before performance is due that the party will not perform.

title: Anticipatory Repudiation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Anticipatory repudiation occurs when a party unequivocally indicates before performance is due that the party will not perform.

title: Any Party May Impeach
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Any party, including the party that called the witness, may attack the witness's credibility.

title: Apparent Ability
subject: Torts
ruleText: Assault requires that the plaintiff reasonably perceive that the defendant has the apparent present ability to carry out the threatened contact.

title: Apparent Authority
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
ruleText: Apparent authority arises when a principal's manifestations to a third party would cause a reasonable third party to believe that the agent is authorized to act on the principal's behalf.

title: Appeals by Prosecution
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The prosecution may appeal a dismissal or legal ruling as long as reversal would not require a retrial after acquittal.

title: Application of Proceeds
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Proceeds of disposition are applied first to the reasonable expenses of retaking, holding, preparing for disposition, processing, and disposing of the collateral, including reasonable attorney's fees and legal expenses if provided by agreement and not prohibited by law; second to the satisfaction of the obligation secured by the security interest under which the disposition is made; third to subordinate security interests or liens if the secured party receives an authenticated demand before distribution; and any surplus goes to the debtor, while the obligor remains liable for any deficiency.

title: Appointment Power
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Principal officers must be appointed by the President with Senate confirmation, while inferior officers may be appointed without Senate confirmation.

title: Appointments Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Principal officers must be appointed by the President with Senate confirmation, while Congress may vest appointment of inferior officers in the President alone, courts of law, or heads of departments.

title: Appreciation of Separate Property
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Passive appreciation of separate property is often treated as separate, while appreciation attributable to marital labor, efforts, or marital funds is often treated as marital or community property to that extent.

title: Appropriation
subject: Torts
ruleText: Appropriation is the unauthorized use of the plaintiff's name or likeness for the defendant's commercial advantage.

title: Appropriation of Name or Likeness
subject: Torts
ruleText: Appropriation occurs when a defendant uses a plaintiff’s name or likeness for commercial advantage without consent.

title: Appurtenant and In Gross Easements
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement appurtenant benefits a parcel of land and runs with that land, while an easement in gross benefits a person or entity independent of ownership of a parcel.

title: Arrest in Public
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may make a warrantless public arrest if they have probable cause to believe the suspect committed a felony, and for misdemeanors committed in the officer's presence.

title: Arrest in Suspect's Home
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Absent consent or exigency, police need an arrest warrant to enter the suspect's own home to make a routine felony arrest.

title: Arrest Warrant — Home Entry
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may enter a suspect’s home to execute an arrest warrant if there is reason to believe the suspect is inside.

title: Arson
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Arson is the malicious burning of the dwelling of another.

title: Arson — Modern Statutes
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Modern arson statutes include burning of structures or personal property and may require only damage by fire or explosion.

title: Article IV Privileges and Immunities Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A state may not discriminate against out-of-state citizens with respect to fundamental rights or important economic activities unless the discrimination is closely related to a substantial state interest.

title: Articles of Incorporation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Articles of incorporation generally must include the corporate name, the number of authorized shares, the street address of the corporation's initial registered office and registered agent, and the name and address of each incorporator, along with any optional provisions.

title: Ascertainable Beneficiaries
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A private trust generally must have definite or reasonably ascertainable beneficiaries so the trustee's duties can be enforced.

title: Assault
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law assault is either attempted battery or intentionally placing another in reasonable apprehension of imminent bodily harm.

title: Assault
subject: Torts
ruleText: Assault occurs when a defendant intentionally causes reasonable apprehension of an imminent harmful or offensive contact.

title: Assault
subject: Torts
ruleText: Assault requires an intentional act causing the plaintiff reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful or offensive contact.

title: Assault — Attempted Battery
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Assault occurs when a defendant attempts to commit a battery but fails.

title: Assault — Reasonable Apprehension
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Assault occurs when a defendant intentionally places another in reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful or offensive contact.

title: Assignment
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An assignment transfers the tenant’s entire remaining interest in the lease.

title: Assignment
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An assignment is a transfer of contractual rights from the assignor to the assignee.

title: Assignment — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An assignment is a transfer of contractual rights from the assignor to the assignee.

title: Assignment Versus Sublease
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An assignment transfers the tenant's entire remaining leasehold interest, while a sublease transfers less than the tenant's entire remaining interest.

title: Assisted Reproduction
subject: Family Law
ruleText: In assisted reproduction cases, intended parents are often recognized as legal parents when statutory consent requirements are met, and a sperm donor who donates without intent to parent is generally not treated as a legal parent under applicable statutes.

title: Associational Standing
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: An association may sue on behalf of its members when members would have standing, the interests are germane to the organization's purpose, and neither the claim nor the relief requires individual member participation.

title: Assumption and Subject To
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A buyer taking subject to a mortgage is not personally liable on the note, while a buyer who assumes the mortgage becomes personally liable to the lender.

title: Assumption of Mortgage
subject: Real Property
ruleText: When a buyer assumes the mortgage, the buyer becomes personally liable for the debt.

title: Assumption of Risk
subject: Torts
ruleText: Assumption of risk applies when the plaintiff knew of a specific risk and voluntarily encountered it.

title: Assumption of Risk in Products Cases
subject: Torts
ruleText: A plaintiff who discovers a product defect and voluntarily proceeds to use the product despite the known danger may be barred or have recovery reduced depending on the jurisdiction's fault system.

title: Attachment — Requirements
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest attaches when value is given, the debtor has rights in the collateral, and there is an authenticated security agreement or possession or control by the secured party.

title: Attachment by Control
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: For collateral such as deposit accounts, electronic chattel paper, investment property, or letter-of-credit rights, a security interest may be enforceable if the secured party has control pursuant to the debtor's security agreement.

title: Attachment by Possession
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest may be enforceable without an authenticated security agreement if the collateral is in the possession of the secured party pursuant to the debtor's security agreement.

title: Attachment of Jeopardy
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Jeopardy attaches in jury trials when the jury is sworn and in bench trials when the first witness is sworn.

title: Attachment of Jeopardy — Bench Trial
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: In a bench trial, jeopardy attaches when the first witness is sworn.

title: Attachment of Jeopardy — Jury Trial
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: In a jury trial, jeopardy attaches when the jury is sworn.

title: Attachment of Sixth Amendment Right to Counsel
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches when formal adversarial judicial proceedings begin, such as by formal charge, indictment, information, arraignment, or preliminary hearing.

title: Attachment of the Right to Counsel
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches at the initiation of formal judicial proceedings, such as indictment, arraignment, or filing of charges.

title: Attachment Requirements
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest attaches and becomes enforceable against the debtor and third parties when value has been given, the debtor has rights in the collateral or the power to transfer rights in the collateral, and the debtor has authenticated a security agreement that reasonably describes the collateral, or the secured party has possession or control of the collateral pursuant to the debtor's security agreement.

title: Attempt
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Attempt requires specific intent to commit the target crime and an overt act that goes beyond preparation and constitutes a substantial step toward commission.

title: Attempt — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Attempt requires the specific intent to commit a crime and a substantial step toward its commission.

title: Attempt — Impossibility
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Factual impossibility is not a defense to attempt, while legal impossibility is a defense.

title: Attempt — Substantial Step
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A substantial step is conduct that strongly corroborates the defendant’s criminal purpose.

title: Attempt Tests
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Courts use tests such as substantial step, dangerous proximity, probable desistance, and unequivocality, but the MBE commonly emphasizes substantial step.

title: Attenuation Doctrine
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Evidence is admissible if the connection between the illegal conduct and the discovery of evidence is sufficiently attenuated.

title: Attorney-Client Privilege
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between client and attorney, or their representatives, made for the purpose of obtaining or providing legal advice.

title: Attorney-Client Privilege
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between an attorney and client made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice.

title: Attorney-Client Privilege — Waiver
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The attorney-client privilege is waived if the communication is disclosed to a third party.

title: Attractive Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner may be liable for an artificial condition highly dangerous to child trespassers if children are likely to trespass, the condition poses an unreasonable risk, children cannot appreciate the danger because of youth, the burden of eliminating the danger is slight compared with the risk, and the landowner fails to use reasonable care.

title: Authentication — Chain of Custody
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Physical evidence may be authenticated by leading evidence of a chain of custody sufficient to show no material alteration.

title: Authentication — Comparison by Trier or Expert
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Handwriting may be authenticated by comparison by an expert witness or the trier of fact with an authenticated specimen.

title: Authentication — Distinctive Characteristics
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence may be authenticated by its appearance, contents, substance, internal patterns, or other distinctive characteristics.

title: Authentication — General Rule
subject: Evidence
ruleText: To authenticate evidence, the proponent must produce sufficient evidence to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is.

title: Authentication — Non-Expert Opinion on Handwriting
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A non-expert may authenticate handwriting based on familiarity not acquired for purposes of litigation.

title: Authentication — Telephone Conversations
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Telephone conversations may be authenticated by evidence that the call was made to the number assigned to a particular person or business.

title: Authentication — Testimony of a Witness with Knowledge
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence may be authenticated by testimony of a witness with knowledge that the item is what it is claimed to be.

title: Authentication — Voice Identification
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A voice may be authenticated by opinion based on hearing the voice at any time under circumstances connecting it to the speaker.

title: Authority to Arrest
subject: Torts
ruleText: A law enforcement officer or private citizen may assert authority to arrest as a defense when the arrest is lawful.

title: Authorized Disposition
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: If a secured party authorizes a disposition of collateral free of the security interest, the transferee takes free of that security interest, though the security interest usually continues in identifiable proceeds.
buzzwords: authorized disposition free of security interest

title: Authorized Inventory Sales
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: An inventory secured party typically authorizes ordinary-course sales of inventory free of the security interest, causing the lien to shift from the sold goods to the identifiable proceeds.

title: Authorized Shares and Issuance
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: The articles fix the number of shares the corporation is authorized to issue. The board may issue shares up to that number in exchange for consideration approved by the board, subject to statutory requirements.

title: Automatic Perfection — PMSI in Consumer Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A purchase-money security interest in consumer goods is automatically perfected upon attachment.

title: Automatic Perfection in Sale of Payment Intangible or Promissory Note
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A sale of a payment intangible or promissory note is automatically perfected upon attachment.

title: Automatic Perfection of PMSI in Consumer Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A purchase-money security interest in consumer goods is automatically perfected upon attachment, except for goods subject to certificate-of-title statutes and certain other exceptions.

title: Automobile Exception
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: If police have probable cause to believe a vehicle contains contraband or evidence, they may search any area of the vehicle and containers that could contain the object of the search.

title: Automobile Exception
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may search a vehicle without a warrant if they have probable cause to believe it contains evidence of a crime.

title: Automobile Exception — Scope
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under the automobile exception, police may search any part of the vehicle and containers that may conceal the object of the search.

title: Avoidable Consequences
subject: Torts
ruleText: A plaintiff may not recover damages that reasonably could have been avoided after the tort occurred.

title: Balancing Test — Byrd
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Courts balance the state’s interest against the federal interest in maintaining the federal system.

title: Ballot Access
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Ballot access restrictions are evaluated by balancing the character and magnitude of the burden against the state's justifications, with severe burdens receiving more exacting scrutiny.

title: Bankruptcy Trustee as Lien Creditor
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A bankruptcy trustee generally has the status of a hypothetical lien creditor as of the commencement of the case and can avoid many unperfected security interests.

title: BARRK Felonies
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Burglary, arson, rape, robbery, and kidnapping are the classic inherently dangerous felonies supporting felony murder.

title: Bases of Expert Opinion
subject: Evidence
ruleText: An expert may base an opinion on facts or data personally observed or made known to the expert, including inadmissible evidence reasonably relied upon by experts in the field.

title: Basic Relevance
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: Evidence is relevant if it has any tendency to make a fact more or less probable than it would be without the evidence and the fact is of consequence in determining the action.

title: Basis of Expert Opinion
subject: Evidence
ruleText: An expert may base an opinion on facts or data in the case that the expert has been made aware of or personally observed, and if experts in the field would reasonably rely on such facts or data, the information need not itself be admissible. But inadmissible facts or data may be disclosed to the jury only if their probative value in helping evaluate the opinion substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect.

title: Batson Challenge
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A Batson challenge requires a prima facie showing of discrimination, a race-neutral explanation, and a determination of purposeful discrimination.

title: Battery
subject: Torts
ruleText: Battery requires an intentional act that causes harmful or offensive contact with the plaintiff's person.

title: Battery
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Battery is the unlawful application of force to another person resulting in harmful or offensive contact.

title: Battery
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law battery is the unlawful application of force to the person of another resulting in bodily injury or offensive touching.

title: Battery
subject: Torts
ruleText: Battery is the intentional infliction of harmful or offensive contact with another person without consent.

title: Battle of the Forms
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, a definite and seasonable expression of acceptance can form a contract despite additional or different terms, and between merchants additional terms may become part of the contract unless an exception applies.

title: Battle of the Forms (UCC 2-207)
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under UCC 2-207, a definite and seasonable expression of acceptance forms a contract despite additional or different terms unless acceptance is expressly conditional on assent to those terms.

title: Beginning Performance in Unilateral Contract
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Beginning the invited performance on a unilateral contract offer generally creates an option making the offer temporarily irrevocable, but acceptance requires complete performance.

title: Bench Trial
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: In a bench trial, the judge serves as the trier of fact and determines both law and facts.

title: Best Evidence — Exceptions
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Other evidence of content is admissible when the original is lost or destroyed, cannot be obtained, is in the opponent’s possession, or is not closely related to a controlling issue.

title: Best Evidence Rule
subject: Evidence
topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
ruleText: To prove the content of a writing, recording, or photograph, the original is required except as otherwise provided by the rules or statute.

title: Best Evidence Rule — General Rule
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The best evidence rule requires the original writing, recording, or photograph to prove its content.

title: Best Evidence Rule — When Applicable
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The best evidence rule applies only when a party seeks to prove the contents of a writing, recording, or photograph.

title: Best Interests of the Child
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Child custody determinations are governed by the best interests of the child, considering factors such as the child's safety, emotional ties, stability, parenting abilities, the child's needs, and in some jurisdictions the child's preferences if of sufficient age and maturity.

title: Bias, Interest, or Motive
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness may be impeached by showing bias, interest, or motive to misrepresent, and extrinsic evidence is generally permitted if relevant.

title: Bicameralism and Presentment
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A law must be passed by both houses of Congress and presented to the President for approval or veto.

title: Bigamous Marriage
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A marriage entered into while one party is still legally married to another person is generally void.

title: Bilateral and Unilateral Contracts
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A bilateral contract is formed by exchange of promises, while a unilateral contract is formed when the offeree accepts by performance.

title: Bilateral Versus Unilateral Conspiracy
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under the common law bilateral approach, at least two guilty minds must genuinely agree; under the MPC unilateral approach, one party's genuine agreement is sufficient even if the other only feigns agreement.

title: Board Action by Written Consent
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Directors may act without a meeting if all directors consent in writing or in an electronic record, unless the governing statute provides otherwise.

title: Board Committees
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: The board may delegate many management functions to committees of directors, but certain fundamental decisions, such as declaring distributions or approving major structural changes, may remain reserved to the full board under statute.

title: Board Management Authority
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: The corporation's business and affairs are managed by or under the direction of the board of directors, except as otherwise provided by statute, the articles, or valid shareholder arrangements in special contexts.

title: Board Meetings, Notice, and Quorum
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A majority of the number of directors fixed by the articles or bylaws generally constitutes a quorum for board action, and directors typically must receive notice of special meetings unless waived. No notice is usually required for regular meetings if the time and place are fixed.

title: Bona Fide Purchaser
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A bona fide purchaser is one who acquires an interest in property for value and without notice of a prior unrecorded interest.

title: Borrowing Statutes
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: A borrowing statute directs the forum to apply another jurisdiction's statute of limitations, often when the cause of action arose elsewhere and the other jurisdiction's period is shorter.

title: Boundary Disputes — Acquiescence
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Boundary by acquiescence may be established when neighbors accept a boundary for a long period.

title: Bounded Area
subject: Torts
ruleText: The plaintiff must be confined within boundaries fixed by the defendant, and mere prevention from going in one direction is not enough if a reasonable escape route remains.

title: Brady Material
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The prosecution must disclose favorable material exculpatory and impeachment evidence in its possession.

title: Breach
subject: Torts
ruleText: Breach is established when the defendant fails to act as a reasonably prudent person would under the circumstances.

title: Breach — Definition
subject: Torts
ruleText: A breach occurs when a defendant’s conduct falls below the applicable standard of care.

title: Breach — Negligence Per Se
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence per se applies when a defendant violates a statute designed to protect against the type of harm suffered by the plaintiff.

title: Breach of the Peace
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A breach of the peace includes conduct likely to lead to violence, force, or public disturbance, such as breaking into locked premises or continuing repossession over the debtor's contemporaneous objection.

title: Breach of Warranty — Express
subject: Torts
ruleText: An express warranty is created by an affirmation of fact or promise relating to the product that becomes part of the basis of the bargain.

title: Breach of Warranty — Implied Warranty of Fitness
subject: Torts
ruleText: An implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose arises when the seller knows the buyer’s purpose and reliance.

title: Breach of Warranty — Implied Warranty of Merchantability
subject: Torts
ruleText: The implied warranty of merchantability requires that goods be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.

title: Breaking
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Breaking requires creating an opening by force, however slight, such as opening a closed door or window.

title: Bruton Rule
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a joint criminal trial, admission of a nontestifying codefendant's confession that facially incriminates the defendant violates the defendant's Confrontation Clause rights, even with a limiting instruction, unless the confession is properly redacted or the codefendant testifies.

title: Burden of Proof
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The prosecution must prove every element of the offense beyond a reasonable doubt.

title: Burglary
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Burglary is the breaking and entering of a dwelling of another at night with intent to commit a felony inside.

title: Burglary — Modern Statutes
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Modern burglary statutes eliminate the nighttime or dwelling requirements and require unlawful entry with intent to commit a crime.

title: Business Judgment Rule
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: The business judgment rule creates a presumption that directors acted on an informed basis, in good faith, and with the honest belief that the action taken was in the corporation's best interests. Courts generally will not second-guess such decisions absent fraud, illegality, conflict of interest, or gross abuse.

title: Business Records
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A record of an act, event, condition, opinion, or diagnosis is admissible as a business record if it was made at or near the time by, or from information transmitted by, someone with knowledge; was kept in the course of a regularly conducted business activity; making the record was a regular practice of that activity; these conditions are shown by testimony or certification of a custodian or qualified witness; and the source or circumstances do not indicate a lack of trustworthiness.

title: Business Records
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Records of a business activity are admissible if made at or near the time by someone with knowledge and kept in the regular course of business.

title: Business Records — Foundation Witness
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The foundation for a business record may be established by a custodian or other qualified witness.

title: But-For Causation
subject: Torts
ruleText: But-for causation is satisfied when the plaintiff’s injury would not have occurred but for the defendant’s conduct.

title: Buyer in Ordinary Course of Business
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A buyer in ordinary course of business, other than one buying farm products from a person engaged in farming operations, takes free of a security interest created by the buyer's seller even if the security interest is perfected and the buyer knows of its existence, so long as the buyer buys in good faith, without knowledge that the sale violates another's rights, and in ordinary course from a seller in the business of selling goods of that kind.

title: Buyer Remedies — Seller Breach
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If the seller breaches, the buyer may recover damages or seek specific performance.

title: Buyer's Cover
subject: Contracts
ruleText: After the seller's breach, a buyer may cover by making a good faith and reasonable substitute purchase and recover the difference between the cover price and contract price plus incidental and consequential damages, less expenses saved.
buzzwords: difference between cover and contract price

title: Buyer's Market Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A buyer may recover the difference between the market price at the time the buyer learned of the breach and the contract price, plus incidental and consequential damages, less expenses saved.

title: Buyer's Right to Recover Identified Goods
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A buyer may in some circumstances recover identified goods, including by replevin or specific performance, when cover is unavailable or the goods are unique or specially situated.

title: Bylaws
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Bylaws are internal rules for managing the corporation and regulating its affairs. They are typically adopted by the incorporators or directors and may later be amended by shareholders or directors as allowed by statute and the articles.

title: Bystander IIED
subject: Torts
ruleText: A bystander may recover for IIED when the defendant intentionally or recklessly causes severe emotional distress through extreme and outrageous conduct directed at a close relative in the bystander's presence or where the defendant knows of the bystander's presence.

title: Capacity — Effect
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract entered into by a person lacking capacity is voidable at the election of the incapacitated party.

title: Capacity — General Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A person lacks capacity to contract if the person is a minor, mentally incapacitated, or intoxicated at the time of contracting.

title: Capacity to Marry and Age
subject: Family Law
ruleText: To enter a valid marriage, each party generally must have legal capacity, including sufficient age and mental capacity, and must not be within a prohibited degree of relationship or already married to someone else.

title: Case Management — Judicial Control
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Courts may issue scheduling orders and manage discovery to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of actions.

title: Case or Controversy Requirement
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Federal courts may decide only actual, ongoing cases or controversies and may not issue advisory opinions.

title: Cash Proceeds Continuation
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected security interest in identifiable cash proceeds generally remains perfected beyond the initial 20-day automatic-perfection period so long as the proceeds remain identifiable.

title: Causation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Result crimes require the defendant's conduct to be both the actual cause and the proximate cause of the prohibited result.

title: Causation — Actual Cause
subject: Torts
ruleText: Actual cause requires that the defendant’s conduct be a cause in fact of the plaintiff’s injury.

title: Causation — Proximate Cause
subject: Torts
ruleText: Proximate cause limits liability to harms that are a foreseeable result of the defendant’s conduct.

title: Causation in Homicide
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The defendant's act must be both the actual and proximate cause of death.

title: Causation in Products Liability
subject: Torts
ruleText: The plaintiff must prove that the product defect was a cause in fact and proximate cause of the injury.

title: Certainty Limitation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Contract damages must be proven with reasonable certainty and may not be based on pure speculation.

title: Certificate-of-Title Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest in goods covered by a certificate-of-title statute is generally perfected by compliance with that statute, usually by notation of the lien on the certificate of title, rather than by filing a financing statement.

title: Chain of Title
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The chain of title is the sequence of recorded instruments that can be located through a reasonable title search under the grantor-grantee indexes or other recording system.

title: Change in Debtor's Location
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: If a debtor changes location to another jurisdiction, a security interest perfected under the law of the original jurisdiction generally remains perfected for four months after the debtor's location changes, or until perfection would have ceased under the original law, whichever occurs earlier. Reperfection in the new jurisdiction is needed to maintain continuous perfection thereafter.

title: Change of Domicile
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A person changes domicile only by being physically present in the new state and intending to remain there indefinitely.

title: Changing Domicile
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: A person changes domicile by being physically present in a new place and forming the intent to remain there indefinitely.

title: Character Evidence — Civil Cases
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Character evidence is generally not admissible in civil cases unless character is an essential element of a claim or defense.

title: Character Evidence — Criminal Defendant Exception
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a criminal case, a defendant may offer evidence of a pertinent character trait, and the prosecution may rebut that evidence.

title: Character Evidence — General Rule
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Character evidence is not admissible to prove that a person acted in accordance with a character trait on a particular occasion.

title: Character Evidence — Homicide Case
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a homicide case, the prosecution may offer evidence of the victim’s peaceful character to rebut evidence that the victim was the first aggressor.

title: Character Evidence — Methods of Proof
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Character may be proven by reputation testimony or opinion testimony, and by specific instances of conduct only on cross-examination.

title: Character Evidence — Victim in Criminal Case
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a criminal case, a defendant may offer evidence of a pertinent character trait of the victim, and the prosecution may rebut or offer evidence of the defendant’s same trait.

title: Character Evidence and Propensity
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of a person's character or character trait is generally not admissible to prove that on a particular occasion the person acted in accordance with the character or trait.

title: Character Evidence in Criminal Cases
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: A criminal defendant may offer evidence of the defendant's pertinent trait, and if admitted, the prosecutor may rebut it. A defendant may also offer evidence of an alleged victim's pertinent trait, subject to important limits.

title: Character for Truthfulness
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness's credibility may be attacked or supported by testimony about the witness's reputation for having a character for truthfulness or untruthfulness, or by opinion testimony about that character. But evidence of truthful character is admissible only after the witness's character for truthfulness has been attacked.

title: Charitable Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A charitable trust is a trust created for a charitable purpose benefiting the public or a sufficiently broad segment of the public, such as relief of poverty, advancement of education, religion, health, or other community benefits.

title: Charring Requirement
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Arson requires material charring of the structure; mere discoloration or smoke damage is insufficient.

title: Chattel Paper
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Chattel paper is a record or records that evidence both a monetary obligation and a security interest in specific goods, a security interest in specific goods and software used with the goods, a lease of specific goods, or a lease of specific goods and software used with the goods.

title: Checkpoints
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Suspicionless checkpoints are generally invalid for ordinary crime control but may be valid for special needs such as border security or sobriety checkpoints.

title: Child Custody — Best Interests and Types
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Child custody determinations are based on the best interests of the child and may include legal custody, physical custody, or joint custody.

title: Child Custody — Modification and Relocation
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Custody orders may be modified upon a substantial change in circumstances, and relocation requires good faith and consistency with the child’s best interests.

title: Child Custody Jurisdiction (UCCJEA)
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Under the UCCJEA, initial custody jurisdiction lies in the child’s home state where the child lived for six months prior to filing.

title: Child Pornography
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Child pornography involving actual minors may be prohibited regardless of whether it is obscene, because the government has a compelling interest in protecting children from exploitation.

title: Child Support — Obligation and Modification
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Parents have a non-waivable duty to support their children, and support may be modified upon a substantial change in circumstances.

title: Child Support Arrears
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Unpaid child support installments generally become vested judgments as they accrue and are enforceable as arrears, often with interest, subject to limited defenses.

title: Child Support Jurisdiction (UIFSA)
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Under UIFSA, the issuing state retains continuing exclusive jurisdiction over child support orders unless all parties leave the state.

title: Children
subject: Torts
ruleText: A child is held to the care of a reasonably careful child of the same age, intelligence, and experience, unless the child is engaged in an adult activity.

title: Choice of Law — Klaxon Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A federal court sitting in diversity applies the choice-of-law rules of the forum state.

title: Choice of Law for Perfection
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Except for certain collateral such as possessory security interests, fixtures, timber to be cut, and as-extracted collateral, the law of the debtor's location generally governs perfection, the effect of perfection or nonperfection, and priority.

title: Choice-of-Law Approaches
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Courts may use different choice-of-law approaches, including the traditional vested-rights or territorial approach, interest analysis, the most significant relationship test, and comparative impairment or better-law approaches, depending on the jurisdiction.

title: Citizenship — Corporations
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A corporation is a citizen of its state of incorporation and the state of its principal place of business.

title: Citizenship — Individuals
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An individual is a citizen of the state in which the person is domiciled.

title: Citizenship of Corporations
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A corporation is a citizen of every state in which it is incorporated and the one state where it has its principal place of business.

title: Citizenship of Individuals
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An individual is a citizen of the state in which the individual is domiciled.

title: Citizenship of Unincorporated Associations
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An unincorporated association takes the citizenship of all of its members.

title: Claim Preclusion
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Claim preclusion bars a later claim when there was a valid final judgment on the merits, the cases involve the same parties or their privies, and the later claim arises from the same transaction or occurrence.
buzzwords: valid final judgment on the merits

title: Claim Preclusion — Final Judgment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A final judgment includes judgments after trial, summary judgment, and default judgment.

title: Claim Preclusion — General Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Claim preclusion bars a subsequent action between the same parties or their privies arising out of the same transaction after a final judgment on the merits.

title: Claim Preclusion — On the Merits
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A judgment is on the merits unless it is based on lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, or failure to join a party.

title: Claim Preclusion — Same Claim
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Claims are considered the same if they arise out of the same transaction or occurrence.

title: Class Action Prerequisites
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A class action requires numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequacy of representation.

title: Class Action Types
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: After satisfying numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequacy, the proposed class must also fit within an authorized class action category under the federal rules.

title: Class Gifts
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If a gift is to a class, surviving class members generally take the entire gift unless an anti-lapse statute applies to a deceased class member within the statute's protection.

title: Class Voting
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Certain corporate actions that affect a class of shares differently, such as amendments altering class rights, may require separate class voting by that class in addition to any overall shareholder vote.

title: Classifying Collateral
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Collateral must be classified because attachment, perfection, priority, and filing rules often depend on whether the collateral is goods, accounts, chattel paper, instruments, investment property, deposit accounts, documents, general intangibles, commercial tort claims, or other defined categories.

title: Clergy-Penitent Privilege
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The clergy-penitent privilege protects confidential communications made to a clergy member for spiritual counseling.

title: Close Corporation Agreements
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Close Corporations and Special Control Devices
ruleText: Close corporation shareholders may adopt agreements restricting share transfer, allocating management powers among shareholders, resolving deadlocks, or creating buy-sell rights, and such agreements are often enforceable if consistent with statute.

title: Co-Tenant Expenses — Contribution
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Co-tenants must contribute to necessary expenses such as taxes and mortgage payments.

title: Co-Tenant Improvements
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A co-tenant is not entitled to contribution for improvements but may recover their value upon partition.

title: Co-Tenant Rent Liability
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A co-tenant in possession is not liable for rent absent ouster.

title: Co-Tenant Repairs
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A co-tenant is not entitled to contribution for repairs unless they are necessary.

title: Codicil
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A codicil is an amendment to a will that must satisfy will formalities.

title: Collateral Classification
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Collateral is classified as consumer goods, inventory, equipment, or farm products based on the debtor’s use.

title: Collateral Description in Financing Statement
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A financing statement sufficiently indicates collateral if it describes the collateral pursuant to UCC methods or if it states that it covers all assets or all personal property of the debtor.

title: Collateral Order Doctrine
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: The collateral order doctrine permits immediate appeal of a narrow class of orders that conclusively resolve an important issue separate from the merits and would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

title: Collection of Accounts and Payment Rights
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: After default, a secured party may notify account debtors or other persons obligated on collateral to make payment directly to the secured party and may enforce the debtor's rights to payment.

title: Color of Title
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Color of title means a claim founded on a defective written instrument, and in some jurisdictions it can aid adverse possession by supporting constructive possession of the whole described tract.

title: Color of Title
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Color of title exists when a possessor enters under a defective written instrument.

title: Coming to the Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Coming to the nuisance is not a complete defense but may be considered in determining the reasonableness of the interference.

title: Commerce Clause — Economic Activity
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may regulate intrastate economic activity that in the aggregate substantially affects interstate commerce.

title: Commerce Clause — General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may regulate channels of interstate commerce, instrumentalities of interstate commerce, and activities that substantially affect interstate commerce.

title: Commerce Clause — Noneconomic Activity
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may not regulate noneconomic activity based solely on its cumulative effect on interstate commerce.

title: Commerce Clause Categories
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may regulate the channels of interstate commerce, the instrumentalities of interstate commerce, and activities that have a substantial effect on interstate commerce.

title: Commercial Reasonableness
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A disposition is commercially reasonable if it is made in the usual manner on a recognized market, at the current price in a recognized market, or otherwise in conformity with reasonable commercial practices among dealers in the type of property sold.

title: Commercial Speech
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Truthful, lawful commercial speech may be regulated only if the government has a substantial interest, the regulation directly advances that interest, and the regulation is not more extensive than necessary.

title: Commercial Speech
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Commercial speech that is lawful and not misleading is protected but subject to intermediate scrutiny.

title: Commercial Suppliers
subject: Torts
ruleText: All commercial suppliers in the distribution chain, including manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers, may be strictly liable for defective products.

title: Commercial Tort Claim
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A commercial tort claim is a claim arising in tort with respect to which the claimant is an organization or, if the claimant is an individual, the claim arose in the course of the claimant's business or profession and does not include damages for personal injury or death.

title: Commingled Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest does not exist in commingled goods as such once they lose their identity in a product or mass, but a security interest may attach to the resulting product or mass.

title: Commingling and Tracing
subject: Family Law
ruleText: When separate and marital funds are commingled, the separate-property claim may be preserved if the separate portion can be adequately traced; otherwise, the asset may be treated as marital or community property.

title: Common Carriers and Innkeepers
subject: Torts
ruleText: Common carriers and innkeepers traditionally owe a high degree of care to passengers and guests, respectively.

title: Common Interest Communities
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Recorded declarations, bylaws, and association rules in common interest communities may create enforceable servitudes and obligations, subject to reasonableness and statutory protections.

title: Common Law Arson
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law arson is the malicious burning of the dwelling of another.

title: Common Law Burglary
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law burglary is the breaking and entering of the dwelling of another at night with intent to commit a felony therein.

title: Common Law Marriage
subject: Family Law
ruleText: In jurisdictions recognizing common law marriage, a valid common law marriage generally requires capacity to marry, present intent or agreement to be married, cohabitation, and holding out to the public as married.

title: Common Law Mens Rea Categories
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law mens rea categories include intent, knowledge, recklessness, and negligence.

title: Common Law Murder
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought.

title: Common Law Rape
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law rape is unlawful sexual intercourse by a man with a woman not his wife by force or threat of force and without consent.

title: Common Scheme Doctrine
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under a common scheme, restrictions apply to all lots when a developer intends uniform restrictions.

title: Common Specific Intent Crimes
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common specific intent crimes include solicitation, attempt, conspiracy, first-degree premeditated murder, assault as attempted battery, larceny, robbery, burglary, embezzlement, false pretenses, and receiving stolen property.

title: Common Veil-Piercing Factors
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Common veil-piercing factors include undercapitalization, failure to observe corporate formalities, commingling of assets, treating corporate assets as personal assets, overlapping ownership and control, and using the corporation to promote fraud or injustice.

title: Community Property System
subject: Family Law
ruleText: In a community property jurisdiction, property acquired during marriage through either spouse's efforts is generally community property owned equally by both spouses, while separate property includes property owned before marriage and certain gifts or inheritances.

title: Compensatory Damages
subject: Torts
ruleText: Compensatory damages compensate the plaintiff for actual losses caused by the defendant’s negligence.

title: Complaint — Requirements
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A complaint must contain a short and plain statement of the grounds for jurisdiction, the claim showing entitlement to relief, and a demand for relief.

title: Complete Diversity
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Complete diversity requires that no plaintiff share citizenship with any defendant.

title: Complete Diversity
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Complete diversity requires that no plaintiff share state citizenship with any defendant.

title: Completely vs Partially Integrated Agreements
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A completely integrated agreement bars extrinsic evidence of additional or contradictory terms, while a partially integrated agreement bars only contradictory terms.

title: Compromise Offers and Negotiations
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of furnishing, promising, or offering valuable consideration to compromise a disputed claim, and conduct or statements made during compromise negotiations, is not admissible to prove or disprove the validity or amount of the claim or to impeach by prior inconsistent statement or contradiction. It may be admissible for another purpose, such as proving bias, prejudice, or obstruction.

title: Compulsory Counterclaim
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A counterclaim is compulsory if it arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the opposing party's claim.

title: Compulsory Joinder — Required Parties
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A party must be joined if complete relief cannot be accorded without them or if their absence may impair their interests or expose existing parties to inconsistent obligations.

title: Compulsory Process
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The defendant has the right to obtain witnesses and evidence in the defendant's favor through compulsory process, subject to ordinary evidentiary rules.

title: Compulsory Process and Defense Evidence
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A criminal defendant has a constitutional right to present a defense, so evidentiary rules may not be applied arbitrarily or disproportionately to exclude critical, reliable defense evidence.

title: Concurrence
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The actus reus and mens rea must coincide in time for criminal liability.

title: Concurrent Conditions
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Concurrent conditions are performances that are due at the same time, so each party's duty is conditioned on the other's readiness and willingness to perform.

title: Condition Precedent
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A condition precedent is an event that must occur before a duty to perform arises.

title: Conditional Relevance
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: When the relevance of evidence depends on whether a fact exists, the evidence is admissible if sufficient proof has been introduced to support a finding that the fact exists.
buzzwords: depends on whether a fact exists

title: Conditions
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A condition is an event, not certain to occur, that must occur before a performance obligation becomes due unless its nonoccurrence is excused.
buzzwords: must occur before performance becomes due

title: Conditions — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A condition is an event that must occur before a duty to perform arises or is discharged.

title: Confessions — Voluntariness
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A confession is inadmissible if it is involuntary under the totality of the circumstances.

title: Confrontation Clause
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In criminal cases, the Sixth Amendment bars admission of testimonial hearsay against the accused unless the declarant is unavailable and the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine the declarant, unless the defendant forfeited confrontation by wrongdoing.

title: Confrontation Clause
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: In criminal cases, testimonial hearsay by an unavailable witness is inadmissible against the defendant unless the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine the witness.

title: Confrontation Clause — General Rule
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a criminal case, the Sixth Amendment Confrontation Clause prohibits the admission of testimonial hearsay unless the declarant is unavailable and the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine.

title: Congressional Power — Legislation
subject: Constitutional Law
