ruleText: Congress has the exclusive power to enact legislation subject to constitutional limitations.

title: Conscious Presence Test
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under the conscious presence test, a person signs in the testator's presence if the testator, through sight, hearing, or general awareness, comprehends that the signing is occurring nearby.

title: Consent
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Consent is generally not a defense to crimes involving breach of the peace or serious bodily injury, but may negate elements of some offenses such as larceny.

title: Consent
subject: Torts
ruleText: Consent is a defense when the plaintiff had capacity and voluntarily agreed to the contact or invasion, and the defendant acted within the scope of that consent.

title: Consent — Express
subject: Torts
ruleText: Express consent occurs when the plaintiff explicitly agrees to the defendant’s conduct through words or writing.

title: Consent — General Rule
subject: Torts
ruleText: Consent is a defense to an intentional tort when the plaintiff voluntarily and knowingly agrees to the defendant’s conduct.

title: Consent — Implied
subject: Torts
ruleText: Implied consent exists when the plaintiff’s conduct reasonably indicates consent to the defendant’s actions.

title: Consent — Invalid Consent
subject: Torts
ruleText: Consent is invalid if obtained through fraud, duress, or if the plaintiff lacks capacity to consent.

title: Consent — Scope
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A consent search is limited to the scope of the consent given.

title: Consent Search
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A consent search is valid if consent is voluntary and given by someone with actual or apparent authority.

title: Consent Search
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may conduct a warrantless search if a person with actual or apparent authority voluntarily consents.

title: Consent to Adoption
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Adoption generally requires the consent of the child's legal parents or the termination of their parental rights, unless consent is excused by statute because of abandonment, unfitness, failure to support, or similar grounds.

title: Consequential Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Consequential damages are recoverable if they were reasonably foreseeable to the breaching party at the time of contract formation and can be proven with reasonable certainty.

title: Consideration
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Consideration is a bargained for legal detriment or benefit exchanged between the parties.

title: Consideration — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Consideration requires a bargained-for exchange in which each party incurs a legal detriment or confers a legal benefit.

title: Conspiracy
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Conspiracy is an agreement between two or more persons to commit an unlawful act, with intent to agree and intent to achieve the criminal objective; many jurisdictions also require an overt act.

title: Conspiracy — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Conspiracy requires an agreement between two or more persons to commit a crime and intent to enter the agreement.

title: Conspiracy — Overt Act
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Most jurisdictions require an overt act in furtherance of the conspiracy by any conspirator.

title: Conspiracy — Pinkerton Liability
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under Pinkerton liability, a conspirator is liable for crimes committed by co-conspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy and that are foreseeable.

title: Conspiracy — Withdrawal
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Withdrawal from a conspiracy requires an affirmative act that communicates abandonment to co-conspirators.

title: Construction Mortgage Exception
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A recorded construction mortgage generally has priority over a later purchase-money security interest in fixtures if the goods become fixtures before completion of construction.

title: Constructive Adverse Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under color of title, constructive adverse possession extends to the entire parcel described in the instrument.

title: Constructive Breaking
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Constructive breaking occurs when entry is gained by fraud, threat, or conspiracy rather than physical force.

title: Constructive Conditions
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A constructive condition is a condition implied by law that requires one party’s performance before the other party’s performance is due.

title: Constructive Eviction
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord's wrongful conduct substantially interferes with the tenant's use and enjoyment, the tenant gives notice and time to cure if appropriate, and the tenant vacates within a reasonable time.

title: Constructive Eviction
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord substantially interferes with use and the tenant vacates within a reasonable time.

title: Constructive Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A constructive trust is an equitable remedy imposed to prevent unjust enrichment when a person acquired property through wrongdoing such as fraud, breach of fiduciary duty, or mistake.

title: Constructive Trust
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A constructive trust is imposed to prevent unjust enrichment.

title: Consumer Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Consumer goods are goods used or bought for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.

title: Consumer-to-Consumer Buyer
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A buyer of consumer goods from a consumer seller takes free of a security interest, even if perfected, if the buyer buys without knowledge of the security interest, for value, primarily for the buyer's personal, family, or household purposes, and before a financing statement covering the goods has been filed.

title: Contact with Plaintiff's Person
subject: Torts
ruleText: Battery includes contact with anything closely connected to the plaintiff's body, such as clothing, a plate in the plaintiff's hand, or an object the plaintiff is holding.

title: Content-Based Regulation
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Content-based regulations of speech are subject to strict scrutiny.

title: Content-Based Restrictions
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A law that regulates speech based on content is subject to strict scrutiny and must be narrowly tailored to serve a compelling governmental interest.

title: Content-Neutral Regulation
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Content-neutral regulations of speech are subject to intermediate scrutiny.

title: Content-Neutral Time, Place, and Manner Restrictions
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A content-neutral regulation of speech in a public forum is valid if it is narrowly tailored to serve an important governmental interest and leaves open ample alternative channels of communication.

title: Contents of Notice in Nonconsumer Transaction
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: In a nonconsumer transaction, a notice of disposition is generally sufficient if it describes the debtor and secured party, describes the collateral, states the method of intended disposition, states that the debtor is entitled to an accounting and the charge for it, and states the time and place of a public disposition or the time after which a private disposition will be made.
buzzwords: time and place of public sale

title: Contingent Remainder
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A contingent remainder is a remainder that depends on an unfulfilled condition precedent or an unascertained person.

title: Contingent Remainder
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A remainder is contingent if it is either given to an unascertained person or subject to a condition precedent.

title: Continuation of Security Interest in Proceeds
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest generally continues in collateral notwithstanding sale, lease, license, exchange, or other disposition unless the secured party authorized the disposition free of the security interest, and it also attaches to any identifiable proceeds of the collateral.

title: Continuation Statement
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A continuation statement may be filed only within six months before the financing statement would lapse, and timely filing continues effectiveness for another five years.

title: Continuing Exclusive Jurisdiction under UIFSA
subject: Family Law
ruleText: The issuing state retains continuing exclusive jurisdiction to modify its child-support order so long as the obligor, obligee, or child remains in that state, or until the parties consent in a record to another state's jurisdiction, subject to UIFSA's rules.

title: Continuing Trespass
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: If the defendant takes property trespassorily without intent to permanently deprive but later forms that intent, the original trespass may continue so that larceny is established.

title: Continuous Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Continuous possession means possession consistent with how an average owner would use the property for the statutory period.

title: Continuous Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Continuous possession requires uninterrupted possession for the statutory period.

title: Contra Proferentem
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Ambiguous contract terms are construed against the drafter.

title: Contracts Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: States may not substantially impair existing contractual obligations unless the law is reasonably and appropriately tailored to serve an important public purpose.

title: Contribution
subject: Torts
ruleText: Contribution allows a tortfeasor who paid more than a fair share of liability to recover the excess from other jointly liable tortfeasors.
buzzwords: paid more than a fair share

title: Contributory Negligence
subject: Torts
ruleText: Under pure contributory negligence, any negligence by the plaintiff that contributes to the injury completely bars recovery, subject to limited exceptions such as last clear chance.

title: Control Limitation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Traditionally, a limited partner risked personal liability if the limited partner participated substantially in control of the business and a third party reasonably believed the limited partner was a general partner. Modern statutes usually abolish or greatly narrow this control-based liability rule.

title: Control of Deposit Account
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A secured party has control of a deposit account if the secured party is the bank with which the account is maintained, the debtor, secured party, and bank agree in an authenticated record that the bank will comply with the secured party's instructions regarding the account without further consent by the debtor, or the secured party becomes the bank's customer with respect to the account.
buzzwords: bank with which account is maintained

title: Control of Investment Property
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest in investment property may be perfected by filing or control, but a secured party with control generally enjoys priority over a secured party who perfects only by filing.

title: Controlling Child Support Order
subject: Family Law
ruleText: When multiple child-support orders exist, UIFSA provides rules to identify a single controlling order, generally favoring the order of the child's home state if one of the parties or the child remains there, or otherwise the most recent appropriate order under the statute.

title: Controlling Shareholder Duties
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A controlling shareholder may owe fiduciary duties to minority shareholders and the corporation when exercising control, particularly in freeze-outs, self-dealing transactions, or oppressive conduct in close corporations.

title: Conversion
subject: Torts
ruleText: Conversion is an intentional exercise of dominion or control over the plaintiff's personal property that so seriously interferes with the right of possession that the defendant may be required to pay the full value of the property.

title: Conversion
subject: Torts
ruleText: Conversion occurs when a defendant intentionally exercises dominion or control over another’s personal property causing serious interference.

title: Conviction on Appeal
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A conviction that satisfies Rule 609 is admissible for impeachment even if an appeal is pending, and evidence of the pending appeal is also admissible.

title: Corporate Adoption of Preincorporation Contracts
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: After formation, a corporation may adopt a preincorporation contract expressly or impliedly, making the corporation liable on the contract, though the promoter remains liable unless there is a novation.

title: Corporate Dissolution
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A corporation may dissolve voluntarily, administratively, or judicially, followed by winding up and distribution of assets.

title: Corporate Dissolution
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A corporation may generally dissolve voluntarily by board action followed by shareholder approval and filing articles of dissolution, after which the corporation continues only to wind up affairs, pay creditors, and distribute remaining assets.

title: Corporate Formation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A corporation is formed by filing articles of incorporation with the state. Upon filing or upon the effective date stated in the articles, the corporation comes into existence as a separate legal entity.

title: Corporate Opportunity Doctrine
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Directors and officers may not take for themselves a business opportunity that is within the corporation's line of business, that the corporation has an interest or expectancy in, or that the corporation is financially able to undertake, unless the opportunity is first offered to the corporation and properly rejected.

title: Corporate Residence for Venue
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A corporate defendant is deemed to reside in any district where it is subject to personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question.

title: Corporation — Duty of Loyalty and Conflicts of Interest
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Conflicted transactions are voidable unless approved by disinterested directors or shareholders after full disclosure or shown to be fair to the corporation.

title: Corporation — Fiduciary Duties of Directors and Officers
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Directors and officers owe duties of care and loyalty to the corporation and its shareholders, subject to the business judgment rule.

title: Corporation — Formation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A corporation is formed by filing articles of incorporation with the state, creating a separate legal entity distinct from its shareholders.

title: Corporation — Piercing the Corporate Veil
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Courts may pierce the corporate veil when shareholders abuse the corporate form through undercapitalization, commingling, or fraud, causing injustice.

title: Corporation — Promoter Liability
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Promoters are personally liable on pre-incorporation contracts unless the corporation adopts the contract and the parties agree to a novation.

title: Corporation by Estoppel
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Under corporation by estoppel, a person who treated an enterprise as a corporation in dealing with it may be estopped from later denying its corporate existence to impose personal liability on the organizers.

title: Counterclaims — Compulsory
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A compulsory counterclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence and must be raised in the defendant’s answer.

title: Counterclaims — Permissive
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A permissive counterclaim does not arise from the same transaction or occurrence and may be brought separately.

title: Counteroffer
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A counteroffer is a rejection of the original offer and a new offer by the offeree on different terms.

title: Course of Dealing
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Course of dealing refers to prior conduct between the parties that establishes a common basis of understanding.

title: Course of Performance
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Course of performance refers to how the parties have acted under the current contract.

title: Course of Performance, Course of Dealing, and Usage of Trade
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, course of performance, course of dealing, and usage of trade may be used to explain or supplement contract terms.

title: Covenant Against Encumbrances
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The covenant against encumbrances warrants that the land is free from undisclosed encumbrances.

title: Covenant of Further Assurances
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The covenant of further assurances requires the grantor to take reasonable steps to perfect title.

title: Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The landlord impliedly promises that the tenant's possession will not be disturbed by the landlord or someone with superior title.

title: Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The covenant of quiet enjoyment warrants that the grantee will not be disturbed by superior title.

title: Covenant of Right to Convey
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The covenant of right to convey warrants that the grantor has the authority to transfer the property.

title: Covenant of Seisin
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The covenant of seisin warrants that the grantor owns the estate conveyed.

title: Covenant of Warranty
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The covenant of warranty obligates the grantor to defend the grantee against lawful claims of title.

title: Creation of Agency
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Agency is the fiduciary relationship that arises when a principal manifests assent that an agent shall act on the principal's behalf and subject to the principal's control, and the agent manifests assent or otherwise consents so to act.

title: Creation of Easement — Express Grant
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement may be created by express grant that satisfies the statute of frauds.

title: Creation of Easement — Implication
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement may be implied when prior use was apparent, continuous, and reasonably necessary.

title: Creation of Easement — Necessity
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement by necessity arises when land is landlocked and access is necessary.

title: Creation of Easement — Prescription
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement by prescription arises from open, notorious, adverse, and continuous use.

title: Creation of Joint Tenancy
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A joint tenancy must be expressly created with clear survivorship language.

title: Crime — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A crime requires a voluntary act or omission accompanied by a culpable mental state.

title: Crime-Fraud Exception
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The attorney-client privilege does not apply to communications made for the purpose of seeking assistance to commit or cover up a future crime or fraud.

title: Criminal Convictions — Crimes Involving Dishonesty
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of a crime involving dishonesty or false statement must be admitted for impeachment purposes.

title: Criminal Convictions — Felonies
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Felony convictions may be admitted to impeach a witness if the probative value outweighs the prejudicial effect.

title: Criminal Convictions — Time Limit
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Convictions more than ten years old are admissible only if their probative value substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect.

title: Criminal Mischief
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Criminal mischief is the intentional or reckless damage or destruction of the property of another.

title: Critical Stage
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Once the right to counsel attaches, it applies to all critical stages of the prosecution.

title: Critical Stage — Examples
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Critical stages include post-indictment interrogations, lineups, preliminary hearings, and plea negotiations.

title: Crossclaim
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A crossclaim may be asserted against a coparty if it arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the original action or of a counterclaim in the action.

title: Cruel and Unusual Punishment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Eighth Amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishment, including certain barbaric methods and, in rare cases, grossly disproportionate sentences.

title: Cumulative Voting
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Cumulative voting allows shareholders in director elections to multiply the number of shares owned by the number of directors to be elected and cast the resulting votes for one or more candidates, if authorized by the corporation's governing law or charter.

title: Cure (UCC)
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, a seller may cure a nonconforming tender within the contract time or within a reasonable time if the seller reasonably believed the tender would be acceptable.

title: Curtilage
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Curtilage is the area immediately surrounding a home that is associated with the intimate activities of the home.

title: Custody — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Custody exists when a reasonable person would not feel free to terminate the interrogation and leave.

title: Custody for Miranda
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Custody exists when a reasonable person in the suspect's position would not feel free to terminate questioning and leave.

title: Custody Versus Possession
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: If the victim gives the defendant mere custody and the defendant wrongfully converts the property, larceny may occur; if the defendant had lawful possession, embezzlement may apply.

title: Custom
subject: Torts
ruleText: Evidence of custom is relevant to breach but is not conclusive because reasonable care may require more or less than custom.

title: Cy Pres
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If a charitable purpose becomes unlawful, impossible, impracticable, or wasteful, a court may apply cy pres to modify the trust in a manner as near as possible to the settlor's charitable intent.

title: Damages — Requirement
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence requires proof of actual damages to be actionable.

title: De Facto Corporation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A de facto corporation may exist when there is a valid incorporation statute, a good-faith colorable attempt to comply with it, and actual use of the corporate form, protecting participants from personal liability in some jurisdictions.

title: De Facto Parent Concepts
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Some jurisdictions recognize a de facto parent or psychological parent doctrine granting standing to a nonbiological adult who formed a parent-like relationship with the child under circumstances recognized by law, but the doctrine is limited by constitutional protections for legal parents.

title: Dead Man's Statute
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Under some state dead man's statutes, an interested survivor may be barred from testifying about communications or transactions with a deceased person when offered against the deceased's estate. Federal courts apply state competency law in civil cases where state law supplies the rule of decision.

title: Death or Incapacity
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Death or incapacity discharges contractual duties when performance depends on the personal skill or judgment of the obligor.

title: Death Penalty Limits
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The death penalty may not be imposed for crimes against individuals where the victim was not killed, nor on intellectually disabled defendants or offenders under 18 at the time of the crime.

title: Debtor Name for Individual
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: For an individual debtor, the financing statement must comply with the applicable jurisdiction's rule, often requiring either the name on the debtor's unexpired driver's license or, in some jurisdictions, the individual's correct personal name.

title: Debtor Name for Registered Organization
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: For a registered organization, the financing statement must provide the name stated on the public organic record most recently filed with or issued by the jurisdiction of organization.

title: Debtor Rights After Default
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A debtor has the right to redeem collateral by paying the full balance before disposition.

title: Debtor Rights in Collateral
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A debtor has rights in collateral when the debtor has an ownership interest or the power to transfer rights.

title: Debtor's Rights in Collateral
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: The debtor must have rights in the collateral, or the power to transfer rights in the collateral to a secured party, before a security interest can attach.

title: Declarant — Definition
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The declarant is the person who made the statement.

title: Declarant Unavailable — Definition
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A declarant is unavailable when the declarant is exempted by privilege, refuses to testify, lacks memory, is dead or ill, or cannot be procured by reasonable means.

title: Declining Supplemental Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A federal court may decline supplemental jurisdiction if the state law claim is novel or complex, substantially predominates, or all claims supporting original jurisdiction have been dismissed.

title: Deeds — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A deed transfers an interest in land and requires execution and delivery to be effective.

title: Defamation
subject: Torts
ruleText: Defamation requires a defamatory statement of fact concerning the plaintiff, publication to a third person, fault by the defendant, falsity where required, and damages unless the statement is actionable per se.

title: Defamation — Damages
subject: Torts
ruleText: In defamation per se cases, damages are presumed, while in other cases the plaintiff must prove actual damages.

title: Defamation — Defamation Per Se
subject: Torts
ruleText: Defamation per se involves statements concerning crime, loathsome disease, professional incompetence, or sexual misconduct.

title: Defamation — Definition
subject: Torts
ruleText: Defamation is a false statement of fact concerning the plaintiff that is published to a third party and harms the plaintiff’s reputation.

title: Defamation — Identification
subject: Torts
ruleText: The defamatory statement must be of and concerning the plaintiff.

title: Defamation — Libel and Slander
subject: Torts
ruleText: Libel is defamation in written or recorded form, while slander is defamation in spoken form.

title: Defamation — Private Figure
subject: Torts
ruleText: A private figure must prove negligence regarding the truth or falsity of the statement to recover for defamation.

title: Defamation — Privileges
subject: Torts
ruleText: Absolute or qualified privileges may protect a defendant from defamation liability.

title: Defamation — Public Figure or Public Official
subject: Torts
ruleText: A public figure or public official must prove actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for the truth.

title: Defamation — Publication
subject: Torts
ruleText: Publication occurs when the defamatory statement is communicated to any third person who understands it.

title: Defamation — Truth as a Defense
subject: Torts
ruleText: Truth is an absolute defense to defamation.

title: Defamation of Public Officials and Figures
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A public official or public figure must prove falsity and actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for truth, by clear and convincing evidence.

title: Defamation Per Se
subject: Torts
ruleText: Statements are traditionally defamatory per se if they impute serious crime, loathsome disease, unchastity, or conduct adversely affecting business, trade, profession, or office.

title: Default
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Default may be entered when a party fails to plead or otherwise defend.

title: Default
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Article 9 does not define default, so default is determined by the parties' agreement and applicable law.

title: Default — Secured Party Rights
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Upon default, a secured party may repossess collateral without breach of the peace and dispose of it commercially reasonably.

title: Default Judgment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A default judgment may be entered when a defendant fails to plead or otherwise defend.

title: Default Judgment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Default judgment is the judgment entered against a party after default, subject to the federal rules and any required procedures.

title: Defeasible Fees
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A defeasible fee is a fee simple that may end upon the occurrence or nonoccurrence of a specified event.

title: Defects Making Title Unmarketable
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Title is commonly unmarketable if subject to adverse possession claims, undisclosed encumbrances, zoning violations, or other defects likely to trigger litigation.

title: Defendants in Products Liability
subject: Torts
ruleText: Manufacturers, wholesalers, retailers, and commercial sellers may be held liable for defective products.

title: Defense of Others
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person may use reasonable force to defend another when the defender reasonably believes the other person is entitled to use self-defense.

title: Defense of Others
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A defendant may use force in defense of others when the defendant reasonably believes the defended person may lawfully use such force.

title: Defense of Others
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A defendant may use force to defend others if the defendant reasonably believes the other person would have the right to self-defense.

title: Defense of Others
subject: Torts
ruleText: Defense of others is justified when the defendant reasonably believes that the person defended would have the right to use self-defense.

title: Defense of Property
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person may use reasonable force to prevent or terminate an unlawful intrusion upon property but may not use deadly force.

title: Defense of Property
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Reasonable nondeadly force may be used to prevent unlawful interference with property, but deadly force may not be used solely to protect property.

title: Defense of Property
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person may use reasonable nondeadly force to prevent or terminate another's intrusion on land or chattels after a request to desist if feasible, but may not use deadly force solely to protect property.

title: Defense of Property
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A defendant may use reasonable non-deadly force to protect property but may not use deadly force solely to protect property.

title: Defenses — Assumption of Risk
subject: Torts
ruleText: Assumption of risk is a defense to products liability when the plaintiff knowingly and voluntarily encounters the risk.

title: Defenses — Comparative Fault
subject: Torts
ruleText: Comparative fault may reduce recovery in products liability when the plaintiff’s misuse or negligence contributes to the injury.

title: Defenses — Justification or Privilege
subject: Torts
ruleText: Justification or privilege is a defense when the defendant acts to protect a legitimate economic interest using lawful means.

title: Defenses to Abnormally Dangerous Activity Claims
subject: Torts
ruleText: Assumption of risk is a defense to strict liability, while ordinary contributory negligence usually is not unless the plaintiff knowingly and unreasonably encountered the risk.

title: Defenses to Fault Divorce
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Traditional defenses to a fault-based divorce include recrimination, condonation, connivance, collusion, and provocation, though the modern significance of these defenses varies.

title: Defenses to Recognition of Judgments
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: A party resisting recognition of a sister-state judgment may challenge the rendering court's jurisdiction, claim the judgment is not final or valid, or raise certain due process objections, but may not relitigate the merits once jurisdiction and validity are established.

title: Defenses to Strict Liability
subject: Torts
ruleText: Comparative negligence is not a defense to strict liability, but assumption of risk is a defense.

title: Deficiency and Surplus
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: If collateral-sale proceeds exceed the secured obligation and proper expenses, the debtor is entitled to a surplus; if proceeds are insufficient, the obligor is liable for the deficiency unless the law or agreement limits recovery.

title: Deficiency and Surplus
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: After disposition, the secured party may seek a deficiency or must return any surplus.

title: Deficiency Judgment
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If foreclosure sale proceeds are insufficient to satisfy the debt, the lender may generally seek a deficiency judgment against a personally liable borrower, subject to statutory limits.

title: Deficiency Judgment
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If foreclosure sale proceeds are insufficient, the lender may seek a deficiency judgment.

title: Definition of Hearsay
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement.

title: Degrees of Murder — First Degree
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: First-degree murder requires willful, deliberate, and premeditated killing or murder committed during enumerated felonies.

title: Degrees of Murder — Second Degree
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Second-degree murder includes all other murders that do not qualify as first-degree murder.

title: Delegable Duties
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Contractual duties may be delegated unless performance depends on the personal skill or judgment of the obligor or delegation is prohibited by contract.

title: Delegation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Delegation is the transfer of a contractual duty to perform from one party to another, but the delegating party usually remains liable absent novation.

title: Delegation — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A delegation is a transfer of contractual duties from the delegator to the delegatee.

title: Delegation by Trustee
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A trustee may delegate investment and management functions that a prudent trustee of comparable skills could properly delegate, but must use reasonable care in selecting the agent, setting the scope of delegation, and reviewing the agent's actions.

title: Delegation of Legislative Power
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may delegate legislative authority if it provides an intelligible principle to guide the exercise of the delegated power.

title: Deliberate Elicitation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: After attachment, police may not deliberately elicit incriminating statements from the defendant about the charged offense without counsel or valid waiver.

title: Delivery of Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A deed is effective only upon delivery, meaning the grantor manifests present intent that the deed operate to transfer an interest.

title: Delivery of Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Delivery of a deed requires intent to make the deed presently effective.

title: Delivery of Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under the majority English rule, the landlord must deliver actual possession at the start of the lease; under the minority American rule, the landlord need only deliver legal possession.

title: Demand Requirement
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Before filing a derivative action, a shareholder usually must make a written demand requesting that the corporation bring the action, and then wait the required statutory period unless irreparable injury would result or demand is excused under applicable law.

title: Depecage
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Depecage is the process of applying the laws of different jurisdictions to different issues within the same case.

title: Dependent Relative Revocation
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Dependent relative revocation allows a court to disregard a revocation if the testator revoked a will or gift based on a mistaken assumption of law or fact and would not have revoked but for that mistake, provided disregarding the revocation better carries out the testator's probable intent.

title: Dependent Relative Revocation
subject: Trusts
ruleText: Dependent relative revocation applies when a testator revokes a will based on a mistaken belief.

title: Deposit Account
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A deposit account is a demand, time, savings, passbook, or similar account maintained with a bank, but it does not include investment property or accounts evidenced by an instrument.

title: Deposition Limits
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Depositions are subject to numerical, temporal, and proportionality limits under the federal rules and court supervision.

title: Depositions — Limits
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Each deposition is limited to one day of seven hours unless otherwise ordered by the court.

title: Depraved-Heart Murder
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Depraved-heart murder is a killing caused by reckless conduct manifesting extreme indifference to human life.

title: Derivative Action
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A derivative action is a suit brought by a shareholder on behalf of the corporation to enforce a corporate cause of action when those in control of the corporation fail to do so.

title: Derivative Action Requirements
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A derivative plaintiff generally must have been a shareholder at the time of the challenged act or have acquired shares by operation of law, must fairly and adequately represent the corporation's interests, and must make a written demand on the corporation unless demand is excused where recognized by law.

title: Description of Collateral
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Collateral is sufficiently described if it reasonably identifies what is described, but supergeneric descriptions are insufficient in a security agreement.

title: Design Defect
subject: Torts
ruleText: A design defect exists when the product design creates a foreseeable risk of harm that could have been reduced or avoided by a reasonable alternative design and the failure to adopt that design renders the product not reasonably safe.

title: Design Defect — Consumer Expectation Test
subject: Torts
ruleText: A design defect exists when a product is more dangerous than an ordinary consumer would expect.

title: Design Defect — Risk-Utility Test
subject: Torts
ruleText: A design defect exists when the foreseeable risks of harm could have been reduced or avoided by a reasonable alternative design.

title: Designated Public Forums
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A designated public forum is government property intentionally opened for expressive activity, and speech restrictions there are reviewed similarly to those in a traditional public forum.

title: Destructibility of Contingent Remainders
subject: Real Property
ruleText: At common law, a contingent remainder had to vest at or before termination of the preceding freehold estate or it was destroyed, but most jurisdictions have abolished this rule.

title: Destruction of Subject Matter
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Destruction of the subject matter discharges contractual duties when the subject matter essential to performance is destroyed without fault.

title: Diminished Capacity
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Diminished capacity allows evidence of mental abnormality short of insanity to negate a specific-intent element in jurisdictions recognizing the doctrine.

title: Direct Action
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A shareholder may bring a direct action for injuries suffered individually, such as denial of voting rights, denial of inspection rights, or oppression, rather than injuries suffered by the corporation as a whole.

title: Direct Cause Test
subject: Torts
ruleText: Under the direct cause test, the defendant is liable if the harm results directly from the defendant’s conduct without an intervening cause.

title: Director Conflict-of-Interest Transaction
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A director's conflicting-interest transaction is generally not automatically void if it is disclosed and approved by disinterested directors or shareholders, or if the transaction was fair to the corporation at the time it was entered.

title: Director Duty of Care
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A director must act in good faith, with the care that a person in a like position would reasonably believe appropriate under similar circumstances, and in a manner the director reasonably believes to be in the best interests of the corporation.

title: Director Duty of Loyalty
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A director owes the corporation a duty of loyalty and must not engage in self-dealing, usurp corporate opportunities, compete unfairly with the corporation, or otherwise place personal interests above the corporation's interests.

title: Director Liability for Unlawful Distribution
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Directors who vote for or assent to an unlawful distribution may be personally liable to the corporation for the amount exceeding what could have been properly distributed if they failed to comply with the applicable standard of conduct.

title: Director's Objection
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A director who is present at a meeting when corporate action is taken is generally deemed to have assented unless the director objects at the beginning of the meeting or promptly upon arrival to holding it or transacting business, or the director votes against or abstains and the abstention or dissent is recorded.

title: Disabilities
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If the true owner is under a statutory disability such as minority, insanity, or imprisonment when adverse possession begins, the statutory period may be tolled or extended, but later-arising disabilities usually do not count and disabilities generally cannot be tacked.

title: Disabilities — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The statutory period is tolled if the true owner is under a disability at the start of adverse possession.

title: Discharge — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Discharge terminates contractual duties when performance is no longer required.

title: Discharge by Novation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A novation discharges the original duty when the parties agree that a new obligor or new contract replaces the original one.

title: Disclaimer
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A valid disclaimer causes the disclaimant to be treated as having predeceased the decedent for purposes of the disclaimed interest.

title: Disclaimer of Implied Warranties
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Implied warranties may be disclaimed by language meeting UCC requirements, such as mention of merchantability for that warranty, or by expressions like as is, subject to statutory rules.

title: Disclosure of Inadmissible Facts
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Inadmissible facts underlying an expert’s opinion may be disclosed to the jury only if their probative value substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect.

title: Disclosure Requirements
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A limited partnership must generally comply with statutory naming and filing requirements so that third parties can determine the entity's existence and identify persons with management authority.

title: Discovery — Privilege
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Privileged matters, including attorney-client communications, are not discoverable.

title: Discovery — Proportionality Factors
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Proportionality considers the importance of the issues, amount in controversy, access to information, resources of the parties, and burden versus benefit.

title: Discovery — Sanctions
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Courts may impose sanctions for failure to comply with discovery obligations.

title: Discovery — Scope
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Parties may obtain discovery regarding any nonprivileged matter that is relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case.

title: Discovery Devices
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Discovery devices include depositions, interrogatories, requests for production, requests for admission, and physical or mental examinations.

title: Discovery Sanctions
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may impose sanctions for failure to comply with discovery obligations or court orders, including orders compelling discovery.

title: Discretionary Trust and Creditors
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Because the beneficiary cannot compel a distribution from a purely discretionary trust, most creditors likewise cannot compel a distribution, though some exception creditors may reach distributions once the trustee decides to make them or if discretion is abused.

title: Discriminatory Intent
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A facially neutral law violates equal protection only if it has a discriminatory impact and discriminatory intent.

title: Discriminatory State Laws
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A state law that discriminates against interstate commerce on its face, in purpose, or in practical effect is usually invalid unless the state proves it is necessary to achieve an important noneconomic purpose and no reasonable nondiscriminatory alternative exists.

title: Disposition of Collateral
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: After default, a secured party may sell, lease, license, or otherwise dispose of collateral in its present condition or following commercially reasonable preparation or processing.

title: Dissociation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Dissociation is the change in the relationship among the partners caused by a partner's ceasing to be associated in carrying on the business. It may be rightful or wrongful and does not always cause dissolution.

title: Dissolution and Winding Up
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Dissolution is the event triggering the winding up of partnership business. After dissolution, the partnership continues only for the purpose of winding up, including completing unfinished business, collecting assets, paying creditors, and distributing the surplus.

title: Distribution of Foreclosure Surplus
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Any foreclosure surplus is distributed first to junior lienholders in order of priority and then to the mortgagor.

title: Distributions and Dividends
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Corporations may make distributions only if solvent, meaning they can pay debts as they come due and assets exceed liabilities.

title: Distributions to Shareholders
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A corporation may make distributions to shareholders unless, after giving effect to the distribution, the corporation would be unable to pay its debts as they become due in the usual course of business or its total assets would be less than the sum of its total liabilities and any preferential amounts due to superior shares on dissolution.
buzzwords: unable to pay debts as they become due

title: Diversity Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Diversity jurisdiction exists when there is complete diversity between opposing parties and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs.

title: Diversity Jurisdiction — General Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Diversity jurisdiction exists when there is complete diversity between plaintiffs and defendants and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000.

title: Divorce — Grounds and Jurisdiction
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Divorce may be granted on fault or no-fault grounds, and jurisdiction exists where at least one spouse is domiciled in the state.

title: Doctrine of Waste
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A life tenant has a duty not to commit waste that unreasonably impairs the future interest.

title: Doctrine of Worthier Title
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The Doctrine of Worthier Title treated a remainder in the grantor's heirs as a reversion in the grantor, but most jurisdictions have abolished or limited the doctrine.

title: Documents
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Documents are documents of title, including bills of lading, dock warrants, dock receipts, warehouse receipts, and orders for delivery of goods, and may be tangible or electronic if recognized by law.

title: Domestic Animals with Known Dangerous Propensities
subject: Torts
ruleText: The owner of a domestic animal is strictly liable if the owner knew or should have known of the animal's dangerous propensities abnormal to its class.

title: Domestic Violence — Protective Orders
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Courts may issue protective orders to prevent domestic violence, including ex parte orders based on affidavits of abuse or threats.

title: Domestic Violence and Custody
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Domestic violence is a major factor in custody determinations and may create a presumption against awarding custody to an abusive parent under applicable statute, because abuse directly affects the child's safety and best interests.

title: Domicile Definition
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Domicile is the place where a person has a physical presence with the intent to make that place a permanent home or to remain there indefinitely.

title: Domicile of Children
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: A child's domicile is generally the domicile of the child's parents or legal custodian, unless modified by statute or court order.

title: Domicile of Corporations
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: For many conflict and jurisdiction purposes, a corporation is associated with its state of incorporation and often the state of its principal place of business.

title: Dormant Commerce Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: State and local laws may not discriminate against or unduly burden interstate commerce, even when Congress has not acted.

title: Dormant Commerce Clause — Discrimination
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: State laws that discriminate against out-of-state commerce are presumptively invalid unless narrowly tailored to serve an important state interest.

title: Dormant Commerce Clause — General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: State laws may not discriminate against or unduly burden interstate commerce.

title: Dormant Commerce Clause — Market Participant
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: When a state acts as a market participant, it may favor its own citizens over others.

title: Double Jeopardy — General Rule
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Double Jeopardy Clause prohibits multiple prosecutions or multiple punishments for the same offense.

title: Dram Shop Liability
subject: Torts
ruleText: A commercial vendor may be liable for serving alcohol to an intoxicated person who later causes injury to a third party.

title: Dual-Status and Transformation Rules
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: In nonconsumer-goods transactions, many jurisdictions apply the dual-status rule, allowing a security interest to remain purchase-money to the extent it secures purchase-money obligations, even if mixed with non-purchase-money debt. Consumer-goods transactions may instead apply either a dual-status or transformation approach depending on jurisdiction.

title: Due Process — Minimum Contacts
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Due process requires that the defendant have minimum contacts with the forum such that jurisdiction does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.

title: Due Process — Suggestive Identification
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An identification violates due process if the procedure was unnecessarily suggestive and created a substantial likelihood of misidentification.

title: Due Process Clause — General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Due Process Clause prohibits the government from depriving any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law.

title: Due Process Notice
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Due process requires notice reasonably calculated under the circumstances to apprise interested parties of the action and give them an opportunity to be heard.

title: Duplicates
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A duplicate is admissible to the same extent as an original unless there is a genuine question about authenticity or it would be unfair.

title: Duplicates
subject: Evidence
topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
ruleText: A duplicate is admissible to the same extent as the original unless there is a genuine question about the original's authenticity or the circumstances make it unfair to admit the duplicate.

title: Duration of Felony Murder Liability
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Felony murder liability lasts through the felony and immediate flight until the felons reach a place of temporary safety.

title: Duration of Financing Statement
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A filed financing statement is generally effective for five years from the date of filing unless a continuation statement is timely filed.

title: Duress
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Duress is a defense when the defendant commits a crime due to an immediate threat of death or serious bodily injury.

title: Duress
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Duress renders a contract voidable when assent is induced by an improper threat that leaves the victim with no reasonable alternative.

title: Duress
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract is voidable for duress when assent is induced by an improper threat leaving the victim no reasonable alternative.

title: Duress
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Duress is a defense when the defendant commits a crime due to a threat of imminent death or serious bodily harm that would cause a reasonable person to yield.

title: Duress
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A will or provision is invalid for duress if the testator was forced to make the disposition by a wrongful threat that overcame the testator's free will.

title: Duress — Limitation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Duress is not a defense to intentional homicide.

title: Duty — Children on Land
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner owes a duty of reasonable care to child trespassers when an artificial condition poses an unreasonable risk of harm.

title: Duty — Common Carrier
subject: Torts
ruleText: A common carrier owes passengers a duty of reasonable care under the circumstances.

title: Duty — Employer
subject: Torts
ruleText: An employer owes employees a duty to provide a reasonably safe workplace.

title: Duty — General Rule
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person owes a duty to exercise reasonable care to avoid foreseeable risks of harm to others.

title: Duty — Innkeeper
subject: Torts
ruleText: An innkeeper owes guests a duty of reasonable care to protect against foreseeable harms.

title: Duty — Landlord
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landlord owes a duty of reasonable care for common areas under the landlord’s control.

title: Duty — Landowner to Invitees
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner owes invitees a duty to use reasonable care to inspect, discover, and remedy dangerous conditions.

title: Duty — Landowner to Licensees
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner owes licensees a duty to warn of known dangerous conditions not obvious to the licensee.

title: Duty — Landowner to Trespassers
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner owes trespassers a duty to refrain from willful or wanton misconduct.

title: Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress
subject: Torts
ruleText: A duty exists for negligent infliction of emotional distress when a defendant’s negligence causes foreseeable emotional harm.

title: Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress (Bystander)
subject: Torts
ruleText: A bystander may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress when the bystander closely relates to the victim and contemporaneously perceives the injury.

title: Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress (Zone of Danger)
subject: Torts
ruleText: A plaintiff may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress when the plaintiff is within the zone of danger and suffers emotional distress.

title: Duty — No Duty to Act
subject: Torts
ruleText: There is no general duty to act or rescue another person absent a special relationship.

title: Duty — Parent to Child
subject: Torts
ruleText: A parent owes a duty to exercise reasonable care to control a child to prevent foreseeable harm.

title: Duty — Special Relationships
subject: Torts
ruleText: A duty to act arises when a special relationship exists, such as common carrier–passenger, innkeeper–guest, employer–employee, or parent–child.

title: Duty — To Whom (Foreseeable Plaintiffs)
subject: Torts
ruleText: A duty of care is owed only to foreseeable plaintiffs who are foreseeably at risk from the defendant’s conduct.

title: Duty — Undertaking
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person who voluntarily undertakes to render services has a duty to act with reasonable care.

title: Duty of Impartiality
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If a trust has multiple beneficiaries, the trustee must act impartially in investing, managing, and distributing trust property, giving due regard to each beneficiary's respective interests.

title: Duty to Control Third Parties
subject: Torts
ruleText: A duty to control the conduct of a third party may arise when the defendant has a special relationship with either the third party or the potential victim and the harm is foreseeable.

title: Duty to Inform and Account
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A trustee must keep qualified beneficiaries reasonably informed about trust administration and material facts necessary to protect their interests, and must provide accountings as required by statute or trust terms.

title: Duty to Invitees
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner owes an invitee a duty to use reasonable care to protect against known dangers and those that would be revealed by reasonable inspection.

title: Duty to Licensees
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner owes a licensee a duty to warn of known dangerous conditions that the licensee is unlikely to discover.

title: Duty to Mitigate
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A landlord has a duty to mitigate damages by making reasonable efforts to re-rent the premises.

title: Duty to Segregate and Earmark
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A trustee must keep trust property separate from the trustee's own property and properly earmark it as trust property.

title: Duty to Supplement
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A party must timely supplement discovery responses if they learn the information is incomplete or incorrect.

title: Duty to Support Child
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Both legal parents owe a duty to support their child, and the amount is generally determined under statutory guidelines based primarily on the parents' incomes and the child's needs.

title: Duty to Trespassers
subject: Torts
ruleText: A landowner generally owes an undiscovered trespasser only a duty to refrain from willful, wanton, or intentional misconduct, but owes a known or anticipated trespasser a duty to warn of known artificial hidden dangerous conditions.

title: Duty When Defendant Creates Peril
subject: Torts
ruleText: A defendant who creates a risk of harm, even innocently, generally has a duty to take reasonable steps to prevent or reduce the harm.

title: Dwelling
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A dwelling is a structure regularly used for sleeping, together with outbuildings within the curtilage.

title: Dying Declaration
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a prosecution for homicide or in a civil case, a statement made by a declarant while believing the declarant's death to be imminent, about its cause or circumstances, is admissible if the declarant is unavailable.

title: Dying Declaration
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A dying declaration is admissible when the declarant believes death is imminent and the statement concerns the cause or circumstances of death.

title: Dying Declaration — Case Limitation
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Dying declarations are admissible only in homicide prosecutions or civil cases.

title: Dying Declarations — Confrontation Clause
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Dying declarations are admissible even if testimonial and do not violate the Confrontation Clause.

title: Easement
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement is a nonpossessory right to use another's land for a particular purpose.

title: Easement Appurtenant
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement appurtenant benefits a dominant estate and burdens a servient estate.

title: Easement by Implication
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement by implication may arise when there was prior common ownership, prior use before severance, the use was apparent and continuous, and the easement is reasonably necessary to enjoyment of the dominant parcel.

title: Easement by Necessity
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement by necessity arises when a parcel is severed from common ownership and one part is left landlocked, making access strictly necessary.

title: Easement in Gross
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement in gross benefits a person rather than a parcel of land.

title: Easements — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An easement is a nonpossessory interest that grants the right to use another’s land.

title: Economic Damages
subject: Torts
ruleText: Economic damages compensate for monetary losses such as medical expenses and lost wages.

title: Economic Rights of Limited Partners
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Limited partners generally have the right to share in profits, losses, and distributions as provided by the partnership agreement, and they may transfer economic interests subject to agreement and statute.

title: Economic Substantive Due Process
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Economic regulations are reviewed under rational basis scrutiny.

title: Effect of Foreclosure on Interests
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Foreclosure extinguishes interests junior to the foreclosing mortgage if those holders are properly joined, but it does not eliminate senior interests.

title: Effect of Foreclosure on Senior Interests
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Senior interests are not affected by foreclosure and remain attached to the property.

title: Effect of Lapse
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Upon lapse, the financing statement ceases to be effective and the security interest becomes unperfected, and it is deemed never to have been perfected as against a purchaser of the collateral for value.
buzzwords: deemed never perfected against purchaser for value

title: Effect of Secured Party's Noncompliance
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A person entitled to notification or whose rights were affected may recover damages caused by a secured party's failure to comply with Article 9, and in consumer-goods transactions statutory minimum damages may apply. In nonconsumer cases, noncompliance may also create a rebuttable presumption that the collateral was worth at least the debt, limiting or eliminating a deficiency unless the secured party proves otherwise.

title: Effective Assistance of Counsel
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The defendant must show deficient performance by counsel and a reasonable probability that, but for the deficiency, the result would have been different.

title: Eggshell Plaintiff Rule
subject: Torts
ruleText: A defendant takes the plaintiff as found and is liable for the full extent of harm even if the plaintiff's unusual fragility makes the injury worse than expected.

title: Eggshell Plaintiff Rule
subject: Torts
ruleText: Under the eggshell plaintiff rule, a defendant is liable for all injuries caused, even if the plaintiff is unusually vulnerable.

title: Elective Share
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A surviving spouse may renounce the will and claim the statutory elective share, which is a forced share of the decedent's estate or augmented estate designed to protect the spouse from disinheritance.

title: Elective Share
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A surviving spouse may claim an elective share of the decedent’s estate.

title: Elements — Interference Torts
subject: Torts
ruleText: Interference torts require intentional conduct, knowledge of the relationship, causation, and damages.

title: Elements of a Valid Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A valid trust generally requires a settlor with capacity, present intent to create a trust, identifiable trust property, ascertainable beneficiaries unless the trust is charitable or honorary where allowed, a valid trust purpose, and compliance with any required formalities.

title: Eleventh Amendment Sovereign Immunity
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Eleventh Amendment generally bars suits in federal court against a state by private parties for damages or retroactive relief, absent consent, valid congressional abrogation under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment, or certain other exceptions.

title: Embezzlement
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Embezzlement is the fraudulent conversion of the personal property of another by a person already in lawful possession of that property.

title: Embezzlement
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Embezzlement is the fraudulent conversion of personal property by a person in lawful possession of the property.

title: Emergency Doctrine
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Statements made to assist police during an ongoing emergency are non-testimonial.

title: Eminent Domain — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Eminent domain allows the government to take private property for public use with just compensation.

title: Emotional Distress Without Physical Harm
subject: Torts
ruleText: Claims for emotional distress without physical harm are limited and generally require a recognized theory such as zone-of-danger or bystander recovery, depending on jurisdiction.

title: Employer Liability for Intentional Torts
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An employer may be vicariously liable for an employee's intentional tort if the act was within the scope of employment, such as when force is job-related, the act was motivated by a purpose to serve the employer, or the conduct was foreseeable in the employment context.

title: Enforcement under UCCJEA
subject: Family Law
ruleText: The UCCJEA provides procedures for registration and enforcement of child custody determinations made by other states, and courts generally must enforce a valid sister-state custody order.

title: Entrapment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Entrapment exists when police induce a crime that the defendant was not predisposed to commit.

title: Entrapment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Entrapment occurs when law enforcement induces a defendant to commit a crime that the defendant was not predisposed to commit.

title: Entry Into Third Party's Home
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police generally need a search warrant for the third party's home, in addition to an arrest warrant for the suspect or another applicable exception.

title: Entry of Judgment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Entry of judgment makes the court's decision operative and starts important post judgment and appellate deadlines.

title: Enumerated First-Degree Murder
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Many statutes classify as first-degree murder killings committed by poison, lying in wait, torture, or during certain enumerated felonies.

title: Equal Protection Clause — General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Equal Protection Clause requires that similarly situated persons be treated alike by the government.

title: Equal Protection Framework
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Equal protection analysis asks whether the government has drawn a classification and, if so, applies rational basis, intermediate scrutiny, or strict scrutiny depending on the classification or right burdened.

title: Equipment
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Equipment is a residual category of goods consisting of goods used or bought for use primarily in business that are not inventory, farm products, or consumer goods.

title: Equitable Conversion
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under equitable conversion, once a specifically enforceable land sale contract is formed, the buyer is viewed as the equitable owner and the seller holds legal title in trust pending closing.

title: Equitable Conversion
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under equitable conversion, the buyer is treated as the equitable owner of the land once the contract is signed.

title: Equitable Distribution Factors
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Courts in equitable distribution jurisdictions consider factors such as duration of marriage, age and health, income and earning capacity, contributions to the marriage, childcare responsibilities, economic misconduct where relevant, and each spouse's future financial circumstances.

title: Equitable Distribution System
subject: Family Law
ruleText: In an equitable distribution jurisdiction, the court identifies marital and separate property, values the marital estate, and divides marital property in a manner that is fair though not necessarily equal.

title: Equitable Servitude
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An equitable servitude is enforceable in equity if there is a writing or common scheme, intent that the promise run, the promise touches and concerns the land, and the burdened party has notice.

title: Equitable Servitude — Elements
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An equitable servitude requires intent, touch and concern, and notice.

title: Equitable Servitudes — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An equitable servitude is a covenant enforceable in equity that binds successors with notice.

title: Equity of Redemption
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The mortgagor has the right to redeem the property by paying the debt before foreclosure.

title: Equity of Redemption
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Before foreclosure, the mortgagor has the equitable right to redeem the property by paying the debt, interest, and costs, and this right cannot be clogged at the time the mortgage is created.

title: Erie Doctrine
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: In a diversity case, a federal court applies federal procedural law and state substantive law.

title: Erie Doctrine — General Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: In diversity cases, federal courts must apply federal procedural law and state substantive law.

title: Escrow Delivery
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If a grantor delivers a deed to an escrow agent with instructions to deliver upon satisfaction of conditions, title passes when the conditions are met and escrow is properly completed.

title: Establishment Clause — General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Establishment Clause prohibits government action that endorses, advances, or excessively entangles the government with religion.

title: Establishment Clause — Government Aid to Religion
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Government aid to religion is permitted if it is neutral, available to a broad class of recipients, and does not promote religion.

title: Establishment Clause — Lemon Test
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Under the Lemon test, a law must have a secular purpose, a primary effect that neither advances nor inhibits religion, and must not foster excessive government entanglement with religion.

title: Establishment Clause — Religious Displays
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Religious displays are constitutional if a reasonable observer would not perceive the display as government endorsement of religion.

title: Establishment Clause General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The government may not establish religion, favor one religion over another, or unduly entangle itself with religion.

title: Estoppel by Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If a grantor purports to convey property the grantor does not own and later acquires title, the title automatically passes to the grantee under estoppel by deed in many jurisdictions.

title: Evasion of Law
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Evasion of law refers to a party's attempt to circumvent the law of a state with a strong connection to the matter by creating formal ties to another jurisdiction, and courts may disregard such manipulation when justified.

title: Events Causing Dissolution
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Dissolution may occur by the partners' will, expiration of a term or completion of an undertaking, an event agreed upon in the partnership agreement, judicial decree, or in some cases certain partner dissociations in an at-will partnership.

title: Ex Parte Divorce
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A state may grant an ex parte divorce if it has jurisdiction over the plaintiff spouse's domicile, but without personal jurisdiction over the absent spouse, the court generally cannot adjudicate personal obligations such as support or property division against that spouse.

title: Ex parte Young Exception
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A plaintiff may sue a state officer in federal court for prospective injunctive relief to stop an ongoing violation of federal law.

title: Ex Post Facto Laws and Bills of Attainder
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: An ex post facto law retroactively imposes criminal punishment or increases punishment for past conduct, and a bill of attainder legislatively inflicts punishment on named persons or an identifiable group without a judicial trial; both are prohibited.

title: Exactions
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Land-use exactions must have an essential nexus and rough proportionality to the government interest.

title: Examples of Authentication
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Authentication may be shown by testimony of a witness with knowledge, distinctive characteristics and surrounding circumstances, voice identification, comparison by an expert or the trier of fact, evidence about telephone or electronic communications, chain of custody, and other methods recognized by the rules.

title: Examples of State Substantive Law
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: State substantive law generally includes elements of claims and defenses, statutes of limitation, choice of law rules, and rules affecting primary conduct or outcome in a significant way.

title: Exceptions for Independent Contractors
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A principal may be liable for an independent contractor's torts when the principal is negligent in selection, when the work is inherently dangerous, when a nondelegable duty is involved, or when the principal retains substantial control over the work.

title: Exceptions to Double Jeopardy Bar
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Retrial is generally allowed after mistrial for hung jury, defendant's successful appeal not based on insufficient evidence, or separate-sovereigns prosecutions.

title: Exceptions to Mootness
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A case may avoid mootness if the issue is capable of repetition yet evading review, the defendant voluntarily ceases the conduct but may resume it, a class action remains live, or collateral consequences persist.
buzzwords: capable of repetition yet evading review

title: Exceptions to Spendthrift Protection
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Common exceptions allow claims by child or spousal support claimants, providers of necessities, some governmental claims, and sometimes judgment creditors who provided services to protect the beneficiary's interest, depending on statute.

title: Excited Utterance
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement relating to a startling event or condition, made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement that it caused, is admissible as an excited utterance.

title: Excited Utterance
subject: Evidence
ruleText: An excited utterance is a statement relating to a startling event made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement caused by the event.

title: Exclusionary Rule
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Evidence obtained in violation of the Constitution may be excluded from the prosecution's case-in-chief, along with derivative evidence, unless an exception applies.

title: Exclusionary Rule — General Rule
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The exclusionary rule prohibits the use of evidence obtained in violation of the Fourth Amendment in the prosecution’s case-in-chief.

title: Exclusionary Rule — Scope
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The exclusionary rule applies only to criminal prosecutions and only to government misconduct.

title: Exclusive Continuing Jurisdiction
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Once a state validly makes a child custody determination, that state generally retains exclusive continuing jurisdiction until neither the child nor a parent has a significant connection with the state and substantial evidence is no longer available there, or all relevant persons leave the state.

title: Exclusive Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Exclusive possession requires that the possessor not share control of the property with the true owner or the public.

title: Exculpatory Clause
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: An exculpatory clause may relieve a trustee of liability for ordinary negligence in some jurisdictions, but it generally cannot excuse bad faith, reckless indifference, or abuse of a fiduciary relationship, and may be invalid if improperly inserted by the trustee.

title: Excuse of Conditions — Estoppel
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A condition may be excused by estoppel when a party’s conduct causes reasonable reliance that the condition will not be enforced.

title: Excuse of Conditions — Substantial Performance
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An express condition may be excused when a party has substantially performed under the contract.
