ruleText: Lay opinion testimony is admissible if it is rationally based on the witness's perception, helpful to understanding the witness's testimony or determining a fact in issue, and not based on scientific, technical, or other specialized knowledge within Rule 702.

title: Lay Opinion Testimony
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Lay opinion testimony is admissible if it is rationally based on the witness’s perception, helpful to understanding the testimony, and not based on scientific or technical knowledge.

title: Leading Questions
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Leading questions should not be used on direct examination except as necessary to develop the witness's testimony, but they are ordinarily allowed on cross-examination and with hostile witnesses, adverse parties, or witnesses identified with an adverse party.

title: Learned Treatise
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement contained in a treatise, periodical, or pamphlet may be read into evidence if called to the attention of an expert witness on cross-examination or relied on by the expert on direct examination, and the publication is established as a reliable authority by the expert, another expert, judicial notice, or other evidence. The statement may be read into evidence but not received as an exhibit.

title: Learned Treatises
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Statements in learned treatises may be read into evidence if established as reliable authority and relied upon by an expert.

title: Leasehold Estates — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A leasehold estate grants the tenant the right to exclusive possession of property for a period of time.

title: Leave to Amend
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Leave to amend should be freely given when justice so requires.

title: Legal and Physical Custody
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Legal custody concerns authority to make major decisions for the child, while physical custody concerns where the child lives and the day-to-day caretaking arrangement. Each may be sole or joint.

title: Legal Certainty Test
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: The amount in controversy requirement is not satisfied if it appears to a legal certainty that the plaintiff cannot recover more than $75,000.

title: Legal Duties to Act
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Legal duties to act arise from statute, contract, special relationship, voluntary assumption of care, or creation of the peril.

title: Legislative Veto
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A one-house or two-house legislative veto of executive action is unconstitutional because Congress must act through bicameralism and presentment unless the Constitution provides otherwise.

title: Legislative Veto
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may not invalidate executive action through a legislative veto without bicameralism and presentment.

title: Lesser Included Offenses
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Double jeopardy bars prosecution for a lesser included offense after conviction or acquittal of the greater offense.

title: Levels of Scrutiny — Overview
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Equal protection challenges are analyzed under strict scrutiny, intermediate scrutiny, or rational basis review depending on the classification.

title: Liability After Delegation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: The delegator remains liable under the contract unless there is a novation.

title: Liability After Dissolution
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: After dissolution, a partnership may be bound by acts appropriate for winding up or by acts that would have bound the partnership before dissolution if the third party lacked notice of the dissolution, subject to statutory filing and notice provisions.

title: Liability After Sublease
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A sublessee is generally not in privity of estate or privity of contract with the landlord and is usually liable only to the original tenant absent an agreement with the landlord.

title: Liability Insurance
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: Evidence that a person was or was not insured against liability is not admissible to prove whether the person acted negligently or otherwise wrongfully, but it may be admissible for another purpose, such as proving bias, agency, ownership, or control.

title: Liability Insurance — Permitted Uses
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of liability insurance may be admitted to prove ownership, control, or bias.

title: Liberty Interests
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Liberty interests include freedom from bodily restraint and certain important reputational or status harms when combined with a change in legal status, as well as some interests created by law.

title: License
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A license is a revocable privilege to enter another's land and does not create an interest in land.

title: Lien Creditor Versus Secured Party
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A lien creditor generally takes priority over an unperfected security interest but is subordinate to a perfected security interest. A lien creditor also loses to a security interest that becomes perfected by filing before the lien arises, even if attachment occurs later.

title: Life Estate
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A life estate is an estate measured by the life of a specified person.

title: Life Estate
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A life estate is an estate measured by the life of a person and ends automatically at that person's death.

title: Life Estate Pur Autre Vie
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A life estate pur autre vie is a life estate measured by the life of someone other than the holder of the estate.

title: Limitations on Damages — Certainty
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Damages must be proven with reasonable certainty and may not be speculative.

title: Limitations on Damages — Foreseeability
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Damages are recoverable only if they were foreseeable at the time of contracting.

title: Limitations on Damages — Mitigation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: The non-breaching party has a duty to mitigate damages and cannot recover losses that could have been avoided.

title: Limited and Nonpublic Forums
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: In a limited or nonpublic forum, the government may impose restrictions that are reasonable and viewpoint neutral.

title: Limited Liability Companies (LLCs)
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: LLCs combine limited liability of corporations with pass-through taxation and flexible management governed primarily by an operating agreement.

title: Limited Liability Partnership
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A limited liability partnership is created by filing the required statement with the state, and its principal effect is that partners are generally not personally liable for obligations of the partnership solely by reason of being partners.

title: Limited Partner Liability
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A limited partner is generally not personally liable for the obligations of the limited partnership solely by reason of being a limited partner, even if the limited partner participates in management under modern statutes.

title: Limited Partnership Formation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A limited partnership is formed by filing a certificate of limited partnership or similar document with the appropriate state office, naming at least one general partner and one or more limited partners.

title: Limited Partnerships (LP and LLP)
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: In a limited partnership, general partners manage and are personally liable, while limited partners have limited liability if they do not participate in control; LLPs limit liability for partnership obligations.

title: Limits on Assignment
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Assignment may be ineffective if it materially changes the obligor's duty or risk, is prohibited by law, or is validly barred by contract or public policy.

title: Limits on Commerce Power Over Noneconomic Activity
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress generally may not regulate noneconomic intrastate activity based solely on attenuated effects on interstate commerce, unless the regulation is part of a broader regulatory scheme and the activity is essential to that scheme.

title: Limits on Judicial Power
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may limit the jurisdiction of federal courts, but may not dictate the outcome of particular cases.

title: Lineups — Right to Counsel
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A defendant has the right to counsel at post-indictment lineups and showups.

title: Liquidated Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A liquidated damages clause is enforceable if the amount is a reasonable forecast of anticipated damages and actual damages would be difficult to calculate.

title: Liquidated Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A liquidated damages clause is enforceable if damages were difficult to estimate at formation and the stipulated amount is a reasonable forecast of probable loss.

title: LLC — Fiduciary Duties and Management
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: LLC managers and members owe fiduciary duties as defined by statute and the operating agreement, which may modify but not eliminate the duty of loyalty in many states.

title: LLC Dissociation and Dissolution
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A member may dissociate under the statute or operating agreement, and dissolution may occur upon events specified in the operating agreement, member consent, expiration of duration, judicial decree, or other statutory grounds, followed by winding up of LLC affairs.

title: LLC Fiduciary Duties
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Depending on the statute and operating agreement, members in a member-managed LLC and managers in a manager-managed LLC generally owe duties of loyalty and care to the LLC and, in some circumstances, to the other members, though the operating agreement may modify these duties within statutory bounds.

title: LLC Formation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A limited liability company is formed by filing articles of organization or a certificate of formation with the state, creating a separate legal entity distinct from its members.

title: Location of Individual Debtor
subject: Secured Transactions
subtopic: Choice of Law and Filing Location
ruleText: An individual debtor is located at the individual's principal residence.

title: Location of Registered Organization
subject: Secured Transactions
subtopic: Choice of Law and Filing Location
ruleText: A registered organization organized under state law is located in its state of organization.

title: Location of Unregistered Organization
subject: Secured Transactions
subtopic: Choice of Law and Filing Location
ruleText: An organization that is not a registered organization is generally located at its place of business if it has only one, or at its chief executive office if it has more than one place of business.

title: Long Arm Statute
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court ordinarily needs a statute authorizing personal jurisdiction, such as a long arm statute, in addition to compliance with constitutional due process.

title: Long-Arm Statute
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A long-arm statute authorizes personal jurisdiction over nonresident defendants to the extent permitted by due process.

title: Loss of Chance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Some jurisdictions allow a medical malpractice plaintiff to recover for the loss of a substantial chance of a better outcome caused by negligence.

title: Loss-Allocating and Conduct-Regulating Rules
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Some modern approaches distinguish conduct-regulating rules, such as safety standards, from loss-allocating rules, such as damage caps or immunities, because the states with the strongest interests may differ depending on the kind of rule at issue.

title: Lost or Destroyed Originals
subject: Evidence
topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
ruleText: An original is not required, and other evidence of the content is admissible, if all originals are lost or destroyed and not by the proponent acting in bad faith; an original cannot be obtained by available judicial process; the party against whom it would be offered had control of the original and failed to produce it after notice; or the writing is not closely related to a controlling issue.

title: Lost Volume Seller
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A lost volume seller is one who could have made both the breached sale and the resale, and may recover lost profits when ordinary resale or market damages are inadequate.

title: Lost Will Presumption
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If a will last known to be in the testator's possession cannot be found at death, there is a rebuttable presumption that the testator destroyed it with intent to revoke.

title: M'Naghten Test
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under M'Naghten, the defendant is insane if, because of mental disease or defect, the defendant did not know the nature and quality of the act or did not know it was wrong.

title: Mailbox Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Acceptance is effective upon dispatch if properly addressed and mailed, unless the offer provides otherwise.

title: Mailbox Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Unless the offer provides otherwise, an acceptance sent by an authorized means is effective upon dispatch.

title: Major Statute of Frauds Categories
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Common Statute of Frauds categories include marriage contracts, suretyship promises, contracts not performable within one year, contracts for interests in land, and contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more under the UCC.

title: Malice Aforethought
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Malice aforethought is satisfied by intent to kill, intent to inflict serious bodily injury, depraved-heart recklessness, or felony murder.

title: Malice Aforethought — Depraved Heart
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Malice aforethought exists when the defendant acts with reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life.

title: Malice Aforethought — Felony Murder
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Malice aforethought is implied when a death occurs during the commission or attempted commission of a felony.

title: Malice Aforethought — Intent to Cause Serious Bodily Injury
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Malice aforethought exists when the defendant intends to cause serious bodily injury and death results.

title: Malice Aforethought — Intent to Kill
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Malice aforethought exists when the defendant intends to kill another human being.

title: Management Rights
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Absent agreement otherwise, all partners have equal rights in the management and conduct of the partnership business, and ordinary matters are decided by a majority of the partners while matters outside the ordinary course generally require unanimous consent.

title: Manager Authority
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Managers in a manager-managed LLC have actual and often apparent authority to bind the LLC in the ordinary course of the LLC's business, subject to limitations in the operating agreement and statutory notice rules.

title: Manager-Managed LLC
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: In a manager-managed LLC, management authority is vested in one or more managers rather than the members, and ordinary management powers belong to the managers, while members usually act more like passive owners unless the operating agreement provides otherwise.

title: Mandatory Disposition of Consumer Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: If a secured party has taken possession of consumer goods and the debtor has paid 60 percent of the cash price in a purchase-money transaction or 60 percent of the principal amount of a non-purchase-money obligation secured by the goods, the secured party must dispose of the collateral within 90 days after taking possession unless the debtor waives the right after default.

title: Mandatory Initial Disclosures
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Parties must provide certain basic information early in the case without awaiting a discovery request, including likely witnesses, relevant documents, damages information, and insurance information.

title: Manufacturing Defect
subject: Torts
ruleText: A manufacturing defect exists when a product departs from its intended design even though all possible care was exercised.

title: Manufacturing Defect
subject: Torts
ruleText: A manufacturing defect exists when a product departs from its intended design and is more dangerous than consumers expect.

title: Marital and Separate Debt
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Debts incurred during marriage for marital purposes are generally marital debts, while debts clearly tied to separate purposes or separate property may be treated as separate debts, subject to statutory variation.

title: Marital Communications Privilege
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The marital communications privilege protects confidential communications made between spouses during a valid marriage. Either spouse may assert the privilege, and it survives divorce as to communications made during the marriage.

title: Marital Presumption of Parentage
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A child born to a married person is generally presumed to be the child of that person's spouse, though the presumption may be rebutted under applicable law.

title: Marital Property
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Marital property generally includes property acquired by either spouse during the marriage, except for property classified as separate by statute or agreement.

title: Market Participant Exception
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: When a state acts as a market participant rather than a regulator, it may favor its own citizens in dealing with government-owned businesses or resources.

title: Market Reports and Commercial Publications
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Market quotations, lists, directories, or other compilations generally relied on by the public or by persons in particular occupations are admissible.

title: Marketable Title
subject: Real Property
ruleText: At closing, the seller must provide marketable title, meaning title reasonably free from doubt and not exposing the buyer to probable litigation.

title: Marketable Title — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: At closing, the seller must deliver marketable title free from unreasonable doubt.

title: Marriage — Common Law Marriage
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Where recognized, a common law marriage requires capacity, present intent to be married, cohabitation, and holding out as married.

title: Marriage — Formation Requirements
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A valid marriage requires legal capacity, mutual consent, and compliance with statutory formalities, unless an exception applies.

title: Marriage — Void and Voidable Marriages
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Void marriages are invalid from inception, while voidable marriages are valid unless annulled due to defects such as fraud, duress, incapacity, or age.

title: Massiah Doctrine
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under the Massiah doctrine, the government violates the Sixth Amendment by deliberately eliciting statements from a charged defendant through an informant.

title: Material Breach
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A material breach is a substantial failure of performance that permits the nonbreaching party to withhold its own performance and sue for damages.

title: Material Breach
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A material breach excuses the non-breaching party from further performance under the contract.

title: Mathews Balancing Test
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Courts balance the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous deprivation and value of additional safeguards, and the government's interest including administrative burden.

title: Mayhem
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mayhem occurs when a defendant intentionally maims or disfigures another person.

title: Member Liability
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: LLC members are generally not personally liable for the debts, obligations, or liabilities of the LLC solely by reason of being members or participating in management.

title: Member-Managed LLC
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: In a member-managed LLC, each member has authority to participate in management, and each member is generally an agent of the LLC for matters in the ordinary course of the LLC's activities.

title: Mens Rea — General Principle
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mens rea is the culpable mental state required to commit a crime.

title: Mental Disabilities
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person with a mental disability is generally held to the ordinary reasonable person standard.

title: Mental Incapacity
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract is voidable for mental incapacity if the person was unable to understand the nature and consequences of the transaction or was unable to act reasonably and the other party had reason to know.

title: Merchant Confirmatory Memo
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Between merchants, a signed confirmatory memo sufficient against the sender binds the recipient if the recipient has reason to know its contents and does not object in writing within 10 days.
buzzwords: does not object in writing within 10 days

title: Mere Presence
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mere presence at the scene of a crime or mere knowledge of the crime is insufficient to establish accomplice liability.

title: Merger — Attempt and Solicitation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Attempt and solicitation merge into the completed crime if the crime is successfully committed.

title: Merger — Conspiracy
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Conspiracy does not merge into the completed crime.

title: Merger by Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Upon delivery and acceptance of the deed, the land sale contract generally merges into the deed, and the buyer's rights are then determined by the deed's covenants rather than the contract, absent fraud or collateral promises.

title: Merger Clauses
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A merger clause provides strong evidence that an agreement is completely integrated.

title: Merger Doctrine
subject: Real Property
ruleText: At closing, the contract merges into the deed and contractual obligations are extinguished.

title: Merger Limitation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Felony murder does not apply when the underlying felony is an assaultive felony that is integral to the homicide.

title: Merger of Solicitation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Solicitation merges into conspiracy if the person solicited agrees, and into the completed offense if the crime is accomplished.

title: Mergers and Consolidations
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A merger generally requires board approval and shareholder approval by the requisite vote of each constituent corporation, unless a statutory short-form merger or other exception applies. The surviving entity succeeds to the rights and liabilities of the constituents by operation of law.

title: Methods of Proving Character
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: When evidence of a person's character or character trait is admissible, it may be proved by testimony about reputation or by testimony in the form of an opinion. On cross-examination of the character witness, the court may allow inquiry into relevant specific instances of conduct. When character is an essential element of a charge, claim, or defense, specific instances are also admissible.

title: Methods of Service — Corporations
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Corporations may be served by delivering process to an officer, managing agent, or authorized agent.

title: Methods of Service — Individuals
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Individuals may be served personally, at their dwelling with a suitable person, or by an authorized agent.

title: Minimum Contacts
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Personal jurisdiction is proper when the defendant has minimum contacts with the forum such that exercising jurisdiction does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.

title: Ministerial Exception
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Religion Clauses bar many employment-discrimination suits by ministers or similar key religious personnel against their religious institutions concerning hiring and firing decisions.

title: Minor's Capacity
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract entered by a minor is generally voidable at the minor's option, subject to liability for necessaries.

title: Minority Oppression in Close Corporations
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Close Corporations and Special Control Devices
ruleText: Minority oppression occurs when controlling shareholders in a close corporation frustrate the reasonable expectations of minority owners through exclusion from management, withholding distributions, termination of employment, or other unfair conduct. Courts may grant equitable relief, including buyouts or dissolution.

title: Miranda — General Rule
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Miranda warnings are required before custodial interrogation by law enforcement.

title: Miranda — Public Safety Exception
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Miranda warnings are not required when police questioning is reasonably prompted by an immediate concern for public safety.

title: Miranda — Waiver
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A Miranda waiver must be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent.

title: Miranda Warnings
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Miranda warnings are required before custodial interrogation by known government agents.

title: Miranda Warnings — Content
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Miranda warnings must inform the suspect of the right to remain silent, that statements may be used against the suspect, and the right to an attorney.

title: Mirror Image Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: At common law, acceptance must mirror the terms of the offer or it is treated as a counteroffer.

title: Misdemeanor Manslaughter
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An unintended killing during the commission of a misdemeanor or other unlawful act not qualifying for felony murder may constitute involuntary manslaughter.

title: Misrepresentation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract is voidable when a party's assent is induced by a fraudulent or material misrepresentation on which the party justifiably relied.

title: Misrepresentation — Fraudulent
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Fraudulent misrepresentation occurs when a party makes a false statement of material fact with intent to deceive, inducing reliance.

title: Misrepresentation — Negligent or Innocent
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract is voidable when a party justifiably relies on a false statement of material fact, even if the misrepresentation is negligent or innocent.

title: Mistake of Fact
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mistake of fact is a defense if it negates the required mens rea of the offense.

title: Mistake of Fact
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mistake of fact is a defense if it negates the required mens rea; for specific intent crimes, the mistake need only be honest, while for general intent crimes it usually must be honest and reasonable.

title: Mistake of Law
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mistake of law is generally no defense unless the defendant reasonably relies on an official statement of law or the law itself makes knowledge of illegality an element.

title: Mistake of Law
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Mistake of law is generally not a defense unless the defendant reasonably relied on an official statement of the law.

title: Mistrial — Defendant Request
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: When a defendant requests a mistrial, retrial is generally permitted unless the mistrial was caused by prosecutorial misconduct intended to provoke the request.

title: Mistrial — Manifest Necessity
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: When a mistrial is declared due to manifest necessity, retrial is not barred by double jeopardy.

title: Mitigation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An injured party may not recover damages that could have been avoided without undue risk, burden, or humiliation.

title: Mitigation of Damages
subject: Real Property
ruleText: In most modern jurisdictions, the landlord must make reasonable efforts to mitigate damages when a tenant abandons the premises before the lease term ends.

title: Modern Per Stirpes
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under modern per stirpes, shares are first divided at the nearest generation with at least one living taker, and the descendants of deceased members of that generation take by representation.

title: Modification of Child Support
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Child support may generally be modified upon a substantial change in circumstances, such as a significant change in income or the child's needs, and modifications usually operate prospectively from the time permitted by statute.

title: Modification of Custody
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A custody order may generally be modified upon a substantial change in circumstances affecting the child and a showing that modification is in the child's best interests.

title: Modification of Spousal Support
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A spousal-support award may generally be modified upon a substantial and continuing change in circumstances, unless the award is nonmodifiable by valid agreement or is a final lump-sum/property award.

title: Modification or Rescission of Contract
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract may be modified or rescinded by the original parties until the third-party beneficiary’s rights have vested.

title: Modified Comparative Negligence
subject: Torts
ruleText: Under modified comparative negligence, the plaintiff's recovery is reduced by fault but barred if the plaintiff's fault reaches or exceeds the jurisdiction's threshold, typically 50 percent or 51 percent.

title: Mootness
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A case is moot when the issues are no longer live or the parties lack a legally cognizable interest in the outcome.

title: Mootness
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A case is moot when intervening events eliminate the controversy between the parties.

title: Mootness — Exceptions
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Mootness does not apply when the issue is capable of repetition yet evading review.

title: Moral Obligation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A moral obligation alone is not sufficient consideration to support a promise.

title: Mortgage
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A mortgage is a security interest in real property that secures repayment of a debt or other obligation.

title: Mortgage — Creation
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A mortgage requires a debt and an intent to secure the debt with land.

title: Mortgage — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A mortgage is a security interest in land intended to secure repayment of a debt.

title: Mortgage — Lien Theory
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under lien theory, the mortgagee holds a lien while the mortgagor retains title.

title: Mortgage — Priority Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Mortgage priority is determined by the order of recording unless modified by statute.

title: Mortgage — Title Theory
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under title theory, the mortgagee holds legal title until the mortgage is satisfied.

title: Most Significant Relationship in Contracts
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Under the Restatement Second, contract issues are generally governed by the law of the state with the most significant relationship to the transaction and the parties, considering contacts such as the place of contracting, negotiation, performance, location of the subject matter, and the parties' domiciles.

title: Most Significant Relationship in Torts
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Under the Restatement Second, tort issues are governed by the law of the state with the most significant relationship to the occurrence and the parties, considering contacts such as the place of injury, place of conduct, domicile of the parties, and the place where the relationship is centered, along with policy factors.

title: Motion for New Trial
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may grant a new trial for errors of law, newly discovered evidence, or a verdict against the weight of the evidence.

title: Motion for New Trial
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may grant a new trial for reasons such as prejudicial error, misconduct, newly discovered evidence, or a verdict against the great weight of the evidence.

title: Motion to Dismiss for Improper Venue
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A Rule 12(b)(3) motion challenges venue as improper.

title: Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Personal Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A Rule 12(b)(2) motion challenges the court's personal jurisdiction over the defendant.

title: Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A Rule 12(b)(1) motion challenges the court's subject matter jurisdiction.

title: MPC Insanity Test
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under the MPC, the defendant is insane if, because of mental disease or defect, the defendant lacked substantial capacity either to appreciate the criminality of conduct or to conform conduct to the law.

title: MPC Mental States
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The MPC mental states are purpose, knowledge, recklessness, and negligence, ranked from most culpable to least culpable.

title: Multiple Punishments
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Double jeopardy bars multiple punishments for the same offense unless the legislature clearly intended cumulative punishment.

title: Multiple Punishments
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Double jeopardy prohibits multiple punishments for the same offense unless the legislature clearly intended cumulative punishment.

title: Multiple Sufficient Causes
subject: Torts
ruleText: When multiple acts are each sufficient to cause the injury, each act is an actual cause of the harm.

title: Multiple Sufficient Causes
subject: Torts
ruleText: When two or more independent acts each would have been sufficient to cause the harm, each act is an actual cause if it was a substantial factor in bringing about the harm.

title: Murder — Common Law Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Common law murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought.

title: Mutual Assent
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract requires mutual assent, which is ordinarily shown by a valid offer and acceptance.

title: Mutual Mistake
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract may be avoided for mutual mistake when both parties were mistaken about a basic assumption that materially affects the agreed exchange, unless the party seeking relief bore the risk of the mistake.

title: Mutual Mistake
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract is voidable for mutual mistake when both parties share a mistaken belief about a basic assumption that has a material effect on the exchange.

title: Necessary and Proper Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may use any means rationally related to the implementation of an enumerated power, so long as the means are not prohibited by the Constitution.

title: Necessary and Proper Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may enact laws that are necessary and proper to carry out its enumerated powers.

title: Necessary Parties — Foreclosure
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Junior lienholders must be joined in a foreclosure action to terminate their interests.

title: Necessity
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Necessity applies when the defendant reasonably believes criminal conduct is necessary to prevent a greater imminent harm and there is no reasonable legal alternative.

title: Necessity
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Necessity is a defense when the defendant reasonably believes criminal conduct is necessary to prevent a greater harm.

title: Necessity — Private
subject: Torts
ruleText: Private necessity is a qualified defense that allows entry onto another’s land to prevent serious harm but requires payment for actual damage caused.

title: Necessity — Public
subject: Torts
ruleText: Public necessity is a complete defense when the defendant acts to prevent a public disaster affecting the community.

title: Negligence — Elements
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence requires a duty of care, breach of that duty, causation, and damages.

title: Negligence Per Se
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence per se applies when the defendant violates a statute, the plaintiff is within the class the statute protects, and the harm is of the type the statute seeks to prevent.

title: Negligence Per Se — Definition
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence per se applies when a defendant violates a statute that establishes a standard of care.

title: Negligence Per Se — Excused Violation
subject: Torts
ruleText: A statutory violation may be excused when compliance is impossible, more dangerous, or due to an emergency not caused by the defendant.

title: Negligence Per Se — Protected Class
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence per se applies only if the plaintiff is within the class of persons the statute was designed to protect.

title: Negligence Per Se — Type of Harm
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligence per se applies only if the harm suffered is the type of harm the statute was intended to prevent.

title: Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress: Bystander
subject: Torts
ruleText: A bystander may recover in many jurisdictions when closely related to the injured victim, present at the scene, and contemporaneously aware of the injury-producing event.

title: Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress: Zone of Danger
subject: Torts
ruleText: A plaintiff may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress when placed in immediate risk of physical harm by the defendant's negligence and emotional distress results, often with required physical manifestations depending on jurisdiction.

title: Negligent Misrepresentation
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligent misrepresentation requires a misrepresentation made without reasonable care, justifiable reliance by the plaintiff, and pecuniary loss, often in a business or professional context.

title: Negligent Misrepresentation
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligent misrepresentation occurs when a defendant makes a false statement of material fact without reasonable care and the plaintiff justifiably relies and suffers damages.

title: Negligent Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Negligent nuisance occurs when the defendant’s unreasonable interference results from negligent conduct.

title: No Filing Requirement for General Partnership
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A general partnership need not file any organizational document to exist; it can arise by operation of law from the parties' conduct.

title: No Further Inquiry Rule
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If a trustee engages in self-dealing, the transaction is generally voidable by the beneficiaries without any need to prove bad faith or unfairness, unless the transaction was authorized, approved, or otherwise excused.

title: No General Duty to Rescue
subject: Torts
ruleText: There is generally no duty to rescue or aid another absent a special relationship, statutory duty, voluntary undertaking, or creation of the peril.

title: No Search — Open Fields
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: There is no reasonable expectation of privacy in open fields outside the curtilage of a home.

title: No-Contest Clause
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A no-contest clause provides that a beneficiary who contests the will forfeits benefits under the will, but many jurisdictions refuse to enforce the clause when the contestant had probable cause to challenge the will.

title: No-Fault Divorce
subject: Family Law
ruleText: No-fault divorce permits dissolution of marriage based on irretrievable breakdown, irreconcilable differences, or separation for a statutory period without proof of marital misconduct.

title: Non-Economic Damages
subject: Torts
ruleText: Non-economic damages compensate for pain and suffering, emotional distress, and loss of enjoyment of life.

title: Non-Hearsay — Prior Consistent Statement
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A prior consistent statement is not hearsay when offered to rebut a charge of recent fabrication or improper influence.

title: Non-Hearsay — Prior Inconsistent Statement (Under Oath)
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A prior inconsistent statement is not hearsay if it was made under oath at a trial, hearing, or deposition.

title: Non-Hearsay — Statement of Party Opponent
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement made by a party and offered against that party is not hearsay.

title: Non-Testimonial Statements
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Non-testimonial statements are not subject to the Confrontation Clause and are governed only by hearsay rules.

title: Nonconforming Use
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A lawful use existing before enactment of a zoning ordinance may continue as a nonconforming use, though it generally may not be expanded and may be lost by abandonment or destruction under local law.

title: Nonconforming Use
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A nonconforming use is an existing use that does not comply with new zoning regulations.

title: Nonconforming Use — Limitation
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A nonconforming use may continue but may not be expanded or resumed after abandonment.

title: Nondelegable Duties
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Duties involving special skill, personal judgment, or a contract term prohibiting delegation generally may not be delegated.

title: Nondelegable Duties
subject: Torts
ruleText: A nondelegable duty is one that the defendant cannot avoid by hiring an independent contractor, so the defendant remains liable for negligence in performing that duty.

title: Nondelegation Doctrine
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may delegate legislative authority if it supplies an intelligible principle to guide the exercise of delegated discretion.

title: Nondisclosure
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Nondisclosure constitutes misrepresentation when there is a duty to disclose material facts.

title: Nondisclosure
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Nondisclosure may be equivalent to misrepresentation when a party has a duty to disclose, such as to correct a prior statement, correct a basic mistake, or where a relationship of trust exists.

title: Nondiscriminatory Burdens on Interstate Commerce
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A nondiscriminatory law affecting interstate commerce is valid unless the burden on interstate commerce is clearly excessive in relation to the putative local benefits.

title: Nonhearsay Uses of Statements
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement is not hearsay if offered for a nontruth purpose, such as to show its effect on the listener, to provide notice, to prove a verbal act or legally operative words, to show the declarant's state of mind circumstantially, or to impeach.

title: Nonmarital Children
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A nonmarital child generally inherits from the mother automatically and from the father if paternity is established under the applicable statute, such as by adjudication, acknowledgment, or posthumous proof.

title: Nonmarital Children
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Classifications disadvantaging nonmarital children are subject to intermediate scrutiny.

title: Nonmutual Issue Preclusion Caution
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Nonmutual issue preclusion may be allowed in some circumstances, but fairness limitations apply and exam analysis must consider whether the party against whom preclusion is asserted had a full and fair opportunity to litigate.

title: Not Hearsay — Effect on Listener
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Statements offered to show their effect on the listener are not hearsay.

title: Not Hearsay — Impeachment
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Prior statements offered solely to impeach a witness are not hearsay.

title: Not Hearsay — State of Mind
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Statements offered to show the declarant’s state of mind, such as intent or motive, are not hearsay.

title: Not Hearsay — Verbal Acts
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Statements that have independent legal significance, such as words of contract formation or defamation, are not hearsay.

title: Notice — Due Process
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Due process requires notice reasonably calculated to apprise interested parties of the action.

title: Notice — Servitudes
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Notice for servitudes may be actual, record, or inquiry notice.

title: Notice and Hearing
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Procedural due process generally requires notice and an opportunity to be heard before deprivation.

title: Notice and Opportunity to Be Heard
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Due process requires notice reasonably calculated under the circumstances to apprise interested parties of the action and an opportunity to be heard.

title: Notice of Disposition
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Except in certain situations such as perishable collateral or recognized-market sales, a secured party disposing of collateral after default must send reasonable authenticated notice of disposition to the debtor, any secondary obligor, and other secured parties or lienholders who have properly notified the secured party or are revealed by filing-office searches in the specified circumstances.

title: Notice Recording Statute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under a notice statute, a bona fide purchaser without notice of a prior interest prevails.

title: Notice Statute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: In a notice jurisdiction, a subsequent bona fide purchaser prevails over a prior unrecorded interest, even if the subsequent purchaser records later.

title: Novation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A novation is a substituted contract in which all necessary parties agree that a new party will replace an original party and the original party is released.

title: Novation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A novation is a substituted contract that releases the original obligor and replaces it with a new party.

title: Novation — Discharge
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A novation discharges the original obligor when the parties substitute a new party to the contract.

title: Nuisance — Definition
subject: Torts
ruleText: Nuisance is an unreasonable interference with the use and enjoyment of land.

title: Number of Jurors
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A jury must consist of at least six jurors, but need not consist of twelve jurors.

title: Objection to Strict Foreclosure
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A secured party may not accept collateral in full or partial satisfaction if the debtor objects within 20 days after the proposal is sent, or if certain other notified parties entitled to notice timely object.

title: Objections and Offers of Proof
subject: Evidence
ruleText: To preserve error in admitting evidence, a party must timely object or move to strike and state the specific ground unless apparent from the context. To preserve error in excluding evidence, a party must inform the court of the substance of the evidence by an offer of proof unless the substance is apparent from the context.

title: Objective Theory of Assent
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Courts generally determine assent under an objective standard by looking at the parties' outward words and conduct rather than undisclosed intent.

title: Obscenity
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Material is obscene if the average person applying contemporary community standards would find it appeals to the prurient interest, it depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way as defined by law, and taken as a whole it lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value.

title: Obscenity — Miller Test
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Obscenity is defined by whether the average person would find the work appeals to prurient interest, depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way, and lacks serious value.

title: Offense-Specific Nature
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies only to the offense formally charged and same-offense equivalents under Blockburger, not to unrelated uncharged offenses.

title: Offense-Specific Nature
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is offense-specific and applies only to the charged offense.

title: Offensive Issue Preclusion
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Offensive issue preclusion may be denied if the plaintiff could have joined the prior action or if application would be unfair.

title: Offer
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An offer is a manifestation of willingness to enter into a bargain that justifies the offeree in understanding that acceptance will conclude the deal.

title: Offer — Definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An offer is a manifestation of present intent to enter into a bargain, such that acceptance will create a contract.

title: Offer definition
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An offer is a manifestation of present contractual intent to enter a bargain that is definite and certain and communicated to the offeree.

title: Offer vs Preliminary Negotiations
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A statement is not an offer if it merely reflects preliminary negotiations or a willingness to discuss terms.

title: Offered for the Truth
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement is offered for the truth when its relevance depends on the statement being true.
buzzwords: depends on the statement being true

title: Offers to Pay Medical Expenses
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of furnishing, promising to pay, or offering to pay medical, hospital, or similar expenses resulting from an injury is not admissible to prove liability for the injury.

title: Officer Authority
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Corporate officers have actual authority as granted by the bylaws, board resolutions, or their office, and may also have apparent authority to bind the corporation in matters ordinary for their position.

title: Officer Fiduciary Duties
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Corporate officers owe fiduciary duties of care and loyalty similar to those owed by directors, adapted to their role and authority within the corporation.

title: Officer Liability on Corporate Obligations
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An officer who acts for a disclosed corporation is generally not personally liable on a corporate contract if the officer acts with authority and indicates representative capacity, unless the officer personally guarantees the obligation or lacks authority.

title: Omissions
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An omission creates criminal liability only when the defendant had a legal duty to act and was physically capable of acting.

title: Omitted Spouse
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A spouse who married the testator after execution of the will and is omitted may receive a statutory intestate share or elective provision unless the omission was intentional, the spouse was provided for outside the will in lieu of testamentary transfer, or the will was made in contemplation of marriage.

title: One Person, One Vote
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Equal Protection Clause requires electoral districts to be substantially equal in population.

title: One Person, One Vote
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Legislative voting districts within a state must be substantially equal in population so that each person's vote carries roughly equal weight.

title: One Year Limit in Diversity Cases
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A diversity case generally may not be removed more than one year after commencement of the action unless an exception applies.

title: One Year Provision
subject: Contracts
ruleText: The one year provision applies only if the contract cannot possibly be fully performed within one year from the date it was made.

title: Open and Notorious
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Possession is open and notorious when it is visible and obvious such that the true owner is on notice.

title: Open and Notorious Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Possession is open and notorious if it is sufficiently visible and obvious to put a reasonable owner on notice of the adverse claim.

title: Open Fields and Curtilage
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Curtilage is treated like the home for Fourth Amendment purposes, while open fields are not protected even if privately owned.

title: Operating Agreement
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An operating agreement governs the relations among the members and managers and between them and the LLC, including management structure, allocations, distributions, transfer restrictions, and dissolution rights, subject to nonwaivable statutory limits.

title: Opinion on Credibility
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness generally may not directly opine that another witness is truthful on a particular occasion, because credibility determinations are for the jury.

title: Option Contract
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An option contract is a promise to keep an offer open for a stated period that is binding if supported by consideration or another recognized substitute.

title: Option Contracts
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An option contract is a promise to keep an offer open that is supported by consideration or made irrevocable by statute.

title: Original — Definition
subject: Evidence
ruleText: An original includes the writing or recording itself and any counterpart intended to have the same effect.

title: Other Acts Evidence — Notice Requirement
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a criminal case, the prosecution must provide reasonable notice of its intent to introduce other acts evidence.

title: Other Acts Evidence — Permitted Purposes
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Other acts evidence may be admitted to prove motive, opportunity, intent, preparation, plan, knowledge, identity, or absence of mistake.

title: Other Acts Evidence — Rule 404(b)
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of other crimes, wrongs, or acts is not admissible to prove character but may be admitted for other purposes such as motive, intent, or identity.

title: Other Buyers of Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A buyer of goods who is not a buyer in ordinary course generally takes subject to a perfected security interest but may take free of an unperfected security interest if the buyer gives value and receives delivery without knowledge of the security interest.
buzzwords: takes subject to perfected security interest

title: Other Crimes, Wrongs, or Acts
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of a crime, wrong, or other act is not admissible to prove a person's character in order to show that on a particular occasion the person acted in accordance with the character, but it may be admissible for another purpose, such as proving motive, opportunity, intent, preparation, plan, knowledge, identity, absence of mistake, or lack of accident.

title: Ouster
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Ouster occurs when one cotenant wrongfully excludes another cotenant from possession, giving rise to a possible claim for rent and often starting adverse possession if other requirements are met.

title: Ouster
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Ouster occurs when one co-tenant wrongfully excludes another from possession.

title: Outcome-Determinative Test
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A law is substantive if applying federal law instead of state law would affect the outcome of the case.

title: Output Contracts
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An output contract is enforceable when one party agrees to sell all of its output to another in good faith.

title: Overbreadth
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A law regulating speech is facially invalid if it prohibits a substantial amount of protected speech relative to its plainly legitimate sweep.

title: Overbreadth Doctrine
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A law is unconstitutionally overbroad if it prohibits a substantial amount of protected conduct.

title: Parent-Subsidiary Structure
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A parent corporation that owns a controlling interest in a subsidiary does not automatically become liable for the subsidiary's obligations merely by ownership; each corporation remains a separate legal entity absent veil-piercing, assumption of liability, or another exception.

title: Parentage (Paternity)
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Paternity may be established through marriage, voluntary acknowledgment, adjudication, or genetic testing, with a marital presumption for children born during marriage.

title: Parental Discipline
subject: Torts
ruleText: A parent or one in loco parentis may use reasonable force to discipline a child when reasonably necessary.

title: Parental Rights as Fundamental Rights
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Parents have a fundamental constitutional right to make decisions concerning the care, custody, and control of their children, subject to the state's power to protect children from harm.

title: Parenting Time and Visitation
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A noncustodial parent is generally entitled to reasonable parenting time unless visitation would seriously endanger the child's physical, mental, moral, or emotional health.

title: Parol Evidence — Permitted Uses
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Parol evidence is admissible to explain ambiguities, supplement incomplete terms, establish defenses to formation, or show a condition precedent to effectiveness.

title: Parol Evidence Exceptions
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Parol evidence may be admitted to show fraud, duress, mistake, ambiguity, lack of consideration, a condition precedent, or later modifications.

title: Parol Evidence Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: When the parties adopt a final written agreement, prior or contemporaneous agreements are not admissible to contradict it, and if the writing is complete, additional consistent terms are also barred.

title: Parol Evidence Rule — General Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: The parol evidence rule bars the admission of prior or contemporaneous extrinsic evidence that contradicts a final written agreement.

title: Part Performance
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Part performance may permit enforcement of an oral land contract when the buyer's acts unmistakably indicate the existence of the contract, commonly through possession plus payment, improvements, or both, depending on jurisdiction.

title: Part Performance Exception
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Part performance removes a land contract from the statute of frauds when the buyer takes possession, pays consideration, or makes improvements.

title: Part Performance Exception for Land
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Part performance may permit enforcement of a land contract when the buyer's conduct clearly indicates the existence of the contract, such as payment, possession, and improvements.

title: Partial Actual Eviction
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If the landlord physically excludes the tenant from part of the premises, the tenant may generally remain and withhold all rent at common law, though some modern jurisdictions apportion rent.

title: Partial Regulatory Taking
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: If a regulation does not eliminate all economic value, courts consider economic impact, interference with investment-backed expectations, and the character of the governmental action.

title: Partial Revocation by Physical Act
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: In some jurisdictions, a will may be partially revoked by a physical act performed with intent to revoke that part, but other jurisdictions allow only total revocation by act.

title: Partisan Gerrymandering
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Claims of partisan gerrymandering present nonjusticiable political questions in federal court.

title: Partition
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Any cotenant has a right to partition, either in kind by physical division or by sale and division of proceeds when partition in kind is impracticable or unfair.

title: Partition — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Any co-tenant may seek partition of the property.

title: Partition by Sale
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Partition by sale is ordered when physical division is impracticable or inequitable.

title: Partition in Kind
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Partition in kind physically divides the property and is preferred when feasible.

title: Partner Authority
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Each partner is an agent of the partnership for carrying on the partnership's business in the ordinary course, and an act of a partner for apparently carrying on the ordinary course of the partnership business binds the partnership unless the partner lacked authority and the third party knew or had notice of that lack of authority.

title: Partner Duty of Care
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A partner owes the partnership and the other partners a duty of care limited to refraining from grossly negligent or reckless conduct, intentional misconduct, or a knowing violation of law.

title: Partner Duty of Loyalty
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A partner owes the partnership and the other partners a duty of loyalty, which includes accounting for benefits derived from partnership business or use of partnership property, refraining from dealing with the partnership on behalf of an adverse interest, and refraining from competing with the partnership before dissolution.

title: Partner Liability for Partnership Obligations
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: In a general partnership, all partners are jointly and severally liable for all obligations of the partnership unless otherwise provided by law.

title: Partnership — Fiduciary Duties
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Partners owe duties of loyalty and care to the partnership and to each other, including avoiding self-dealing and usurpation of partnership opportunities.

title: Partnership — Formation
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A partnership is formed when two or more persons carry on as co-owners of a business for profit, regardless of intent to form a partnership.

title: Partnership — Partner Authority and Liability
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Each partner is an agent of the partnership and may bind it in the ordinary course of business, and partners are jointly and severally liable for partnership obligations.

title: Partnership Liability for Partner Torts
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A partnership is liable for loss or injury caused to a person by a partner's wrongful act or omission acting in the ordinary course of the partnership business or with authority of the partnership.

title: Partnership Property
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Property acquired by a partnership is property of the partnership and not of the partners individually.

title: Party Autonomy and Choice-of-Law Clauses
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Courts generally enforce a contractual choice-of-law clause if the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction or another reasonable basis for the choice exists, and if applying that law would not violate a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest whose law would otherwise apply.

title: Party Opponent — Adoptive Admission
subject: Evidence
ruleText: An adoptive admission occurs when a party manifests belief in the truth of another person’s statement.

title: Party Opponent — Agent or Employee
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement made by an agent or employee concerning a matter within the scope of the relationship during its existence is not hearsay.

title: Party Opponent — Authorized Statement
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement made by a person authorized by the party to speak on the subject is not hearsay.

title: Party Opponent — Co-Conspirator Statement
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement made by a co-conspirator during and in furtherance of the conspiracy is not hearsay.

title: Past Consideration
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Past consideration does not support a contract because it was not bargained for in exchange for the promise.

title: Past Consideration
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Past consideration is not valid consideration because it was not bargained for at the time of the promise.

title: Past Recollection Recorded — Use at Trial
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A recorded recollection may be read into evidence but may not be received as an exhibit unless offered by an adverse party.

title: Payment Intangible
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A payment intangible is a general intangible under which the account debtor's principal obligation is a monetary obligation.

title: Penal and Revenue Laws
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: As a traditional rule, one state will not directly enforce another state's penal laws and often will not enforce another sovereign's revenue laws, though statutory exceptions may exist.

title: Penalty Clauses
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A liquidated damages clause that functions as a penalty is unenforceable.

title: Per Capita at Each Generation
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under per capita at each generation, the estate is divided equally among living takers at the first generation with survivors, and the shares of deceased persons at that generation are pooled and divided equally at the next generation.

title: Peremptory Challenges — Equal Protection
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Peremptory challenges may not be used to exclude jurors based on race or gender.

title: Perfect Tender Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, a buyer may reject goods if the goods or the tender fail in any respect to conform to the contract, subject to the seller's right to cure and other limits.

title: Perfect Tender Rule (UCC)
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, the seller must deliver goods that conform exactly to the contract or the buyer may reject the goods.

title: Perfected Versus Unperfected
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected security interest generally has priority over a conflicting unperfected security interest.

title: Perfection — Control
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest in deposit accounts, investment property, and electronic chattel paper may be perfected by control.

title: Perfection — Debtor Name
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A financing statement must provide the debtor’s correct legal name.

title: Perfection — Filing
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Perfection by filing requires a financing statement that provides the debtor’s name, the secured party’s name, and indicates the collateral.

title: Perfection — General Rule
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest is perfected when it has attached and the secured party has taken all required steps.

title: Perfection — Possession
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A secured party may perfect by possession of tangible collateral.

title: Perfection by Possession
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest in tangible negotiable documents, goods, instruments, money, or tangible chattel paper may generally be perfected by the secured party's possession of the collateral.

title: Perfection of Deposit Accounts by Control
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest in a deposit account as original collateral may be perfected only by control.

title: Periodic Tenancy
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A periodic tenancy continues for successive periods until properly terminated by notice from either the landlord or the tenant.

title: Periodic Tenancy
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A periodic tenancy continues for successive periods until terminated by proper notice.

title: Permissible Scope of Premarital Agreements
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A premarital agreement may generally address property rights, disposition of property on divorce or death, spousal support, and other financial matters, but provisions adversely affecting child support or child custody are not binding on the court.

title: Permissive Counterclaim
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A counterclaim is permissive if it does not arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the opposing party's claim.
buzzwords: does not arise out of same transaction or occurrence

title: Permissive Intervention
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Permissive intervention may be allowed when the applicant's claim or defense and the main action share a common question of law or fact.
buzzwords: common question of law or fact

title: Permissive Joinder
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Parties may be permissively joined if their claims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence and share at least one common question of law or fact.
buzzwords: common question of law or fact

title: Permissive Joinder of Defendants
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Defendants may be joined if the claims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence and share a common question of law or fact.

title: Permissive Joinder of Plaintiffs
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Plaintiffs may join in one action if their claims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence and share a common question of law or fact.
buzzwords: common question of law or fact

title: Permissive Waste
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Permissive waste is neglect by the tenant in possession, such as failure to maintain the property, pay taxes, or pay interest on mortgages to the extent required.

title: Personal Injury Damages
subject: Torts
ruleText: A prevailing plaintiff may recover past and future medical expenses, lost earnings or earning capacity, pain and suffering, and property damage caused by the tort.

title: Personal Jurisdiction
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: A court may exercise personal jurisdiction over a defendant when the forum's law authorizes jurisdiction and the exercise of jurisdiction complies with due process, including sufficient contacts with the forum and fairness.

title: Personal Jurisdiction — General Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court has personal jurisdiction over a defendant if authorized by statute and consistent with due process.

title: Personal Knowledge
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness may testify only if evidence is introduced sufficient to support a finding that the witness has personal knowledge of the matter.

title: Photographic Identifications
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel does not apply to photographic identifications.

title: Physical Disabilities
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person with a physical disability is held to the standard of a reasonably careful person with the same disability.
buzzwords: reasonably careful person with the same disability

title: Physical Taking
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A permanent physical occupation authorized by the government is a taking requiring just compensation, no matter how small the occupation.

title: Physician and Psychotherapist Privileges
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Under federal common law, there is no general physician-patient privilege, but there is a psychotherapist-patient privilege protecting confidential communications for diagnosis or treatment of mental or emotional conditions. In diversity cases, state privilege law may recognize broader privileges.

title: Physician-Patient Privilege
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The physician-patient privilege protects confidential communications made for purposes of medical diagnosis or treatment.

title: Piercing the Corporate Veil
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A court may pierce the corporate veil and hold shareholders personally liable when the corporation is used to perpetrate fraud, is inadequately capitalized, or is treated as the shareholders' alter ego, and failing to pierce would sanction unfairness or injustice.

title: Pinkerton Liability
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A conspirator is liable for crimes of co-conspirators committed in furtherance of the conspiracy, within its scope, and reasonably foreseeable.

title: Place of Contracting
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Under the traditional approach, issues of contract validity and interpretation are generally governed by the law of the place where the contract was made, while performance issues may be governed by the place of performance.

title: Place of the Wrong in Torts
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Under the traditional vested-rights approach, tort issues are generally governed by the law of the place of the wrong, usually the place where the last event necessary to create liability occurred, often the place of injury.

title: Plain Error
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A court may take notice of a plain error affecting a substantial right even if the claim of error was not properly preserved.

title: Plain View
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may seize evidence in plain view if they are lawfully present, have lawful access, and its incriminating character is immediately apparent.

title: Plain View Doctrine
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may seize evidence without a warrant if they are lawfully present, the item is in plain view, and its incriminating nature is immediately apparent.

title: Plaintiffs in Products Liability
subject: Torts
ruleText: Any foreseeable user, consumer, or bystander injured by a defective product may recover.

title: Pleading Standard — Rule 8
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Under Rule 8, a pleading must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim that is plausible on its face.

title: Pleadings — General Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Pleadings are formal documents filed by the parties stating their claims and defenses.

title: Pleas, Plea Discussions, and Related Statements
subject: Evidence
topic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: In a civil or criminal case, evidence of a withdrawn guilty plea, a nolo contendere plea, statements made during a proceeding on either plea, or statements made during plea discussions with the prosecuting authority that did not result in a guilty plea or resulted in a later-withdrawn guilty plea is generally inadmissible against the defendant.

title: PMSI in Consumer Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A purchase-money security interest in consumer goods may receive PMSI priority and is often automatically perfected upon attachment, though certificate-of-title rules and buyer rules can affect the outcome.

title: PMSI in Equipment
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected purchase-money security interest in equipment has priority over a conflicting security interest in the same equipment if the PMSI is perfected when the debtor receives possession of the equipment or within 20 days thereafter.

title: PMSI in Fixtures
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected purchase-money security interest in fixtures generally has priority over a conflicting interest of an encumbrancer or owner of the related real property if the debtor has an interest of record in or possession of the real property and the security interest is perfected by a fixture filing before the goods become fixtures or within 20 days thereafter.
buzzwords: before goods become fixtures or within 20 days

title: PMSI in Inventory
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected purchase-money security interest in inventory has priority over a conflicting security interest in the same inventory and its identifiable cash proceeds if the PMSI is perfected when the debtor receives possession of the inventory and the PMSI secured party sends an authenticated notice to the holder of the conflicting security interest before the debtor receives possession, stating that the PMSI secured party expects to acquire a PMSI in the debtor's inventory.

title: Police-Initiated Interrogation After Attachment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: After the right to counsel attaches, police may not deliberately elicit statements from the defendant without counsel present.

title: Policy Exclusion — Liability Insurance
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence that a person was insured against liability is not admissible to prove negligence or wrongful conduct.

title: Policy Exclusion — Offers to Pay Medical Expenses
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of offers to pay medical expenses is not admissible to prove liability for an injury.

title: Policy Exclusion — Pleas and Plea Discussions
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of withdrawn guilty pleas, nolo contendere pleas, and plea discussions is not admissible against the defendant.

title: Policy Exclusion — Settlement Offers
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of settlement offers or negotiations is not admissible to prove liability or the amount of a claim.

title: Policy Exclusion — Subsequent Remedial Measures
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of subsequent remedial measures is not admissible to prove negligence, culpable conduct, or product defect.

title: Political Question Doctrine
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A federal court will not decide an issue committed by the Constitution to another branch or lacking judicially manageable standards for resolution.

title: Political Question Doctrine
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Courts will not decide political questions that are constitutionally committed to another branch of government.

title: Possession Offenses
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Possession may be actual or constructive and requires knowledge plus control for a sufficient time to terminate possession.

title: Possession Rights of Cotenants
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Each cotenant has an equal right to possess and enjoy the whole property, regardless of the size of that cotenant's fractional share, subject to equal rights of the others.

title: Possibility of Reverter
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A possibility of reverter is the future interest retained by a grantor following a fee simple determinable.

title: Post-Charge Lineups
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
