ruleText: After the Sixth Amendment right attaches, the defendant has a right to counsel at post-charge physical lineups and showups.

title: Pour-Over Gift to Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under the Uniform Testamentary Additions to Trusts Act and similar statutes, a will may validly pour property over to an inter vivos trust identified in the will, even if the trust is amendable or revocable and even if unfunded during life.

title: Power of Appointment
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A power of appointment is an authority enabling the donee to designate who will receive certain property interests, subject to the terms of the instrument creating the power.

title: Preemption
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Federal law preempts conflicting state law under the Supremacy Clause by express preemption, field preemption, or conflict preemption where compliance with both laws is impossible or state law obstructs federal objectives.

title: Preemption — Supremacy Clause
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Federal law preempts state law when Congress expressly preempts, occupies the field, or when state law conflicts with federal law.

title: Preemptive Rights
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Preemptive rights give existing shareholders the right to maintain their proportional ownership by purchasing a proportionate share of certain new issuances, but such rights generally exist only if granted by the articles or governing statute.

title: Preexisting Duty — Common Law Modification
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under common law, a contract modification requires new consideration to be enforceable.

title: Preexisting Duty — UCC Modification
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Under the UCC, a contract modification is enforceable without new consideration if made in good faith.

title: Preexisting Duty Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A promise to perform a preexisting legal duty is not valid consideration unless an exception applies.

title: Preexisting Duty Rule at Common Law
subject: Contracts
ruleText: At common law, a promise to perform a duty already owed does not constitute consideration for a new promise.

title: Preliminary Injunction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A preliminary injunction is provisional equitable relief entered before final judgment to preserve the status quo or prevent irreparable harm while the case is pending.

title: Preliminary Questions of Admissibility
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The judge decides preliminary questions about whether a witness is qualified, a privilege exists, or evidence is admissible, and in doing so is not bound by evidence rules except those on privilege.
buzzwords: not bound by rules except privilege

title: Premarital and Separation Agreements
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Premarital and separation agreements are enforceable if voluntary, supported by full disclosure, and not unconscionable; courts review child-related provisions.

title: Premeditation and Deliberation
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: First-degree murder based on premeditation and deliberation requires a willful intent to kill formed after reflection, even if the reflection is brief.

title: Prescriptive Easement
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A prescriptive easement arises from use that is open and notorious, adverse, continuous, and for the statutory period.

title: Present Covenants
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The present covenants are seisin, right to convey, and against encumbrances, and they are breached, if at all, at the time of conveyance.

title: Present Estates — Fee Simple Absolute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A fee simple absolute is an estate of indefinite duration with no conditions or limitations.

title: Present Estates — Fee Simple Determinable
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A fee simple determinable automatically terminates upon the occurrence of a stated event.

title: Present Estates — Fee Simple Subject to Condition Subsequent
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A fee simple subject to condition subsequent does not automatically terminate but may be terminated by the grantor upon breach of a condition.
buzzwords: fee simple subject to condition subsequent

title: Present Estates — Fee Simple Subject to Executory Limitation
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A fee simple subject to executory limitation automatically transfers to a third party upon the occurrence of a stated event.
buzzwords: fee simple subject to executory limitation

title: Present Sense Impression
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A present sense impression is a statement describing or explaining an event or condition made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it.

title: Present Sense Impression
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A statement describing or explaining an event or condition, made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it, is admissible as a present sense impression.

title: Presidential Immunity
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The President has absolute immunity from civil damages liability for official acts, but no absolute immunity for unofficial conduct or from criminal process rules otherwise applicable.

title: Presidential Power — Youngstown Framework
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Presidential power is strongest when acting with congressional authorization, weaker when Congress is silent, and weakest when acting against congressional will.

title: Presumption of Undue Influence
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A presumption of undue influence may arise when a beneficiary had a confidential relationship with the testator, actively participated in procuring the will, and unduly benefited from it.

title: Pretermitted Child
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A pretermitted child omitted from a will may take an intestate share.

title: Pretermitted Child
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A pretermitted child, usually a child born or adopted after execution of the will and unintentionally omitted, may receive an intestate share or the portion provided by statute unless the omission was intentional or the testator otherwise provided for the child outside the will.

title: Pretrial Conferences
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Courts may hold pretrial conferences to formulate issues, eliminate frivolous claims, and facilitate settlement.

title: Pretrial Identification — General Rule
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Pretrial identification procedures must comply with the Sixth Amendment right to counsel and the Due Process Clause.

title: Pretrial Motions — General Rule
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Pretrial motions are used to challenge pleadings, jurisdiction, venue, or the legal sufficiency of claims before trial.

title: Pretrial Orders
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A pretrial order controls the course of the action unless modified by the court.
buzzwords: controls the course of the action

title: Principal
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A principal is the person who personally commits the criminal act or uses an innocent instrumentality to do so.

title: Principal — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A principal is the person who actually commits the criminal act.

title: Principal Liability on Authorized Contracts
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
ruleText: A principal is liable on a contract made by an agent acting with actual authority, apparent authority, inherent agency power where recognized, or ratified authority.

title: Principal Place of Business
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A corporation’s principal place of business is the state where its high-level officers direct, control, and coordinate activities.

title: Principal Place of Business
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A corporation's principal place of business is usually its nerve center, the place where high level officers direct, control, and coordinate corporate activities.

title: Principal's Direct Liability
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A principal may be directly liable for its own negligence in selecting, supervising, or retaining an agent, or for authorizing or ratifying wrongful conduct.

title: Principal's Duties to Agent
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A principal generally owes the agent duties of compensation as agreed, reimbursement and indemnification for authorized expenses and liabilities, and cooperation in the agency relationship.

title: Prior Consistent Statement
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness's prior consistent statement is defined as not hearsay if the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination about the statement and the statement is offered to rebut an express or implied charge that the witness recently fabricated it or acted from a recent improper influence or motive, or to rehabilitate credibility when attacked on another ground.

title: Prior Identification
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A declarant-witness's prior statement identifying a person after perceiving that person is defined as not hearsay if the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination about the statement.

title: Prior Inconsistent Statement
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness's prior inconsistent statement is defined as not hearsay if the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination about the statement, and the statement was given under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, other proceeding, or deposition.

title: Prior Inconsistent Statement — Extrinsic Evidence
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Extrinsic evidence of a prior inconsistent statement is admissible only if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement.

title: Prior Inconsistent Statement for Impeachment
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness may be impeached by a prior inconsistent statement. If the witness is not the opposing party, extrinsic evidence of the statement is admissible only if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny it and the opposing party is given an opportunity to examine the witness about it, unless justice so requires.

title: Prior Restraint
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Prior restraints on speech are presumptively unconstitutional.

title: Prior Restraints
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Prior restraints on speech are heavily disfavored and generally unconstitutional unless accompanied by important procedural safeguards and supported by an extraordinary justification.

title: Priority — Buyers in Ordinary Course
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A buyer in ordinary course of business takes free of a security interest created by the seller.

title: Priority — General Rule
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Priority among conflicting security interests is determined by the first to file or perfect.

title: Priority — Lien Creditors
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected security interest has priority over a lien creditor.

title: Priority — PMSI in Goods
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A purchase-money security interest in goods has priority if perfected within the statutory time.

title: Priority — Unperfected Interests
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Between unperfected security interests, priority goes to the first to attach.

title: Priority in Chattel Paper
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A purchaser of chattel paper who gives new value and takes possession of tangible chattel paper or obtains control of electronic chattel paper in good faith, in the ordinary course of the purchaser's business, and without knowledge that the purchase violates another's rights may obtain priority over a security interest in the chattel paper claimed merely as proceeds, and in some circumstances over competing chattel-paper interests if the records indicate the purchaser's rights.

title: Priority in Deposit Accounts
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: As among conflicting security interests in the same deposit account perfected by control, priority generally goes first to the secured party having control by becoming the bank's customer, then to the depositary bank itself, and then to secured parties with control agreements, with time of obtaining control often relevant among equal classes.

title: Priority in Instruments
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A purchaser of an instrument who gives value and takes possession in good faith and without knowledge that the purchase violates another's rights may have priority over a perfected security interest perfected only by filing.

title: Priority in Investment Property
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A secured party who has control of investment property generally has priority over a secured party who does not have control.

title: Priority in Proceeds
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A perfected security interest in proceeds generally has the same date of priority as the perfected security interest in the original collateral so long as perfection in the proceeds continues.
buzzwords: same date of priority as original collateral

title: Priority of Mortgages
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Absent an applicable recording statute or exception, mortgage priority is generally determined by the order in which the interests are created or recorded.

title: Private Figures
subject: Torts
ruleText: A private figure generally must prove at least negligence regarding falsity, and actual malice is required for presumed or punitive damages involving matters of public concern.

title: Private Necessity
subject: Torts
ruleText: Private necessity is a qualified privilege to invade another's property to protect a person or property from serious harm, but the defendant remains liable for actual damage caused.

title: Private Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Private nuisance is a substantial and unreasonable interference with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of land.

title: Private Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Private nuisance occurs when a defendant substantially and unreasonably interferes with a private individual’s use or enjoyment of land.

title: Privilege
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Privileged matter is not discoverable even if it is relevant and proportional.

title: Privilege Law in Federal Court
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In a federal question case, privileges are governed by federal common law unless the Constitution, a federal statute, or rules prescribed by the Supreme Court provide otherwise. In a civil case where state law supplies the rule of decision, state privilege law applies.
buzzwords: state law supplies the rule of decision

title: Privileges and Immunities Clause — Article IV
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: States may not discriminate against citizens of other states with respect to fundamental rights or important economic activities.

title: Privileges in Defamation
subject: Torts
ruleText: Absolute privileges protect statements in judicial, legislative, and certain executive proceedings, while qualified privileges protect statements made in good faith on proper occasions to proper recipients.

title: Privity — Tacking
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Privity exists when possession is transferred voluntarily between possessors.

title: Privity After Assignment
subject: Real Property
ruleText: An assignee in possession is in privity of estate with the landlord and is liable on lease covenants that run with the land, while the original tenant generally remains liable on the lease by privity of contract unless released.

title: Privity of Contract
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Privity of contract exists between parties to the lease agreement.

title: Privity of Estate
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Privity of estate exists between parties who hold successive interests in the same property.

title: Probable Cause — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Probable cause exists when there is a fair probability that evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place.

title: Probable Cause — Totality of the Circumstances
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Probable cause is determined under the totality of the circumstances.

title: Probable Cause for Arrest
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Probable cause to arrest exists when facts known to police would warrant a prudent person in believing the suspect committed or is committing a crime.
buzzwords: committed or is committing a crime

title: Probable Cause for Search
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Probable cause to search exists when there is a fair probability that evidence or contraband will be found in the place to be searched.

title: Probate of a Lost or Destroyed Will
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A lost or destroyed will may be admitted to probate if the proponent proves due execution, the contents of the will, and that the will was not revoked, often by clear and convincing evidence.

title: Procedural Due Process
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Procedural due process applies when the government deprives a person of life, liberty, or property, and requires notice and an opportunity to be heard at a meaningful time and in a meaningful manner.

title: Procedural Due Process — Requirement
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Procedural due process requires fair procedures when the government deprives a person of life, liberty, or property.

title: Procedural Due Process — What Process Is Due
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The amount of process due depends on the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous deprivation, and the government’s interest.

title: Proceeds
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Proceeds include whatever is acquired upon the sale, lease, license, exchange, or other disposition of collateral; whatever is collected on or distributed on account of collateral; rights arising out of collateral; claims arising out of loss or damage to collateral; and insurance payable by reason of loss or damage to the collateral.

title: Proceeds
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest attaches to identifiable proceeds of collateral.

title: Proceeds, Products, and Supporting Obligations
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest generally attaches automatically to identifiable proceeds of collateral and also extends to supporting obligations related to the collateral, while products may be covered depending on the collateral classification and agreement terms.

title: Product Misuse
subject: Torts
ruleText: Unforeseeable misuse of a product may defeat or reduce products liability, while foreseeable misuse may still support liability.

title: Products Causation
subject: Torts
ruleText: The plaintiff must prove the product defect actually and proximately caused the injury.

title: Products Liability — General Rule
subject: Torts
ruleText: Products liability imposes liability on manufacturers and sellers for injuries caused by defective products placed into the stream of commerce.

title: Products Liability — Theories of Recovery
subject: Torts
ruleText: Products liability claims may be based on strict liability, negligence, or breach of warranty.

title: Professional Degree or License
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A professional degree or license itself is generally not marital property, but the supporting spouse's contributions may be considered in equitable distribution or support awards.

title: Professionals
subject: Torts
ruleText: Professionals must exercise the knowledge, skill, and care of a reasonably prudent member of the profession in similar circumstances.

title: Profit
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A profit is a nonpossessory interest allowing the holder to enter another's land and remove natural resources such as timber, minerals, or game.

title: Profit Sharing Presumption
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A person who receives a share of partnership profits is presumed to be a partner unless the profits were received in payment of a debt, wages, rent, interest on a loan, or for certain other exceptions.

title: Profits and Losses
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Absent agreement otherwise, partners share profits equally and share losses in the same proportion as profits.

title: Promissory Estoppel
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A promise is enforceable under promissory estoppel when the promisor should reasonably expect reliance, the promise induces actual reliance, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement.

title: Promissory Estoppel — Elements
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Promissory estoppel applies when a promisor should reasonably expect reliance, the promisee relies to their detriment, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement of the promise.

title: Promissory Estoppel — Remedy
subject: Contracts
ruleText: The remedy under promissory estoppel is limited to the amount necessary to prevent injustice.

title: Promoter Fiduciary Duties
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A promoter owes fiduciary duties of good faith and fair dealing to the corporation and its investors, including the duty to disclose material facts and avoid secret profits from transactions involving the corporation.

title: Promoter Liability on Preincorporation Contracts
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A promoter who enters a contract on behalf of a not-yet-formed corporation is personally liable on the contract unless the contract clearly provides otherwise or there is a later novation releasing the promoter.

title: Proof of Foreign Law
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Under modern practice, the content of another state's or nation's law is often treated as a question for the court, which may consider materials beyond the pleadings and ordinary evidence rules to determine the foreign law.

title: Property and Territorial Powers
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress has plenary power over federal property and territories and may regulate and dispose of them.

title: Property Division — Equitable Distribution vs Community Property
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Equitable distribution divides marital property fairly but not necessarily equally, while community property states divide marital property equally.

title: Property Division — Marital vs Separate Property
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Marital property includes assets acquired during marriage, while separate property includes premarital assets, gifts, and inheritances unless transmuted.

title: Property Interests
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A property interest exists when a person has a legitimate claim of entitlement created by law, contract, or established practice, not merely a unilateral expectation.

title: Prosecutorial Misconduct
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Prosecutorial misconduct violates due process when it renders the trial fundamentally unfair.

title: Protected Interests — Liberty
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Liberty interests include freedom from bodily restraint and significant restrictions on personal freedom.

title: Protected Interests — Property
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Property interests include legitimate claims of entitlement created by existing rules or understandings.

title: Protective Sweep of Vehicle During Terry Stop
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may search the passenger compartment for weapons during a lawful stop if they reasonably believe the suspect is dangerous and may gain immediate control of weapons.

title: Proximate Cause
subject: Torts
ruleText: Proximate cause limits liability to those harms that are within the scope of the foreseeable risk created by the defendant's conduct.

title: Proxy Voting
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A proxy is an authorization by a shareholder permitting another person to vote the shareholder's shares. Proxies are generally freely revocable unless coupled with an interest and stated to be irrevocable.

title: Prudent Investor Rule
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under the prudent investor rule, a trustee must invest and manage trust assets as a prudent investor would, by considering risk and return objectives reasonably suited to the trust, diversifying investments unless special circumstances justify otherwise, and evaluating the portfolio as a whole.

title: Psychotherapist-Patient Privilege
subject: Evidence
ruleText: The psychotherapist-patient privilege protects confidential communications made for purposes of mental health treatment.

title: Public Disclosure of Private Facts
subject: Torts
ruleText: This tort requires public disclosure of private information about the plaintiff that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person and is not of legitimate public concern.

title: Public Disclosure of Private Facts
subject: Torts
ruleText: Public disclosure of private facts occurs when a defendant publicly discloses private information that would be highly offensive and is not of legitimate public concern.

title: Public Forum — Designated
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A designated public forum is government property opened for expressive activity and is subject to the same standards as a traditional public forum.

title: Public Forum — Limited or Nonpublic
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: In limited or nonpublic forums, regulations must be reasonable and viewpoint neutral.

title: Public Forum — Traditional
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Traditional public forums include streets and parks, where content-based restrictions are subject to strict scrutiny.

title: Public Function and Entanglement
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Private conduct may be treated as state action if the private actor performs a traditionally exclusive public function or there is significant state entanglement, coercion, or encouragement.

title: Public Necessity
subject: Torts
ruleText: Public necessity is an absolute privilege to interfere with property when reasonably necessary to avert an imminent public disaster.

title: Public Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public.
buzzwords: right common to the general public

title: Public Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public.
buzzwords: right common to the general public

title: Public Officials and Public Figures
subject: Torts
ruleText: A public official or public figure must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant acted with actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for truth.

title: Public Policy
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A contract is unenforceable if it violates public policy.

title: Public Policy Exception
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: A forum may refuse to apply otherwise applicable foreign law when doing so would violate a strong public policy of the forum, but courts use this exception sparingly.

title: Public Records
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Public records setting forth the activities of an office or matters observed pursuant to a legal duty are admissible.

title: Public Records
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A record or statement of a public office is admissible if it sets out the office's activities, a matter observed while under a legal duty to report, or in a civil case or against the government in a criminal case factual findings from a legally authorized investigation, and the opponent does not show lack of trustworthiness.

title: Public Records — Law Enforcement Limitation
subject: Evidence
ruleText: In criminal cases, matters observed by law enforcement personnel are not admissible against the defendant.

title: Public Records and Summaries
subject: Evidence
topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
ruleText: The contents of an official record may be proved by a copy certified as correct or testified to as correct by a witness who has compared it with the original. The contents of voluminous writings, recordings, or photographs that cannot be conveniently examined in court may be presented in the form of a summary, chart, or calculation if the originals or duplicates are made available for examination or copying.

title: Public Safety Exception
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may ask questions reasonably prompted by immediate concern for public safety without first giving Miranda warnings.

title: Public Trial
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment generally guarantees a public criminal trial, though limited closures may be allowed if justified by overriding interests and narrowly tailored.

title: Public Use
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Public use is broadly construed to include a public purpose, such as economic development or elimination of blight, so long as the taking is rationally related to that purpose.

title: Public Use and Compensation
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The government may take private property for public use upon payment of just compensation, generally measured by fair market value.

title: Public Use Requirement
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Public use includes public purpose such as economic development.

title: Punitive Damages
subject: Torts
ruleText: Punitive damages may be awarded when the defendant’s conduct is willful, wanton, or reckless.

title: Purchase Money Mortgage
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A purchase money mortgage given to enable the buyer to acquire the property generally has priority over earlier liens arising through the buyer, at least as to the land purchased.

title: Purchase Money Mortgage — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A purchase money mortgage has priority over prior liens against the mortgagor.

title: Purchase Money Mortgage — Vendor vs Lender
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A vendor purchase money mortgage has priority over a third-party lender purchase money mortgage.

title: Purchase-Money Security Interest Defined
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest is purchase-money to the extent that the goods are purchase-money collateral with respect to that security interest, meaning the obligation is incurred as all or part of the price of the collateral or for value given to enable the debtor to acquire rights in or use of the collateral, if the value is in fact so used.

title: Pure Comparative Negligence
subject: Torts
ruleText: Under pure comparative negligence, the plaintiff's recovery is reduced by the percentage of the plaintiff's fault.
buzzwords: reduced by the percentage of fault

title: Pure Economic Loss
subject: Torts
ruleText: A plaintiff generally may not recover in ordinary negligence for purely economic loss unaccompanied by personal injury or property damage, absent a special relationship or recognized exception.

title: Purpose of Recording Acts
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Recording acts protect bona fide purchasers and creditors by establishing priority rules among competing claimants to the same real property interest.

title: Purposeful Availment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Minimum contacts require that the defendant purposefully avail themselves of the privilege of conducting activities in the forum.

title: Purposeful Availment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Purposeful availment means the defendant deliberately engaged with the forum, invoking the benefits and protections of its laws, rather than being haled there by random, fortuitous, or unilateral acts.

title: Putative Spouse Doctrine
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A putative spouse is entitled to marital protections when a party has a good faith belief in the validity of the marriage.

title: Putative Spouse Doctrine
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A putative spouse is a person who participated in a marriage ceremony in good faith belief that the marriage was valid, and some jurisdictions grant such a person certain marital rights despite the marriage's invalidity.

title: Quitclaim Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has without warranties of title.

title: Quitclaim Deed
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has, if any, without any covenants of title.

title: Race Recording Statute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under a race statute, the first party to record prevails regardless of notice.

title: Race Statute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: In a race jurisdiction, the first grantee to record prevails, regardless of notice.

title: Race-Notice Recording Statute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under a race-notice statute, a bona fide purchaser who records first prevails.

title: Race-Notice Statute
subject: Real Property
ruleText: In a race-notice jurisdiction, a subsequent bona fide purchaser prevails only if the purchaser takes without notice and records first.

title: Racial Gerrymandering
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: If race was the predominant factor in drawing district lines, the districting plan is subject to strict scrutiny.

title: Rape
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Rape is unlawful sexual intercourse accomplished by force or without consent.

title: Rape Shield Rule
subject: Evidence
subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
ruleText: In civil and criminal proceedings involving alleged sexual misconduct, evidence offered to prove that a victim engaged in other sexual behavior or evidence offered to prove a victim's sexual predisposition is generally inadmissible, subject to limited exceptions.

title: Ratification
subject: Business Associations
subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
ruleText: A principal ratifies an unauthorized act by affirming it after the fact or by accepting its benefits with knowledge of the material facts. Ratification retroactively creates the effect of authority if the principal had capacity and the act could have been authorized at the time.

title: Rational Basis — Default Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Rational basis review applies to all classifications not subject to strict or intermediate scrutiny.

title: Rational Basis Review
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A law is upheld under rational basis review if it is rationally related to a legitimate governmental interest.

title: Rational Basis Review — Standard
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Under rational basis review, a law is upheld if it is rationally related to a legitimate government interest.

title: Real Covenant — Elements
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A real covenant requires writing, intent, touch and concern, and privity.

title: Real Covenant Benefit
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The benefit of a real covenant runs if there is a writing, intent, the covenant touches and concerns the land, and vertical privity in most jurisdictions.

title: Real Covenant Burden
subject: Real Property
ruleText: For the burden of a real covenant to run at law, there must be a writing, intent, the covenant must touch and concern the land, vertical privity, and usually horizontal privity, plus notice for a successor.

title: Real Covenants — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A real covenant is a promise concerning land that runs with the land at law.

title: Real Estate Contracts — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A contract for the sale of land requires mutual assent, consideration, and compliance with the statute of frauds.
buzzwords: contract for the sale of land

title: Reasonable Description of Collateral
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A description of collateral is sufficient if it reasonably identifies what is described, including by specific listing, category, type of collateral defined in the UCC, quantity, computational formula, or any other method that objectively determines the collateral. But a supergeneric description such as 'all the debtor's assets' or 'all personal property' is generally insufficient in a security agreement.

title: Reasonable Expectation of Privacy
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A reasonable expectation of privacy exists when a person has a subjective expectation of privacy that society recognizes as reasonable.

title: Reasonably Prudent Person
subject: Torts
ruleText: The ordinary standard of care is that of a reasonably prudent person acting under the same or similar circumstances.

title: Recapture of Chattels
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person may use reasonable force to recapture chattels if in fresh pursuit of one who has wrongfully taken them.

title: Recapture of Chattels
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person wrongfully dispossessed of chattels may use reasonable force in fresh pursuit after a timely demand for return, but not force if the property was lawfully acquired.

title: Receiving Stolen Property
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Receiving stolen property occurs when a defendant knowingly receives stolen property with intent to permanently deprive the owner.

title: Receiving Stolen Property
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Receiving stolen property requires receiving possession and control of property known to be stolen by another, with intent to permanently deprive the owner.

title: Reciprocal Negative Servitude
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under a common development scheme, reciprocal negative servitudes may arise so that lot owners can enforce uniform restrictions against other lots if purchasers had notice of the scheme.

title: Recognition of Foreign Country Judgments
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Foreign country judgments are recognized not by full faith and credit but generally by comity, statute, or uniform act, so long as the foreign tribunal had jurisdiction, used fair procedures, and the judgment is compatible with public policy and due process standards.

title: Recorded Recollection
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A record may be read into evidence as a recorded recollection if the witness once knew about the matter but now cannot recall well enough to testify fully and accurately, the record was made or adopted when the matter was fresh in memory, and the record accurately reflects the witness's knowledge. The exhibit itself is received only if offered by the adverse party.

title: Recorded Recollection
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A recorded recollection is admissible when a witness once had knowledge, now lacks sufficient recollection, and made or adopted a record when the matter was fresh.

title: Recorded Recollection as Hearsay Exception
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A record that meets the recorded recollection requirements is admissible to be read into evidence despite hearsay concerns when the witness cannot remember well enough to testify fully and accurately.

title: Recording Acts — Purpose
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Recording acts protect bona fide purchasers from prior unrecorded interests.

title: Recreational Use Protection
subject: Torts
ruleText: Recreational use statutes often limit landowner liability when land is opened for public recreational use without charge, except for willful or malicious conduct.

title: Refreshing Recollection
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness's recollection may be refreshed with almost any item, whether or not admissible, so long as the witness then testifies from present recollection. The adverse party is entitled to inspect the item, cross-examine the witness about it, and introduce relevant portions into evidence.

title: Registration for Enforcement
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A support order may be registered in another state for enforcement, and once confirmed, it is enforceable in the same manner as a local order, subject to limited defenses to registration.

title: Regulatory Taking
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A regulatory taking occurs when a regulation deprives property of all economically viable use.

title: Rehabilitation
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness may be rehabilitated after impeachment by evidence explaining or countering the impeachment.

title: Rehabilitation of Witnesses
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness may be rehabilitated after an attack on credibility, including by prior consistent statements when allowed, or by evidence of truthful character only after the witness's character for truthfulness has been attacked.

title: Reinitiation by Suspect
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: After invoking Miranda rights, interrogation may resume only if the suspect initiates further communication.

title: Relation Back
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An amended pleading relates back when it arises out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence set out in the original pleading.

title: Relation Back — New Parties
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An amendment adding a new party relates back if the party received notice and knew or should have known they would have been named but for a mistake.

title: Relation Back of Amendments
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An amendment relates back to the date of the original pleading if it arises out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence.

title: Relation Back When Changing Parties
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: An amendment changing a party may relate back when it arises from the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence and the new party received timely notice and knew or should have known that but for a mistake it would have been named originally.

title: Relevance — Admissibility
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Relevant evidence is admissible unless excluded by the Constitution, a statute, the Federal Rules of Evidence, or other rules.

title: Relevance — Definition
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence is relevant if it has any tendency to make a fact of consequence more or less probable than it would be without the evidence.

title: Reliability Factors
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Identification reliability is evaluated based on the witness’s opportunity to view, degree of attention, accuracy of prior description, level of certainty, and time between crime and identification.

title: Reliance Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Reliance damages reimburse expenses incurred in reliance on the contract by putting the injured party in the position occupied before the contract was made.

title: Reliance Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Reliance damages reimburse the non-breaching party for expenses incurred in reliance on the contract.

title: Relief from Judgment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may relieve a party from a final judgment or order for reasons such as mistake, excusable neglect, newly discovered evidence, fraud, or other grounds recognized by rule.

title: Relief from Judgment — Rule 60
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may grant relief from a final judgment for mistake, newly discovered evidence, fraud, or void judgments.

title: Religion Clauses — General Rule
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The First Amendment prohibits the government from establishing religion or prohibiting the free exercise of religion.

title: Religion in Public Schools
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Government-sponsored prayer, devotional Bible reading, and religious exercises in public schools are generally unconstitutional, while neutral accommodation and private student religious expression are treated differently.

title: Religious Beliefs
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of a witness's religious beliefs or opinions is not admissible to attack or support the witness's credibility.

title: Religious Exemptions
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The government is not required to grant religious exemptions from neutral laws of general applicability.

title: Religious Speech in Public Schools
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Government-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause.

title: Relocation of Custodial Parent
subject: Family Law
ruleText: In relocation disputes, courts balance the relocating parent's reasons, the effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, the feasibility of preserving that relationship, and the child's best interests under the governing statute or case law.

title: Remainder
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A remainder is a future interest created in a transferee that becomes possessory upon the natural expiration of the prior estate and does not cut it short.

title: Remand for Lack of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A removed case must be remanded if the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction.

title: Remand for Procedural Defect
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Procedural defects in removal must be raised by timely motion to remand or they are generally waived.

title: Remedies — Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Remedies for nuisance include damages, injunctions, or abatement depending on the circumstances.

title: Remedies — Purpose
subject: Contracts
ruleText: The purpose of contract remedies is to put the non-breaching party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed.

title: Remedies for Breach of Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: For breach of trust, a court may compel performance, enjoin breaches, order redress by money damages or restoration of property, trace and recover trust assets, deny compensation, remove the trustee, or impose other equitable relief.

title: Remedies for Nuisance
subject: Torts
ruleText: Remedies for nuisance may include damages, injunction, or abatement, depending on the balance of hardships and adequacy of legal relief.

title: Remedy — Exclusion
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An identification obtained in violation of the right to counsel or due process is excluded from evidence.

title: Removal
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A defendant may remove a civil action from state court to federal court if the federal court would have had original subject matter jurisdiction over the action.

title: Removal of Executive Officers
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The President generally may remove executive officers, but Congress may limit removal of certain inferior officers or members of independent regulatory commissions when such limits do not unduly interfere with executive power.

title: Removal of Trustee
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A court may remove a trustee for serious breach of trust, lack of cooperation impairing administration, unfitness, unwillingness, persistent failure to administer effectively, or substantial change of circumstances making removal in the beneficiaries' best interests.

title: Removal Power
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The President generally has the power to remove executive officers, subject to limited congressional restrictions.

title: Renewed JMOL
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made after trial if a prior JMOL motion was made.

title: Renewed Judgment as a Matter of Law
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made after verdict only if the party made a JMOL motion before the case was submitted to the jury.

title: Rent and Expenses Among Cotenants
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A cotenant in sole possession generally owes no rent to other cotenants absent ouster or agreement, but cotenants are entitled to contribution for necessary carrying costs such as taxes and mortgage interest, and may receive credits for repairs or improvements in partition.

title: Renvoi
subject: Conflict of Laws
ruleText: Renvoi is the doctrine under which a forum, when directed to apply another jurisdiction's law, may look to that jurisdiction's whole law including its conflict-of-laws rules, though most courts reject renvoi except in limited contexts.

title: Republication by Codicil
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A valid codicil republishes the will as of the date of the codicil, so the will is treated as reexecuted on that later date for many purposes.

title: Reputation Concerning Personal or Family History
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Reputation among a person's family by blood, adoption, or marriage, or among associates or in the community, concerning a person's birth, adoption, legitimacy, ancestry, marriage, divorce, death, relationship by blood, adoption, or marriage, or similar personal or family history is admissible.

title: Requests for Admission
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Requests for admission ask a party to admit the truth of facts, the application of law to fact, or the genuineness of documents.

title: Required Joinder
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A party must be joined if feasible when complete relief cannot be accorded without that party or the absent party claims an interest that may be impaired or may expose existing parties to multiple or inconsistent obligations.

title: Required Mental State
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Criminal statutes may require purpose, knowledge, recklessness, negligence, malice, specific intent, general intent, or no mental state at all for strict liability crimes.

title: Requirements Contracts
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A requirements contract is enforceable when one party agrees to buy all of its requirements from another in good faith.

title: Res Ipsa Loquitur
subject: Torts
ruleText: Res ipsa loquitur permits an inference of negligence when the accident ordinarily does not occur without negligence and the instrumentality was under the defendant’s control.

title: Res Ipsa Loquitur
subject: Torts
ruleText: Res ipsa loquitur permits an inference of negligence when the event ordinarily does not occur absent negligence, the instrumentality was under the defendant's exclusive control, and the plaintiff did not contribute to the harm.
buzzwords: ordinarily does not occur absent negligence

title: Rescission
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Rescission discharges a contract when both parties mutually agree to terminate their contractual obligations.

title: Rescission and Restitution
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A party may seek rescission and restitution when there has been a material breach or a contract defense applies.

title: Rescue Doctrine
subject: Torts
ruleText: If the defendant's negligence imperils a victim, the defendant is generally liable for injuries suffered by a foreseeable rescuer unless the rescuer acts rashly or recklessly.

title: Residence — Corporations
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: For venue purposes, a corporation resides in any judicial district where it is subject to personal jurisdiction.

title: Residence — Individuals
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: For venue purposes, an individual resides in the judicial district where they are domiciled.

title: Residency Requirement for Divorce
subject: Family Law
ruleText: A state court may grant a divorce if the filing spouse satisfies the state's residency or domicile requirement, which is usually established by physical presence and intent to remain.

title: Residual Exception
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A hearsay statement not specifically covered by Rule 803 or 804 may be admitted if it has equivalent circumstantial guarantees of trustworthiness, is offered as evidence of a material fact, is more probative on the point than any other evidence that can be obtained through reasonable efforts, and admitting it will best serve the purposes of the rules and the interests of justice, with reasonable pretrial notice to the adverse party.

title: Respondeat Superior
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: An employer is vicariously liable for torts committed by an employee within the scope of employment under the doctrine of respondeat superior.

title: Respondeat Superior
subject: Torts
ruleText: An employer is vicariously liable for torts committed by an employee acting within the scope of employment.

title: Restitution
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Restitution restores to the non-breaching party any benefit conferred on the breaching party.

title: Restitution and Unjust Enrichment
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Restitution is available to prevent unjust enrichment when one party confers a benefit on another under circumstances making retention of the benefit unjust.

title: Restitution as Remedy
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Restitution restores to the claimant the value of benefits conferred on the other party to prevent unjust enrichment.

title: Resulting Trust
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A resulting trust arises when trust property is returned to the settlor or estate.

title: Resulting Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A resulting trust arises when a trust fails in whole or part or when circumstances show the transferee was not intended to take beneficially, so the property returns to the settlor or the settlor's estate.

title: Retaliatory Eviction
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A landlord may not evict or otherwise retaliate against a residential tenant for reporting housing-code violations or exercising legal rights.

title: Retirement Benefits
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Retirement benefits earned during marriage are generally marital or community property to the extent accrued during the marriage and may be divided directly or by deferred distribution methods, often through a QDRO for certain plans.

title: Retraction of Anticipatory Repudiation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An anticipatory repudiation may be retracted unless the non-repudiating party has materially changed position or indicated the repudiation is final.

title: Retraction of Repudiation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A repudiating party may retract repudiation until the other party materially changes position, indicates the repudiation is final, or files suit.

title: Retreat
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: At common law, retreat before using deadly force is required if safe retreat is known and feasible, except generally in one's own home.

title: Retreat and Self-Defense
subject: Torts
ruleText: Traditionally, retreat is not required before using reasonable nondeadly force, and many jurisdictions do not require retreat before deadly force if the person reasonably faces death or serious bodily harm, especially in the home.

title: Reverse Piercing Concept
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Reverse piercing is the theory that a claimant may seek to reach corporate assets for the liabilities of a shareholder, though courts treat it cautiously because it may prejudice innocent shareholders or creditors.

title: Reversion
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A reversion is a future interest retained by a grantor who conveys less than the grantor's entire estate.

title: Review of Findings of Fact
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Findings of fact are generally reviewed with deference and are not reversed unless the applicable standard for disturbing factual findings is met.

title: Review of Legal Conclusions
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Pure legal conclusions are generally reviewed de novo.

title: Revival of a Revoked Will
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: If a later will that revoked an earlier will is itself revoked, the earlier will is revived only if the testator intended revival or if revival is permitted under the applicable statute and supported by the circumstances.

title: Revival of Will
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A revoked will may be revived if the testator intends revival.

title: Revocable Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under modern law, a trust is presumed revocable unless the terms provide that it is irrevocable, although older law presumed the opposite in some jurisdictions.

title: Revocable Trust
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A revocable trust may be amended or revoked by the settlor during life.

title: Revocable Trust as Will Substitute
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A revocable living trust allows the settlor to retain control during life while providing for management during incapacity and nonprobate transfer at death, often working with a pour-over will.

title: Revocation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A revocation is effective when received by the offeree.

title: Revocation — By Physical Act
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A will may be revoked by a physical act performed with intent to revoke.

title: Revocation — By Subsequent Writing
subject: Trusts
ruleText: A will may be revoked by a later will or codicil that expressly or impliedly revokes the prior will.

title: Revocation — General Rule
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An offeror may revoke an offer at any time before acceptance unless the offer is irrevocable.

title: Revocation — Indirect Revocation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An offer is revoked indirectly when the offeree learns from a reliable source that the offeror has taken action inconsistent with the offer.

title: Revocation by Physical Act
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A will may be revoked by a subsequent physical act, such as burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying, performed with the intent to revoke by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence.

title: Revocation by Subsequent Instrument
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: A will may be revoked by a later will or codicil executed with testamentary formalities if the later instrument expressly revokes the prior will or revokes it by inconsistency.

title: Revocation of Assignment
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An assignment is irrevocable if it is supported by consideration, made in a signed writing, or relied upon by the assignee.

title: Right of Entry
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A right of entry, also called a power of termination, is the future interest retained by a grantor following a fee simple subject to condition subsequent.

title: Right of Redemption
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A debtor, secondary obligor, or other secured party or lienholder may redeem collateral by tendering fulfillment of all obligations secured by the collateral and the reasonable expenses and attorney's fees of the secured party before the secured party has collected collateral, disposed of it, or entered into a contract for disposition, or before the secured party has accepted collateral in satisfaction of the debt.

title: Right to Confront Witnesses
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to confront and cross-examine adverse witnesses.

title: Right to Counsel — Attachment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies to pretrial identifications conducted after the initiation of formal judicial proceedings.

title: Right to Jury Trial
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A defendant has a jury-trial right for serious offenses, generally those punishable by more than six months' imprisonment.

title: Right to Jury Trial — Sixth Amendment
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in criminal prosecutions for serious offenses.

title: Right to Public Trial
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to a public trial.

title: Right to Travel
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Constitution protects the right of interstate travel, including the right to enter and leave states and to be treated equally as a new resident regarding fundamental matters.

title: Right to Vote
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Severe burdens on the right to vote are subject to strict scrutiny, while reasonable, nondiscriminatory election regulations are reviewed under a more balancing-oriented standard.

title: Rights of Account Debtor
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: An account debtor may generally assert against an assignee or secured party any defenses or claims in recoupment arising from the transaction that gave rise to the contract, and, subject to anti-assignment and waiver rules, may in some cases assert other defenses as well.

title: Rights of Assignee
subject: Contracts
ruleText: An assignee stands in the shoes of the assignor and is subject to all defenses that could be asserted against the assignor.

title: Rights of Co-Tenants — Possession
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Each co-tenant has an equal right to possess the whole property.

title: Riparian Rights
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Owners whose land borders a watercourse have riparian rights to make reasonable use of the water, subject to rights of other riparian owners.

title: Ripeness
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A claim is ripe when there is a present, concrete dispute and withholding review would create hardship, especially where further factual development is unnecessary.

title: Ripeness
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: A claim is ripe when it presents a real and immediate dispute appropriate for judicial resolution.

title: Risk of Loss
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Under the traditional equitable conversion rule, the buyer bears the risk of casualty loss after contract formation unless the contract provides otherwise, though many modern statutes place the risk on the seller until possession or title passes.
buzzwords: seller until possession or title passes

title: Risk of Loss
subject: Real Property
ruleText: After equitable conversion, the risk of loss passes to the buyer unless the contract provides otherwise.

title: Risk of Loss in Destination Contracts
subject: Contracts
ruleText: In a destination contract, risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are tendered at the destination.

title: Risk of Loss in Shipment Contracts
subject: Contracts
ruleText: In a shipment contract, risk of loss passes to the buyer when the seller duly delivers the goods to the carrier.

title: Risk-Utility and Hand Formula
subject: Torts
ruleText: Breach may be assessed by balancing the burden of precautions against the probability and gravity of harm, as reflected in the Hand formula.

title: Robbery
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Robbery is larceny from the person or presence of the victim by force or intimidation.

title: Robbery
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Robbery is the taking of personal property from another by force or intimidation with intent to permanently deprive.

title: Robbery — Property Aspect
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Robbery is larceny accomplished by force or intimidation from the person or presence of another.

title: Rule 11
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: By signing a federal paper, a lawyer certifies that it is not presented for an improper purpose, that legal contentions are warranted by law or a nonfrivolous argument, and that factual contentions have or likely will have evidentiary support.

title: Rule 12(b) Motions — Overview
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Rule 12(b) permits defenses to be raised by motion before filing an answer.

title: Rule 12(b)(1) — Subject Matter Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Lack of subject matter jurisdiction may be raised at any time.

title: Rule 12(b)(2) — Personal Jurisdiction
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Lack of personal jurisdiction must be raised in the first Rule 12 response or it is waived.

title: Rule 12(b)(3) — Improper Venue
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Improper venue must be raised in the first Rule 12 response or it is waived.

title: Rule 12(b)(4) and 12(b)(5) — Process and Service
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Insufficient process and insufficient service of process must be raised in the first Rule 12 response or they are waived.

title: Rule 12(b)(6) — Failure to State a Claim
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim tests whether the complaint plausibly alleges entitlement to relief.

title: Rule 12(e) — More Definite Statement
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A motion for a more definite statement is appropriate when a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a response cannot be framed.

title: Rule 12(f) — Motion to Strike
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may strike insufficient defenses or redundant, immaterial, or scandalous matter.

title: Rule 403 — Balancing Test
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Relevant evidence may be excluded if its probative value is substantially outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice, confusion, or misleading the jury.

title: Rule 403 — Other Considerations
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Relevant evidence may also be excluded for undue delay, wasting time, or needless presentation of cumulative evidence.

title: Rule 403 Balancing
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Relevant evidence may be excluded if its probative value is substantially outweighed by a danger of unfair prejudice, confusing the issues, misleading the jury, undue delay, wasting time, or needlessly presenting cumulative evidence.

title: Rule Against Perpetuities — General Rule
subject: Real Property
ruleText: A future interest is invalid unless it must vest, if at all, within 21 years after a life in being at the creation of the interest.

title: Rule in Shelley's Case
subject: Real Property
ruleText: At common law, the Rule in Shelley's Case converted certain future interests given to the grantee's heirs into a remainder in the grantee, but the rule is abolished in most jurisdictions.

title: Rules Enabling Act
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: The Rules Enabling Act prohibits Federal Rules from abridging, enlarging, or modifying substantive rights.

title: Rules of Contract Interpretation
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Contracts are interpreted to give effect to the parties’ intent as expressed in the contract language.

title: Sale of Substantially All Assets
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: A sale, lease, exchange, or other disposition of all or substantially all corporate assets outside the usual and regular course of business generally requires board approval and shareholder approval, while ordinary-course dispositions usually do not.

title: Same Offense
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Under Blockburger, offenses are different if each requires proof of a fact the other does not; lesser-included offenses are generally the same offense as the greater offense.

title: Same Offense — Blockburger Test
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Two offenses are the same offense for double jeopardy purposes unless each offense requires proof of a fact the other does not.

title: School and Workplace Searches
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Certain public school and government workplace searches may be judged by reasonableness rather than probable cause and warrant requirements.

title: Scope of Article 9
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Article 9 governs transactions that create a security interest in personal property or fixtures.

title: Scope of Consent
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Scope of consent is measured by objective reasonableness: what a typical reasonable person would have understood from the exchange.

title: Scope of Cross-Examination
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Cross-examination should not go beyond the subject matter of the direct examination and matters affecting the witness's credibility, although the court may allow inquiry into additional matters as if on direct examination.

title: Scope of Discovery
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Parties may obtain discovery regarding any nonprivileged matter relevant to a party's claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case.

title: Scope of Easement
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The scope of an easement is determined by the grant or the circumstances of creation and may not be unreasonably expanded beyond the contemplated use.

title: Scope of Easement
subject: Real Property
ruleText: The scope of an easement is limited to the terms of its creation.

title: Scope of Employment
subject: Business Associations
ruleText: Conduct is within the scope of employment if it is of the kind the employee was employed to perform, occurs substantially within authorized time and space limits, and is motivated at least in part by a purpose to serve the employer.

title: Scope of Employment
subject: Torts
ruleText: Conduct falls within the scope of employment when it is of the kind the employee was hired to perform, occurs substantially within authorized time and space limits, and is motivated at least in part to serve the employer.

title: Scope of Risk
subject: Torts
ruleText: Strict liability applies only to harm that results from the type of risk that makes the activity abnormally dangerous.

title: Search — Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A search occurs when the government violates a person’s reasonable expectation of privacy.

title: Search — Physical Intrusion
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A search occurs when the government physically intrudes into a constitutionally protected area for the purpose of obtaining information.

title: Search Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A search occurs when the government intrudes on a reasonable expectation of privacy or physically trespasses on a constitutionally protected area to obtain information.

title: Search Incident to Arrest
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may search the arrestee's person and the area within the arrestee's immediate control incident to a lawful custodial arrest.

title: Search Incident to Arrest — Probable Cause to Arrest
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An arrest is valid if supported by probable cause that the suspect committed a crime.

title: Search Incident to Arrest — Vehicle
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may search a vehicle incident to arrest only if the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance or if evidence of the crime of arrest may be found in the vehicle.

title: Search Incident to Lawful Arrest — Area
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may search the area within the arrestee’s immediate control to prevent access to weapons or destruction of evidence.

title: Search Incident to Lawful Arrest — Person
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may search the arrestee’s person incident to a lawful arrest without a warrant.

title: Search Warrant — Third Party Home
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Police may not enter a third party’s home to execute an arrest warrant without a search warrant or consent.

title: Second Amendment
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Second Amendment protects an individual's right to keep and bear arms for lawful purposes such as self-defense, subject to historical and traditionally accepted regulations.

title: Secondary Obligor
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A secondary obligor is an obligor to the extent that the obligor's obligation is secondary, such as a guarantor or surety, or the obligor has recourse against the debtor with respect to the obligation secured.

title: Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: Congress may enforce the Fourteenth Amendment by adopting legislation that is congruent and proportional to constitutional violations by state actors.

title: Secured Party Purchase at Sale
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A secured party may buy collateral at a public disposition, and may buy at a private disposition only if the collateral is of a kind customarily sold on a recognized market or the subject of widely distributed standard price quotations.

title: Security Agreement
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security agreement is an authenticated record that provides a description of the collateral.

title: Security Agreement Requirements
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: Unless possession or control substitutes for a signed record, a security agreement must be authenticated by the debtor and must reasonably identify the collateral.

title: Security Deposits
subject: Real Property
ruleText: Modern statutes commonly regulate the amount, handling, and return of security deposits and may require itemized statements of deductions.

title: Security Interest
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A security interest is an interest in personal property or fixtures that secures payment or performance of an obligation.

title: Seizure — Show of Authority
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A seizure by show of authority occurs only if the defendant submits to the authority.

title: Seizure Definition
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A person is seized when a reasonable person would not feel free to leave due to government force or show of authority, and the person submits or is physically restrained.

title: Seizure of Person
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A person is seized when, under the totality of circumstances, a reasonable person would not feel free to leave.

title: Seizure of Property
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Property is seized when the government meaningfully interferes with an individual’s possessory interest.

title: Self-Authenticating Evidence
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Certain items require no extrinsic evidence of authenticity, including sealed and signed public documents, certified public records, official publications, newspapers and periodicals, trade inscriptions, acknowledged documents, commercial paper, and certain certified electronic records.

title: Self-Authentication — Business Records (Certified)
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Certified business records are self-authenticating if accompanied by a proper certification.

title: Self-Authentication — Definition
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Self-authenticating evidence requires no extrinsic evidence of authenticity to be admitted.

title: Self-Authentication — Public Documents
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Certified copies of public records are self-authenticating.

title: Self-Defense
subject: Torts
ruleText: A person may use reasonable force to defend against an imminent harmful or offensive contact when the person reasonably believes force is necessary.

title: Self-Defense
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A person may use reasonable nondeadly force when reasonably necessary to defend against imminent unlawful force, and deadly force when reasonably necessary to prevent imminent death or serious bodily harm.

title: Self-Defense — Deadly Force
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Deadly force may be used only to prevent imminent death, serious bodily injury, or forcible sexual assault.

title: Self-Defense — Duty to Retreat
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: A defendant must retreat before using deadly force if retreat can be accomplished safely, unless in the defendant’s home.

title: Self-Defense — General Rule
subject: Torts
ruleText: Self-defense is justified when a person reasonably believes force is necessary to prevent imminent unlawful harm.

title: Self-Defense — General Rule
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Self-defense justifies the use of force when the defendant reasonably believes force is necessary to prevent imminent unlawful harm.

title: Self-Defense — Proportional Force
subject: Torts
ruleText: Self-defense permits only reasonable force and does not justify deadly force unless facing a threat of serious bodily harm.

title: Self-Help Repossession
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: After default, a secured party may take possession of collateral without judicial process if it can be done without breach of the peace.

title: Self-Settled Trust
subject: Trusts and Estates
ruleText: Under the traditional rule, if the settlor creates a trust for the settlor's own benefit, the settlor's creditors can generally reach the maximum amount that could be distributed to or for the settlor's benefit, regardless of spendthrift language, subject to domestic asset-protection trust statutes in a few jurisdictions.

title: Seller Remedies — Buyer Breach
subject: Real Property
ruleText: If the buyer breaches, the seller may retain the deposit, sue for damages, or seek specific performance.

title: Seller's Market Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A seller may recover the difference between the market price at the time and place for tender and the unpaid contract price, plus incidental damages, less expenses saved.

title: Seller's Resale Damages
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A seller who resells in good faith and in a commercially reasonable manner may recover the difference between the resale price and contract price plus incidental damages, less expenses saved.
buzzwords: difference between resale and contract price

title: Seller's Right to Cure
subject: Contracts
ruleText: A seller may cure a nonconforming tender within the contract time for performance and, in some cases, after the time if the seller had reasonable grounds to believe the tender would be acceptable.

title: Sensory or Mental Defect
subject: Evidence
ruleText: A witness may be impeached by showing defects in perception, memory, or narration, including impaired vision, hearing, intoxication, or mental condition affecting the ability to observe or recall.

title: Separate Property
subject: Family Law
ruleText: Separate property generally includes property owned before marriage, and property acquired during marriage by gift, devise, descent, or sometimes personal injury damages allocated to personal suffering, unless transmuted or commingled.

title: Separate Sovereigns Doctrine
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: Double jeopardy does not bar successive prosecutions by separate sovereigns for the same conduct.

title: Separation of Powers — General Principle
subject: Constitutional Law
ruleText: The Constitution divides federal power among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches to prevent the concentration of power.

title: Serious vs Petty Offenses
subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
ruleText: An offense is considered serious if the authorized punishment exceeds six months of imprisonment.

title: Seriously Misleading Errors
subject: Secured Transactions
ruleText: A financing statement that fails sufficiently to provide the debtor's name is seriously misleading unless a search of the filing office records under the debtor's correct name, using the filing office's standard search logic, would disclose the record.

title: Seriousness of Interference in Conversion
subject: Torts
ruleText: Courts consider factors such as the extent and duration of dominion, the defendant's intent, good faith, harm done, and inconvenience caused to the plaintiff.

title: Service of Process
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Proper service of process requires delivery of the summons and complaint by a method authorized by rule and reasonably calculated to notify the defendant.

title: Service of Process — Rule 4
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: Service of process must comply with Rule 4 and provide notice of the action and the court.

title: Setting Aside Default Judgment
subject: Civil Procedure
ruleText: A court may set aside a default judgment for good cause, mistake, or excusable neglect.

title: Settlement — Permitted Uses
subject: Evidence
ruleText: Evidence of settlement negotiations may be admitted for purposes such as impeachment or proving bias.

title: Settlement of Claims
subject: Contracts
ruleText: Forbearance to assert a legal claim is valid consideration if the claim is asserted in good faith.

title: Seventh Amendment Jury Trial Right
subject: Civil Procedure
